This document covers 5→> the fly.toml configuration per service, the deploy scripts, secret management, scaling, 6→> cost estimates, the web terminal console service, and the deploy command workflow. 7→ 8→ 9→> **Implementation**: Specified 10→ 11→--- 12→ 13→## Architecture on Fly.io 14→ 15→Fly.io runs each service as a Firecracker microVM (called a "machine"). Each service gets its 16→own app, its own domain (`.fly.dev`), and its own configuration. Services in the same Fly 17→organization communicate over a private IPv6 network (6PN) using `.internal` DNS names. 18→ 19→For example, the Roko CLI orchestrator reaches the API server at 20→`http://roko-serve.internal:8080`. This traffic stays on Fly's private network — it never 21→touches the public internet. 22→ 23→``` 24→ ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐ 25→ │ Fly.io Organization │ 26→ │ │ 27→ │ ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ 28→ HTTPS ──────────▶ │ │ roko-serve │◀──▶│ roko-cli │ │ 29→ (public) │ │ HTTP API │ │ orchestrator │ │ 30→ │ │ port 8080 │ │ port 8080 │ │ 31→ │ └─────────────┘ │ volume: /data │ │ 32→ │ ▲ └──────────────────┘ │ 33→ │ │ .internal │ 34→ │ ▼ │ 35→ │ ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ 36→ │ │ mirage-rs │ │ roko-console │ │ 37→ │ │ EVM fork │ │ web terminal │ │ 38→ │ │ port 8545 │ │ port 3000 │ │ 39→ │ │ volume:state│ │ (Caddy proxy) │ │ 40→ │ └─────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ 41→ │ │ 42→ └───────────────────────────────────────────┘ 43→``` 44→ 45→--- 46→ 47→## Directory Structure 48→ 49→All Fly.io configuration lives in `deploy/`: 50→ 51→``` 52→deploy/ 53→ fly/ 54→ roko-cli/fly.toml # Roko CLI orchestrator 55→ roko-serve/fly.toml # Roko HTTP API server 56→ mirage-rs/fly.toml # mirage-rs EVM fork simulator 57→ console/fly.toml # Web terminal console 58→ console/ 59→ Caddyfile # Reverse proxy for web terminals 60→ static/index.html # xterm.js web terminal UI 61→ scripts/ 62→ fly-deploy.sh # Deploy one or all services 63→ fly-secrets.sh # Set secrets per service 64→ fly-status.sh # Health check all services 65→ fly-logs.sh # Tail logs with service picker 66→``` 67→ 68→--- 69→ 70→## fly.toml Per Service 71→ 72→### roko-cli (Orchestrator) 73→ 74→```toml 75→# deploy/fly/roko-cli/fly.toml 76→app = "roko-cli" 77→primary_region = "iad" 78→ 79→[build] 80→ dockerfile = "docker/roko-cli-full.Dockerfile" 81→ 82→[env] 83→ RUST_LOG = "roko=info" 84→ 85→[mounts] 86→ source = "roko_data" 87→ destination = "/data" 88→ 89→[http_service] 90→ internal_port = 8080 91→ force_https = true 92→ auto_stop_machines = "stop" 93→ auto_start_machines = true 94→ min_machines_running = 0 95→ 96→ [http_service.concurrency] 97→ type = "requests" 98→ hard_limit = 100 99→ soft_limit = 50 100→ 101→[[http_service.checks]] 102→ grace_period = "15s" 103→ interval = "30s" 104→ method = "GET" 105→ path = "/health" 106→ timeout = "5s" 107→ 108→[[services]] 109→ internal_port = 7681 110→ protocol = "tcp" 111→ 112→ [[services.ports]] 113→ port = 7681 114→ handlers = ["tls", "http"] 115→ 116→[[vm]] 117→ size = "shared-cpu-2x" 118→ memory = "2gb" 119→``` 120→ 121→**Key settings**: 122→- `auto_stop_machines = "stop"` with `min_machines_running = 0` — the machine stops when 123→ idle. No CPU/RAM charges while stopped. When a request arrives, Fly starts the machine 124→ (~2-3 second cold start for a Rust binary). 125→- `[mounts]` — persistent Fly volume for `.roko/` state directory. Survives machine stops 126→ and restarts. The volume is created by the deploy script on first run. 127→- Memory is 2GB because the code index (tree-sitter AST + HDC fingerprints + symbol graph) 128→ can grow for large codebases. 129→- Port 7681 is exposed for ttyd (web terminal access via the full image). 130→ 131→### roko-serve (HTTP API) 132→ 133→```toml 134→# deploy/fly/roko-serve/fly.toml 135→app = "roko-serve" 136→primary_region = "iad" 137→ 138→[build] 139→ dockerfile = "docker/roko-serve.Dockerfile" 140→ 141→[env] 142→ RUST_LOG = "roko_serve=info" 143→ 144→[http_service] 145→ internal_port = 8080 146→ force_https = true 147→ auto_stop_machines = "stop" 148→ auto_start_machines = true 149→ min_machines_running = 0 150→ 151→ [http_service.concurrency] 152→ type = "requests" 153→ hard_limit = 500 154→ soft_limit = 250 155→ 156→[[http_service.checks]] 157→ grace_period = "10s" 158→ interval = "30s" 159→ method = "GET" 160→ path = "/health" 161→ timeout = "5s" 162→ 163→[[vm]] 164→ size = "shared-cpu-2x" 165→ memory = "1gb" 166→``` 167→ 168→### mirage-rs (EVM Fork Simulator) 169→ 170→```toml 171→# deploy/fly/mirage-rs/fly.toml 172→app = "roko-mirage" 173→primary_region = "iad" 174→ 175→[build] 176→ dockerfile = "docker/mirage-rs.Dockerfile" 177→ 178→[env] 179→ RUST_LOG = "mirage_rs=info" 180→ 181→[mounts] 182→ source = "mirage_state" 183→ destination = "/data" 184→ 185→[http_service] 186→ internal_port = 8545 187→ force_https = true 188→ auto_stop_machines = "stop" 189→ auto_start_machines = true 190→ min_machines_running = 0 191→ 192→ [http_service.concurrency] 193→ type = "requests" 194→ hard_limit = 200 195→ soft_limit = 100 196→ 197→[[http_service.checks]] 198→ grace_period = "15s" 199→ interval = "30s" 200→ method = "POST" 201→ path = "/" 202→ timeout = "5s" 203→ headers = { "Content-Type" = "application/json" } 204→ body = '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"web3_clientVersion","params":[],"id":1}' 205→ 206→[[vm]] 207→ size = "shared-cpu-2x" 208→ memory = "2gb" 209→``` 210→ 211→mirage-rs uses a JSON-RPC health check (standard Ethereum `web3_clientVersion` call) because 212→it does not expose a separate HTTP health endpoint — the JSON-RPC server is the primary 213→interface. 214→ 215→### Console (Web Terminal) 216→ 217→```toml 218→# deploy/fly/console/fly.toml 219→app = "roko-console" 220→primary_region = "iad" 221→ 222→[build] 223→ dockerfile = "docker/console.Dockerfile" 224→ 225→[env] 226→ SERVICES = "roko-cli:roko-cli.internal:7681,roko-serve:roko-serve.internal:7681" 227→ 228→[http_service] 229→ internal_port = 3000 230→ force_https = true 231→ auto_stop_machines = "stop" 232→ auto_start_machines = true 233→ min_machines_running = 0 234→ 235→[[http_service.checks]] 236→ grace_period = "5s" 237→ interval = "30s" 238→ method = "GET" 239→ path = "/health" 240→ timeout = "3s" 241→ 242→[[vm]] 243→ size = "shared-cpu-1x" 244→ memory = "256mb" 245→``` 246→ 247→The console is a lightweight Caddy reverse proxy that forwards WebSocket connections to each 248→service's ttyd instance over Fly's internal network. It serves a static HTML page with xterm.js 249→that renders the terminal in the browser. 250→ 251→--- 252→ 253→## Deploy Scripts 254→ 255→### `fly-deploy.sh` 256→ 257→```bash 258→#!/bin/bash 259→# deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh 260→# 261→# Deploy one or all Roko services to Fly.io. 262→# 263→# Usage: 264→# ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh all # Deploy everything 265→# ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh roko-cli # Deploy just the CLI 266→# ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh roko-serve # Deploy just the API server 267→# ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh mirage-rs # Deploy just mirage-rs 268→# ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh console # Deploy just the web console 269→ 270→set -euo pipefail 271→ 272→SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)" 273→REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../.." && pwd)" 274→ 275→deploy_service() { 276→ local service="$1" 277→ local fly_dir="$REPO_ROOT/deploy/fly/$service" 278→ 279→ if [ ! -f "$fly_dir/fly.toml" ]; then 280→ echo "Error: No fly.toml found at $fly_dir/fly.toml" 281→ exit 1 282→ fi 283→ 284→ echo "=== Deploying $service ===" 285→ 286→ # Create the app if it doesn't exist 287→ local app_name 288→ app_name=$(grep '^app = ' "$fly_dir/fly.toml" | sed 's/app = "\(.*\)"/\1/') 289→ 290→ if ! fly apps list --json | jq -e ".[] | select(.Name == \"$app_name\")" > /dev/null 2>&1; then 291→ echo "Creating app $app_name..." 292→ fly apps create "$app_name" --org personal 293→ fi 294→ 295→ # Create volume if the service needs one 296→ if grep -q '\[mounts\]' "$fly_dir/fly.toml"; then 297→ local vol_name 298→ vol_name=$(grep 'source = ' "$fly_dir/fly.toml" | head -1 | sed 's/.*source = "\(.*\)"/\1/') 299→ local region 300→ region=$(grep 'primary_region' "$fly_dir/fly.toml" | sed 's/.*= "\(.*\)"/\1/') 301→ 302→ if ! fly volumes list --app "$app_name" --json | jq -e '.[0]' > /dev/null 2>&1; then 303→ echo "Creating volume $vol_name in $region..." 304→ fly volumes create "$vol_name" --app "$app_name" --region "$region" --size 1 305→ fi 306→ fi 307→ 308→ # Deploy 309→ fly deploy --config "$fly_dir/fly.toml" --app "$app_name" --remote-only 310→ 311→ echo "=== $service deployed ===" 312→ echo "" 313→} 314→ 315→case "${1:-all}" in 316→ all) 317→ deploy_service roko-serve 318→ deploy_service roko-cli 319→ deploy_service console 320→ ;; 321→ *) 322→ deploy_service "$1" 323→ ;; 324→esac 325→``` 326→ 327→### `fly-secrets.sh` 328→ 329→```bash 330→#!/bin/bash 331→# deploy/scripts/fly-secrets.sh 332→# 333→# Set secrets for Roko services on Fly.io. 334→# Reads from .env file or environment variables. 335→# 336→# Usage: 337→# ./deploy/scripts/fly-secrets.sh 338→ 339→set -euo pipefail 340→ 341→# Load .env if it exists 342→if [ -f .env ]; then 343→ set -a 344→ source .env 345→ set +a 346→fi 347→ 348→echo "Setting secrets for roko-serve..." 349→fly secrets set \ 350→ ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY:?Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}" \ 351→ --app roko-serve 352→ 353→echo "Setting secrets for roko-cli..." 354→fly secrets set \ 355→ ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}" \ 356→ --app roko-cli 357→ 358→if [ -n "${CONSOLE_AUTH_TOKEN:-}" ]; then 359→ echo "Setting secrets for roko-console..." 360→ fly secrets set \ 361→ CONSOLE_AUTH_TOKEN="$CONSOLE_AUTH_TOKEN" \ 362→ --app roko-console 363→fi 364→ 365→echo "Done. Secrets set." 366→``` 367→ 368→### `fly-status.sh` 369→ 370→```bash 371→#!/bin/bash 372→# deploy/scripts/fly-status.sh 373→# 374→# Check health of all deployed Roko services. 375→ 376→set -euo pipefail 377→ 378→services=("roko-cli" "roko-serve" "roko-console" "roko-mirage") 379→ 380→printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "SERVICE" "STATUS" "URL" "MACHINES" 381→printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "-------" "------" "---" "--------" 382→ 383→for svc in "${services[@]}"; do 384→ if fly apps list --json 2>/dev/null | jq -e ".[] | select(.Name == \"$svc\")" > /dev/null 2>&1; then 385→ url="https://$svc.fly.dev" 386→ machines=$(fly machines list --app "$svc" --json 2>/dev/null | jq 'length') 387→ status=$(curl -sf "$url/health" -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" 2>/dev/null || echo "down") 388→ if [ "$status" = "200" ]; then 389→ printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "$svc" "healthy" "$url" "started:$machines" 390→ else 391→ printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "$svc" "unhealthy" "$url" "$machines" 392→ fi 393→ else 394→ printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "$svc" "not deployed" "-" "-" 395→ fi 396→done 397→``` 398→ 399→--- 400→ 401→## Secret Management on Fly.io 402→ 403→Provider API keys live as Fly secrets. They become environment variables inside the machine. 404→They never appear in fly.toml, Docker images, or logs. 405→ 406→### Secret Flow 407→ 408→``` 409→deploy command 410→ → reads --anthropic-key flag, or $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, or prompts 411→ → runs: fly secrets set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY= --app roko-cli 412→ → secrets are encrypted at rest on Fly's infrastructure 413→ → injected as env vars when the machine starts 414→``` 415→ 416→### Detecting Existing Secrets 417→ 418→On redeploy, the deploy script checks what secrets are already set: 419→ 420→```bash 421→fly secrets list --app roko-cli --json 422→``` 423→ 424→It only prompts for or sets missing secrets. If the user deploys without `--anthropic-key` and 425→the secret already exists on Fly, it skips that secret. 426→ 427→--- 428→ 429→## Cost Estimates 430→ 431→Fly.io pricing for Roko services with auto-stop enabled (prices as of early 2026): 432→ 433→| Service | VM Size | Memory | Auto-Stop | Estimated Cost | 434→|---|---|---|---|---| 435→| roko-cli | shared-cpu-2x | 2GB | Yes | ~$3-7/mo (active hours only) | 436→| roko-serve | shared-cpu-2x | 1GB | Yes | ~$2-5/mo (active hours only) | 437→| mirage-rs | shared-cpu-2x | 2GB | Yes | ~$3-7/mo (active hours only) | 438→| console | shared-cpu-1x | 256MB | Yes | ~$1-2/mo | 439→| Volumes (1GB each) | — | — | — | ~$0.15/mo per volume | 440→ 441→With `auto_stop_machines = "stop"` and `min_machines_running = 0`, machines stop when idle. 442→No CPU/RAM charges while stopped. The machine starts in ~2-3 seconds when a request arrives 443→(cold start for a Rust binary is fast). 444→ 445→For a personal deployment where services are used intermittently, the total cost is typically 446→$5-15/month. For always-on production deployments, set `min_machines_running = 1` to eliminate 447→cold starts, at the cost of continuous charges. 448→ 449→--- 450→ 451→## Custom Domains 452→ 453→When deploying with a custom domain: 454→ 455→```bash 456→# Attach a custom domain to a Fly app 457→fly certs create roko-serve api.example.com 458→ 459→# Fly prints DNS instructions: 460→# Add CNAME: api.example.com → roko-serve.fly.dev 461→# Certificate provisions automatically once DNS propagates. 462→``` 463→ 464→Fly handles TLS via Let's Encrypt. The user points a CNAME at `.fly.dev` and Fly 465→provisions the certificate automatically (HTTP-01 challenge). No ACME DNS records, no cert 466→files, no renewal cron. 467→ 468→### Check Certificate Status 469→ 470→```bash 471→$ fly certs show roko-serve api.example.com 472→ 473→Hostname: api.example.com 474→Status: Ready 475→Certificate: Issued (expires 2026-09-22) 476→``` 477→ 478→--- 479→ 480→## Console Service: Web Terminal 481→ 482→The console service provides browser-based access to any service's terminal via xterm.js. It 483→is a lightweight Caddy reverse proxy that forwards WebSocket connections to each service's ttyd 484→instance over Fly's internal network. 485→ 486→### Caddyfile 487→ 488→``` 489→# deploy/console/Caddyfile 490→{ 491→ admin off 492→} 493→ 494→:3000 { 495→ # Health check 496→ handle /health { 497→ respond "ok" 200 498→ } 499→ 500→ # WebSocket proxy to service ttyd instances 501→ handle /ws/roko-cli { 502→ reverse_proxy roko-cli.internal:7681 503→ } 504→ 505→ handle /ws/roko-serve { 506→ reverse_proxy roko-serve.internal:7681 507→ } 508→ 509→ # Static frontend 510→ handle { 511→ root * /srv/static 512→ file_server 513→ } 514→} 515→``` 516→ 517→### Frontend (xterm.js) 518→ 519→The console frontend is a single HTML page with xterm.js that connects to the WebSocket 520→endpoint for a selected service: 521→ 522→```html 523→ 524→ 525→ 526→ 527→ Roko Console 528→ 529→ 530→ 531→
532→ 533→ 534→
535→
536→ 537→ 538→ 539→ 552→ 553→ 554→``` 555→ 556→### Deliverable 557→ 558→``` 559→https://roko-console.fly.dev/ 560→ ├── [Tab: Roko CLI] → Live TUI in the browser 561→ └── [Tab: Roko Serve] → Live server logs in the browser 562→``` 563→ 564→--- 565→ 566→## Deployment as a Gate Step 567→ 568→A plan can include deployment as a verification step in the gate pipeline: 569→ 570→```markdown 571→### Verification 572→#### INV-005: API Deployment 573→- Deploy to staging: `roko deploy --target fly --env staging` 574→- Health check passes within 30 seconds 575→- Smoke test: `curl https://staging-api.example.com/health` returns 200 576→``` 577→ 578→The Roko Orchestrator treats deployment like any other gate. If the deploy fails or the health 579→check does not pass, the plan does not advance. This closes the loop from PRD to running 580→software. 581→ 582→--- 583→ 584→## Future Deployment Targets 585→ 586→Fly.io is the primary cloud target because it has the best CLI tooling and the simplest mental 587→model (one machine = one service). Planned additions: 588→ 589→- **Railway**: Similar model to Fly, different CLI 590→- **Bare VPS via SSH**: `roko deploy --target ssh --host user@ip` would rsync the binary and 591→ manage it via systemd 592→- **Docker Compose**: For local multi-service testing before cloud deployment (already 593→ implemented — see `03-docker.md`) 594→ 595→The deployment interface is designed as a trait. Adding a target means implementing `deploy()`, 596→`teardown()`, `status()`, and `logs()`. 597→

# Cloud Deployment: Fly.io > Roko services deploy to Fly.io as Firecracker microVMs with persistent volumes, automatic > TLS, private networking, auto-stop on idle, and auto-start on request. This document covers > the fly.toml configuration per service, the deploy scripts, secret management, scaling, > cost estimates, the web terminal console service, and the deploy command workflow. > **Implementation**: Specified --- ## Architecture on Fly.io Fly.io runs each service as a Firecracker microVM (called a "machine"). Each service gets its own app, its own domain (`.fly.dev`), and its own configuration. Services in the same Fly organization communicate over a private IPv6 network (6PN) using `.internal` DNS names. For example, the Roko CLI orchestrator reaches the API server at `http://roko-serve.internal:8080`. This traffic stays on Fly's private network — it never touches the public internet. ``` ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Fly.io Organization │ │ │ │ ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ HTTPS ──────────▶ │ │ roko-serve │◀──▶│ roko-cli │ │ (public) │ │ HTTP API │ │ orchestrator │ │ │ │ port 8080 │ │ port 8080 │ │ │ └─────────────┘ │ volume: /data │ │ │ ▲ └──────────────────┘ │ │ │ .internal │ │ ▼ │ │ ┌─────────────┐ ┌──────────────────┐ │ │ │ mirage-rs │ │ roko-console │ │ │ │ EVM fork │ │ web terminal │ │ │ │ port 8545 │ │ port 3000 │ │ │ │ volume:state│ │ (Caddy proxy) │ │ │ └─────────────┘ └──────────────────┘ │ │ │ └───────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` --- ## Directory Structure All Fly.io configuration lives in `deploy/`: ``` deploy/ fly/ roko-cli/fly.toml # Roko CLI orchestrator roko-serve/fly.toml # Roko HTTP API server mirage-rs/fly.toml # mirage-rs EVM fork simulator console/fly.toml # Web terminal console console/ Caddyfile # Reverse proxy for web terminals static/index.html # xterm.js web terminal UI scripts/ fly-deploy.sh # Deploy one or all services fly-secrets.sh # Set secrets per service fly-status.sh # Health check all services fly-logs.sh # Tail logs with service picker ``` --- ## fly.toml Per Service ### roko-cli (Orchestrator) ```toml # deploy/fly/roko-cli/fly.toml app = "roko-cli" primary_region = "iad" [build] dockerfile = "docker/roko-cli-full.Dockerfile" [env] RUST_LOG = "roko=info" [mounts] source = "roko_data" destination = "/data" [http_service] internal_port = 8080 force_https = true auto_stop_machines = "stop" auto_start_machines = true min_machines_running = 0 [http_service.concurrency] type = "requests" hard_limit = 100 soft_limit = 50 [[http_service.checks]] grace_period = "15s" interval = "30s" method = "GET" path = "/health" timeout = "5s" [[services]] internal_port = 7681 protocol = "tcp" [[services.ports]] port = 7681 handlers = ["tls", "http"] [[vm]] size = "shared-cpu-2x" memory = "2gb" ``` **Key settings**: - `auto_stop_machines = "stop"` with `min_machines_running = 0` — the machine stops when idle. No CPU/RAM charges while stopped. When a request arrives, Fly starts the machine (~2-3 second cold start for a Rust binary). - `[mounts]` — persistent Fly volume for `.roko/` state directory. Survives machine stops and restarts. The volume is created by the deploy script on first run. - Memory is 2GB because the code index (tree-sitter AST + HDC fingerprints + symbol graph) can grow for large codebases. - Port 7681 is exposed for ttyd (web terminal access via the full image). ### roko-serve (HTTP API) ```toml # deploy/fly/roko-serve/fly.toml app = "roko-serve" primary_region = "iad" [build] dockerfile = "docker/roko-serve.Dockerfile" [env] RUST_LOG = "roko_serve=info" [http_service] internal_port = 8080 force_https = true auto_stop_machines = "stop" auto_start_machines = true min_machines_running = 0 [http_service.concurrency] type = "requests" hard_limit = 500 soft_limit = 250 [[http_service.checks]] grace_period = "10s" interval = "30s" method = "GET" path = "/health" timeout = "5s" [[vm]] size = "shared-cpu-2x" memory = "1gb" ``` ### mirage-rs (EVM Fork Simulator) ```toml # deploy/fly/mirage-rs/fly.toml app = "roko-mirage" primary_region = "iad" [build] dockerfile = "docker/mirage-rs.Dockerfile" [env] RUST_LOG = "mirage_rs=info" [mounts] source = "mirage_state" destination = "/data" [http_service] internal_port = 8545 force_https = true auto_stop_machines = "stop" auto_start_machines = true min_machines_running = 0 [http_service.concurrency] type = "requests" hard_limit = 200 soft_limit = 100 [[http_service.checks]] grace_period = "15s" interval = "30s" method = "POST" path = "/" timeout = "5s" headers = { "Content-Type" = "application/json" } body = '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"web3_clientVersion","params":[],"id":1}' [[vm]] size = "shared-cpu-2x" memory = "2gb" ``` mirage-rs uses a JSON-RPC health check (standard Ethereum `web3_clientVersion` call) because it does not expose a separate HTTP health endpoint — the JSON-RPC server is the primary interface. ### Console (Web Terminal) ```toml # deploy/fly/console/fly.toml app = "roko-console" primary_region = "iad" [build] dockerfile = "docker/console.Dockerfile" [env] SERVICES = "roko-cli:roko-cli.internal:7681,roko-serve:roko-serve.internal:7681" [http_service] internal_port = 3000 force_https = true auto_stop_machines = "stop" auto_start_machines = true min_machines_running = 0 [[http_service.checks]] grace_period = "5s" interval = "30s" method = "GET" path = "/health" timeout = "3s" [[vm]] size = "shared-cpu-1x" memory = "256mb" ``` The console is a lightweight Caddy reverse proxy that forwards WebSocket connections to each service's ttyd instance over Fly's internal network. It serves a static HTML page with xterm.js that renders the terminal in the browser. --- ## Deploy Scripts ### `fly-deploy.sh` ```bash #!/bin/bash # deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh # # Deploy one or all Roko services to Fly.io. # # Usage: # ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh all # Deploy everything # ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh roko-cli # Deploy just the CLI # ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh roko-serve # Deploy just the API server # ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh mirage-rs # Deploy just mirage-rs # ./deploy/scripts/fly-deploy.sh console # Deploy just the web console set -euo pipefail SCRIPT_DIR="$(cd "$(dirname "$0")" && pwd)" REPO_ROOT="$(cd "$SCRIPT_DIR/../.." && pwd)" deploy_service() { local service="$1" local fly_dir="$REPO_ROOT/deploy/fly/$service" if [ ! -f "$fly_dir/fly.toml" ]; then echo "Error: No fly.toml found at $fly_dir/fly.toml" exit 1 fi echo "=== Deploying $service ===" # Create the app if it doesn't exist local app_name app_name=$(grep '^app = ' "$fly_dir/fly.toml" | sed 's/app = "\(.*\)"/\1/') if ! fly apps list --json | jq -e ".[] | select(.Name == \"$app_name\")" > /dev/null 2>&1; then echo "Creating app $app_name..." fly apps create "$app_name" --org personal fi # Create volume if the service needs one if grep -q '\[mounts\]' "$fly_dir/fly.toml"; then local vol_name vol_name=$(grep 'source = ' "$fly_dir/fly.toml" | head -1 | sed 's/.*source = "\(.*\)"/\1/') local region region=$(grep 'primary_region' "$fly_dir/fly.toml" | sed 's/.*= "\(.*\)"/\1/') if ! fly volumes list --app "$app_name" --json | jq -e '.[0]' > /dev/null 2>&1; then echo "Creating volume $vol_name in $region..." fly volumes create "$vol_name" --app "$app_name" --region "$region" --size 1 fi fi # Deploy fly deploy --config "$fly_dir/fly.toml" --app "$app_name" --remote-only echo "=== $service deployed ===" echo "" } case "${1:-all}" in all) deploy_service roko-serve deploy_service roko-cli deploy_service console ;; *) deploy_service "$1" ;; esac ``` ### `fly-secrets.sh` ```bash #!/bin/bash # deploy/scripts/fly-secrets.sh # # Set secrets for Roko services on Fly.io. # Reads from .env file or environment variables. # # Usage: # ./deploy/scripts/fly-secrets.sh set -euo pipefail # Load .env if it exists if [ -f .env ]; then set -a source .env set +a fi echo "Setting secrets for roko-serve..." fly secrets set \ ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY:?Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}" \ --app roko-serve echo "Setting secrets for roko-cli..." fly secrets set \ ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY}" \ --app roko-cli if [ -n "${CONSOLE_AUTH_TOKEN:-}" ]; then echo "Setting secrets for roko-console..." fly secrets set \ CONSOLE_AUTH_TOKEN="$CONSOLE_AUTH_TOKEN" \ --app roko-console fi echo "Done. Secrets set." ``` ### `fly-status.sh` ```bash #!/bin/bash # deploy/scripts/fly-status.sh # # Check health of all deployed Roko services. set -euo pipefail services=("roko-cli" "roko-serve" "roko-console" "roko-mirage") printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "SERVICE" "STATUS" "URL" "MACHINES" printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "-------" "------" "---" "--------" for svc in "${services[@]}"; do if fly apps list --json 2>/dev/null | jq -e ".[] | select(.Name == \"$svc\")" > /dev/null 2>&1; then url="https://$svc.fly.dev" machines=$(fly machines list --app "$svc" --json 2>/dev/null | jq 'length') status=$(curl -sf "$url/health" -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" 2>/dev/null || echo "down") if [ "$status" = "200" ]; then printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "$svc" "healthy" "$url" "started:$machines" else printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "$svc" "unhealthy" "$url" "$machines" fi else printf "%-20s %-12s %-40s %-10s\n" "$svc" "not deployed" "-" "-" fi done ``` --- ## Secret Management on Fly.io Provider API keys live as Fly secrets. They become environment variables inside the machine. They never appear in fly.toml, Docker images, or logs. ### Secret Flow ``` deploy command → reads --anthropic-key flag, or $ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, or prompts → runs: fly secrets set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY= --app roko-cli → secrets are encrypted at rest on Fly's infrastructure → injected as env vars when the machine starts ``` ### Detecting Existing Secrets On redeploy, the deploy script checks what secrets are already set: ```bash fly secrets list --app roko-cli --json ``` It only prompts for or sets missing secrets. If the user deploys without `--anthropic-key` and the secret already exists on Fly, it skips that secret. --- ## Cost Estimates Fly.io pricing for Roko services with auto-stop enabled (prices as of early 2026): | Service | VM Size | Memory | Auto-Stop | Estimated Cost | |---|---|---|---|---| | roko-cli | shared-cpu-2x | 2GB | Yes | ~$3-7/mo (active hours only) | | roko-serve | shared-cpu-2x | 1GB | Yes | ~$2-5/mo (active hours only) | | mirage-rs | shared-cpu-2x | 2GB | Yes | ~$3-7/mo (active hours only) | | console | shared-cpu-1x | 256MB | Yes | ~$1-2/mo | | Volumes (1GB each) | — | — | — | ~$0.15/mo per volume | With `auto_stop_machines = "stop"` and `min_machines_running = 0`, machines stop when idle. No CPU/RAM charges while stopped. The machine starts in ~2-3 seconds when a request arrives (cold start for a Rust binary is fast). For a personal deployment where services are used intermittently, the total cost is typically $5-15/month. For always-on production deployments, set `min_machines_running = 1` to eliminate cold starts, at the cost of continuous charges. --- ## Custom Domains When deploying with a custom domain: ```bash # Attach a custom domain to a Fly app fly certs create roko-serve api.example.com # Fly prints DNS instructions: # Add CNAME: api.example.com → roko-serve.fly.dev # Certificate provisions automatically once DNS propagates. ``` Fly handles TLS via Let's Encrypt. The user points a CNAME at `.fly.dev` and Fly provisions the certificate automatically (HTTP-01 challenge). No ACME DNS records, no cert files, no renewal cron. ### Check Certificate Status ```bash $ fly certs show roko-serve api.example.com Hostname: api.example.com Status: Ready Certificate: Issued (expires 2026-09-22) ``` --- ## Console Service: Web Terminal The console service provides browser-based access to any service's terminal via xterm.js. It is a lightweight Caddy reverse proxy that forwards WebSocket connections to each service's ttyd instance over Fly's internal network. ### Caddyfile ``` # deploy/console/Caddyfile { admin off } :3000 { # Health check handle /health { respond "ok" 200 } # WebSocket proxy to service ttyd instances handle /ws/roko-cli { reverse_proxy roko-cli.internal:7681 } handle /ws/roko-serve { reverse_proxy roko-serve.internal:7681 } # Static frontend handle { root * /srv/static file_server } } ``` ### Frontend (xterm.js) The console frontend is a single HTML page with xterm.js that connects to the WebSocket endpoint for a selected service: ```html Roko Console
``` ### Deliverable ``` https://roko-console.fly.dev/ ├── [Tab: Roko CLI] → Live TUI in the browser └── [Tab: Roko Serve] → Live server logs in the browser ``` --- ## Deployment as a Gate Step A plan can include deployment as a verification step in the gate pipeline: ```markdown ### Verification #### INV-005: API Deployment - Deploy to staging: `roko deploy --target fly --env staging` - Health check passes within 30 seconds - Smoke test: `curl https://staging-api.example.com/health` returns 200 ``` The Roko Orchestrator treats deployment like any other gate. If the deploy fails or the health check does not pass, the plan does not advance. This closes the loop from PRD to running software. --- ## Future Deployment Targets Fly.io is the primary cloud target because it has the best CLI tooling and the simplest mental model (one machine = one service). Planned additions: - **Railway**: Similar model to Fly, different CLI - **Bare VPS via SSH**: `roko deploy --target ssh --host user@ip` would rsync the binary and manage it via systemd - **Docker Compose**: For local multi-service testing before cloud deployment (already implemented — see `03-docker.md`) The deployment interface is designed as a trait. Adding a target means implementing `deploy()`, `teardown()`, `status()`, and `logs()`.

1→# Edge and Embedded Deployment
     2→
     3→> Roko's core cognitive primitives can be compiled to a minimal ~500KB binary for deployment on
     4→> resource-constrained devices, edge nodes, IoT gateways, and embedded systems. This document
     5→> covers the minimal feature set, the binary size budget, the no_std considerations, use cases
     6→> for edge deployment, and the relationship to the WASM deployment target.
     7→
     8→
     9→> **Implementation**: Specified
    10→
    11→---
    12→
    13→## Edge Deployment Philosophy
    14→
    15→Edge deployment targets environments where resources are severely constrained: limited memory
    16→(< 64MB), limited storage (< 10MB), limited or intermittent network connectivity, and limited
    17→CPU. Examples include IoT gateways, network edge nodes, embedded Linux devices, and Cloudflare
    18→Workers.
    19→
    20→The design principle: **the core cognitive primitives (Engram, Score, Scorer, Router, Composer)
    21→are pure computation with no I/O dependencies**. They can run anywhere that has a Rust allocator.
    22→The I/O-dependent components (LLM backends, filesystem persistence, process supervision) are
    23→optional and excluded from edge builds.
    24→
    25→This maps directly to the Synapse Architecture's trait system — each trait is independently
    26→implementable. An edge deployment implements `Scorer` and `Router` locally while delegating
    27→`Substrate` (persistence) and `Gate` (verification) to a remote core node.
    28→
    29→---
    30→
    31→## Minimal Feature Set
    32→
    33→Edge builds disable all optional features and compile only the core cognitive kernel:
    34→
    35→```bash
    36→cargo build --release \
    37→    -p roko-core \
    38→    --no-default-features \
    39→    --features "serde,hdc,decay" \
    40→    --target aarch64-unknown-linux-musl
    41→```
    42→
    43→### What Is Included
    44→
    45→| Component | Size Contribution | Purpose |
    46→|---|---|---|
    47→| `Engram` struct + `Score` | ~15KB | Core data type, 7-axis appraisal |
    48→| `ContentHash` (BLAKE3) | ~30KB | Content addressing |
    49→| Synapse traits (6 trait definitions) | ~5KB | Trait interfaces only |
    50→| `DefaultScorer` | ~10KB | Score computation |
    51→| `DefaultRouter` | ~10KB | Selection logic |
    52→| `DefaultComposer` | ~15KB | Context assembly under budget |
    53→| `HdcVector` (HDC primitives) | ~20KB | Hamming similarity, XOR bundling |
    54→| `Decay` (time-based decay) | ~5KB | HalfLife, TTL, Ebbinghaus |
    55→| serde + serde_json | ~200KB | Serialization for IPC |
    56→| Allocator + runtime | ~50KB | Minimal Rust runtime |
    57→| **Total** | **~360KB** | Core cognitive kernel |
    58→
    59→### What Is Excluded
    60→
    61→| Component | Why Excluded | Alternative for Edge |
    62→|---|---|---|
    63→| Tokio async runtime | ~2MB, unnecessary for synchronous edge processing | Use `smol` or bare `poll` |
    64→| LLM provider backends (reqwest + TLS) | ~5MB, requires network | Proxy through core node |
    65→| ratatui TUI | ~500KB, no display on edge | Not applicable |
    66→| Tree-sitter (code parsing) | ~3MB, C FFI dependency | Use pre-computed indexes |
    67→| alloy (Ethereum primitives) | ~10MB, chain-specific | Not needed on edge |
    68→| `FileSubstrate` (JSONL persistence) | Requires writable filesystem | Use `MemorySubstrate` |
    69→| `ProcessSupervisor` | Requires process spawning | Not applicable |
    70→
    71→---
    72→
    73→## Binary Size Budget
    74→
    75→The target for edge deployment is a ~500KB stripped binary (or WASM module). This budget
    76→constrains which components can be included:
    77→
    78→```
    79→Budget: 500KB
    80→
    81→Core cognitive kernel:     ~360KB (72% of budget)
    82→IPC / networking stub:      ~50KB (10% of budget)
    83→Application logic:          ~90KB (18% of budget — user's edge agent)
    84→───────────────────────────────────
    85→Total:                     ~500KB
    86→```
    87→
    88→### Size Optimization
    89→
    90→To achieve the 500KB target, apply aggressive size optimization:
    91→
    92→```toml
    93→# Cargo.toml [profile.release] overrides for edge target
    94→[profile.edge]
    95→inherits = "release"
    96→opt-level = "z"        # Optimize for size (not speed)
    97→lto = "fat"            # Full LTO for maximum dead code elimination
    98→codegen-units = 1      # Single codegen unit
    99→strip = true           # Strip all symbols
   100→panic = "abort"        # No unwinding tables
   101→```
   102→
   103→Build with the edge profile:
   104→
   105→```bash
   106→cargo build --profile edge -p roko-core --no-default-features --features "serde,hdc,decay"
   107→```
   108→
   109→Additional size reduction strategies:
   110→
   111→- Replace `serde_json` with `miniserde` or `nanoserde` (~50KB → ~10KB)
   112→- Use `blake3` with the `no_std` feature (removes threading support, saves ~15KB)
   113→- Replace `BTreeMap` in Engram tags with a fixed-size array for known tag keys
   114→- Use `#[cfg(feature = "edge")]` to gate out any convenience methods that inflate binary size
   115→
   116→---
   117→
   118→## Use Cases
   119→
   120→### 1. Edge Scoring and Pre-Filtering
   121→
   122→An edge node receives a stream of events (sensor data, log entries, API responses) and uses
   123→Roko's `Scorer` to determine which events are novel enough to forward to the core agent:
   124→
   125→```rust
   126→use roko_core::{Engram, Score, Kind, Body};
   127→use roko_std::default_scorer::DefaultScorer;
   128→
   129→fn should_forward(event: &str) -> bool {
   130→    let engram = Engram::builder()
   131→        .kind(Kind::Observation)
   132→        .body(Body::Text(event.to_string()))
   133→        .build();
   134→
   135→    let scorer = DefaultScorer::default();
   136→    let score = scorer.score(&engram);
   137→
   138→    // Forward only high-novelty or high-utility events
   139→    score.novelty > 0.5 || score.utility > 0.7
   140→}
   141→```
   142→
   143→This reduces bandwidth between edge and core by ~80%, matching the 16 T0 Probes pattern from
   144→the dual-process cognition model — most events can be classified without invoking an LLM.
   145→
   146→### 2. Local Knowledge Cache
   147→
   148→An edge node maintains a `MemorySubstrate` with recently relevant Engrams, serving as a local
   149→cache that avoids round-trips to the core agent for repeated queries:
   150→
   151→```rust
   152→use roko_std::memory_substrate::MemorySubstrate;
   153→
   154→let mut cache = MemorySubstrate::new();
   155→
   156→// Store high-utility Engrams locally
   157→if score.utility > 0.8 {
   158→    cache.put(engram).await?;
   159→}
   160→
   161→// Check local cache before forwarding to core
   162→if let Some(cached) = cache.query(Query::similar(&query_engram)).await? {
   163→    return Ok(cached);
   164→}
   165→// Cache miss — forward to core agent
   166→```
   167→
   168→### 3. HDC-Based Similarity Search at the Edge
   169→
   170→Edge nodes can perform HDC vector similarity search locally, enabling fast approximate matching
   171→without network access:
   172→
   173→```rust
   174→use roko_primitives::HdcVector;
   175→
   176→// Pre-computed HDC fingerprints for known patterns
   177→let known_patterns: Vec<(String, HdcVector)> = load_from_flash();
   178→
   179→// Encode incoming data as HDC vector
   180→let query = HdcVector::encode(incoming_data);
   181→
   182→// Find closest match
   183→let best = known_patterns.iter()
   184→    .map(|(label, vec)| (label, query.hamming_similarity(vec)))
   185→    .max_by(|a, b| a.1.partial_cmp(&b.1).unwrap());
   186→```
   187→
   188→HDC vector comparison is ~50ns per 10,000-bit vector on ARM Cortex-A72 — fast enough for
   189→real-time classification at the edge.
   190→
   191→### 4. Offline Agent with Periodic Sync
   192→
   193→An edge agent operates autonomously when disconnected, accumulating Engrams in a
   194→`MemorySubstrate`. When connectivity is restored, it syncs with the core agent:
   195→
   196→```rust
   197→// Offline: accumulate observations
   198→loop {
   199→    let observation = read_sensor();
   200→    let engram = process(observation);
   201→    local_substrate.put(engram).await?;
   202→
   203→    if is_connected() {
   204→        // Online: sync accumulated Engrams to core
   205→        let pending = local_substrate.export().await?;
   206→        send_to_core(pending).await?;
   207→
   208→        // Receive updated knowledge from core
   209→        let updates = receive_from_core().await?;
   210→        for engram in updates {
   211→            local_substrate.put(engram).await?;
   212→        }
   213→    }
   214→
   215→    sleep(tick_interval);
   216→}
   217→```
   218→
   219→---
   220→
   221→## Edge-Core Communication Protocol
   222→
   223→Edge nodes communicate with core agents using a lightweight JSON-RPC protocol over any
   224→available transport (HTTP, MQTT, serial, BLE):
   225→
   226→```json
   227→// Edge → Core: forward high-novelty Engram
   228→{
   229→    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
   230→    "method": "substrate.put",
   231→    "params": {
   232→        "engram": {
   233→            "id": "blake3:a1b2c3...",
   234→            "kind": "Observation",
   235→            "body": {"text": "sensor reading: 42.5"},
   236→            "score": {"novelty": 0.8, "utility": 0.6, "confidence": 0.9},
   237→            "tags": {"source": "edge-node-7", "domain": "sensor"}
   238→        }
   239→    },
   240→    "id": 1
   241→}
   242→
   243→// Core → Edge: knowledge update
   244→{
   245→    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
   246→    "method": "substrate.sync",
   247→    "params": {
   248→        "engrams": [
   249→            {"id": "blake3:d4e5f6...", "kind": "Heuristic", "body": {"text": "..."}}
   250→        ],
   251→        "since_ms": 1712345678000
   252→    },
   253→    "id": 2
   254→}
   255→```
   256→
   257→---
   258→
   259→## Relationship to WASM Deployment
   260→
   261→Edge deployment and WASM deployment share the same core: the cognitive kernel compiled without
   262→I/O dependencies. The difference is the compilation target:
   263→
   264→| Aspect | Edge (native) | WASM |
   265→|---|---|---|
   266→| Target triple | `aarch64-unknown-linux-musl` | `wasm32-wasi` |
   267→| Binary format | ELF static binary | `.wasm` module |
   268→| Runtime | Linux kernel | WASM runtime (wasmtime, browser) |
   269→| Performance | Full native speed, SIMD | ~2-5× slower, limited SIMD |
   270→| Binary size | ~500KB | ~500KB (gzipped) |
   271→| Filesystem | Possible (musl libc) | Not available |
   272→| Networking | Possible (TCP/UDP) | Via host imports only |
   273→
   274→For ARM edge devices running Linux, native edge deployment is preferred (better performance,
   275→full system access). For browser-based or sandboxed environments, WASM deployment is necessary.
   276→The shared feature flag system (`--no-default-features --features "serde,hdc,decay"`) ensures
   277→both targets compile the same cognitive kernel.
   278→
   279→---
   280→
   281→## Current Status
   282→
   283→Edge deployment is at **Tier 3H** priority (P3 — future). The core cognitive primitives already
   284→compile with `--no-default-features` (this is tested in CI), and the `MemorySubstrate` is used
   285→extensively in unit tests. The ~500KB binary size target has not been validated with a dedicated
   286→edge build profile.
   287→
   288→What needs to be done for edge readiness:
   289→
   290→1. Create a `[profile.edge]` in workspace `Cargo.toml` with size-optimized settings
   291→2. Validate that `roko-core` compiles standalone without pulling in Tokio or other heavy deps
   292→3. Measure actual binary size with the edge profile
   293→4. Create an example edge agent (`examples/edge-agent/`) demonstrating the scoring + forwarding
   294→   pattern
   295→5. Define the edge-core sync protocol and implement it in a lightweight transport crate
   296→

# Edge and Embedded Deployment

> Roko's core cognitive primitives can be compiled to a minimal ~500KB binary for deployment on
> resource-constrained devices, edge nodes, IoT gateways, and embedded systems. This document
> covers the minimal feature set, the binary size budget, the no_std considerations, use cases
> for edge deployment, and the relationship to the WASM deployment target.


> **Implementation**: Specified

---

## Edge Deployment Philosophy

Edge deployment targets environments where resources are severely constrained: limited memory
(< 64MB), limited storage (< 10MB), limited or intermittent network connectivity, and limited
CPU. Examples include IoT gateways, network edge nodes, embedded Linux devices, and Cloudflare
Workers.

The design principle: **the core cognitive primitives (Engram, Score, Scorer, Router, Composer)
are pure computation with no I/O dependencies**. They can run anywhere that has a Rust allocator.
The I/O-dependent components (LLM backends, filesystem persistence, process supervision) are
optional and excluded from edge builds.

This maps directly to the Synapse Architecture's trait system — each trait is independently
implementable. An edge deployment implements `Scorer` and `Router` locally while delegating
`Substrate` (persistence) and `Gate` (verification) to a remote core node.

---

## Minimal Feature Set

Edge builds disable all optional features and compile only the core cognitive kernel:

```bash
cargo build --release \
    -p roko-core \
    --no-default-features \
    --features "serde,hdc,decay" \
    --target aarch64-unknown-linux-musl
```

### What Is Included

| Component | Size Contribution | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `Engram` struct + `Score` | ~15KB | Core data type, 7-axis appraisal |
| `ContentHash` (BLAKE3) | ~30KB | Content addressing |
| Synapse traits (6 trait definitions) | ~5KB | Trait interfaces only |
| `DefaultScorer` | ~10KB | Score computation |
| `DefaultRouter` | ~10KB | Selection logic |
| `DefaultComposer` | ~15KB | Context assembly under budget |
| `HdcVector` (HDC primitives) | ~20KB | Hamming similarity, XOR bundling |
| `Decay` (time-based decay) | ~5KB | HalfLife, TTL, Ebbinghaus |
| serde + serde_json | ~200KB | Serialization for IPC |
| Allocator + runtime | ~50KB | Minimal Rust runtime |
| **Total** | **~360KB** | Core cognitive kernel |

### What Is Excluded

| Component | Why Excluded | Alternative for Edge |
|---|---|---|
| Tokio async runtime | ~2MB, unnecessary for synchronous edge processing | Use `smol` or bare `poll` |
| LLM provider backends (reqwest + TLS) | ~5MB, requires network | Proxy through core node |
| ratatui TUI | ~500KB, no display on edge | Not applicable |
| Tree-sitter (code parsing) | ~3MB, C FFI dependency | Use pre-computed indexes |
| alloy (Ethereum primitives) | ~10MB, chain-specific | Not needed on edge |
| `FileSubstrate` (JSONL persistence) | Requires writable filesystem | Use `MemorySubstrate` |
| `ProcessSupervisor` | Requires process spawning | Not applicable |

---

## Binary Size Budget

The target for edge deployment is a ~500KB stripped binary (or WASM module). This budget
constrains which components can be included:

```
Budget: 500KB

Core cognitive kernel:     ~360KB (72% of budget)
IPC / networking stub:      ~50KB (10% of budget)
Application logic:          ~90KB (18% of budget — user's edge agent)
───────────────────────────────────
Total:                     ~500KB
```

### Size Optimization

To achieve the 500KB target, apply aggressive size optimization:

```toml
# Cargo.toml [profile.release] overrides for edge target
[profile.edge]
inherits = "release"
opt-level = "z"        # Optimize for size (not speed)
lto = "fat"            # Full LTO for maximum dead code elimination
codegen-units = 1      # Single codegen unit
strip = true           # Strip all symbols
panic = "abort"        # No unwinding tables
```

Build with the edge profile:

```bash
cargo build --profile edge -p roko-core --no-default-features --features "serde,hdc,decay"
```

Additional size reduction strategies:

- Replace `serde_json` with `miniserde` or `nanoserde` (~50KB → ~10KB)
- Use `blake3` with the `no_std` feature (removes threading support, saves ~15KB)
- Replace `BTreeMap` in Engram tags with a fixed-size array for known tag keys
- Use `#[cfg(feature = "edge")]` to gate out any convenience methods that inflate binary size

---

## Use Cases

### 1. Edge Scoring and Pre-Filtering

An edge node receives a stream of events (sensor data, log entries, API responses) and uses
Roko's `Scorer` to determine which events are novel enough to forward to the core agent:

```rust
use roko_core::{Engram, Score, Kind, Body};
use roko_std::default_scorer::DefaultScorer;

fn should_forward(event: &str) -> bool {
    let engram = Engram::builder()
        .kind(Kind::Observation)
        .body(Body::Text(event.to_string()))
        .build();

    let scorer = DefaultScorer::default();
    let score = scorer.score(&engram);

    // Forward only high-novelty or high-utility events
    score.novelty > 0.5 || score.utility > 0.7
}
```

This reduces bandwidth between edge and core by ~80%, matching the 16 T0 Probes pattern from
the dual-process cognition model — most events can be classified without invoking an LLM.

### 2. Local Knowledge Cache

An edge node maintains a `MemorySubstrate` with recently relevant Engrams, serving as a local
cache that avoids round-trips to the core agent for repeated queries:

```rust
use roko_std::memory_substrate::MemorySubstrate;

let mut cache = MemorySubstrate::new();

// Store high-utility Engrams locally
if score.utility > 0.8 {
    cache.put(engram).await?;
}

// Check local cache before forwarding to core
if let Some(cached) = cache.query(Query::similar(&query_engram)).await? {
    return Ok(cached);
}
// Cache miss — forward to core agent
```

### 3. HDC-Based Similarity Search at the Edge

Edge nodes can perform HDC vector similarity search locally, enabling fast approximate matching
without network access:

```rust
use roko_primitives::HdcVector;

// Pre-computed HDC fingerprints for known patterns
let known_patterns: Vec<(String, HdcVector)> = load_from_flash();

// Encode incoming data as HDC vector
let query = HdcVector::encode(incoming_data);

// Find closest match
let best = known_patterns.iter()
    .map(|(label, vec)| (label, query.hamming_similarity(vec)))
    .max_by(|a, b| a.1.partial_cmp(&b.1).unwrap());
```

HDC vector comparison is ~50ns per 10,000-bit vector on ARM Cortex-A72 — fast enough for
real-time classification at the edge.

### 4. Offline Agent with Periodic Sync

An edge agent operates autonomously when disconnected, accumulating Engrams in a
`MemorySubstrate`. When connectivity is restored, it syncs with the core agent:

```rust
// Offline: accumulate observations
loop {
    let observation = read_sensor();
    let engram = process(observation);
    local_substrate.put(engram).await?;

    if is_connected() {
        // Online: sync accumulated Engrams to core
        let pending = local_substrate.export().await?;
        send_to_core(pending).await?;

        // Receive updated knowledge from core
        let updates = receive_from_core().await?;
        for engram in updates {
            local_substrate.put(engram).await?;
        }
    }

    sleep(tick_interval);
}
```

---

## Edge-Core Communication Protocol

Edge nodes communicate with core agents using a lightweight JSON-RPC protocol over any
available transport (HTTP, MQTT, serial, BLE):

```json
// Edge → Core: forward high-novelty Engram
{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "substrate.put",
    "params": {
        "engram": {
            "id": "blake3:a1b2c3...",
            "kind": "Observation",
            "body": {"text": "sensor reading: 42.5"},
            "score": {"novelty": 0.8, "utility": 0.6, "confidence": 0.9},
            "tags": {"source": "edge-node-7", "domain": "sensor"}
        }
    },
    "id": 1
}

// Core → Edge: knowledge update
{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "substrate.sync",
    "params": {
        "engrams": [
            {"id": "blake3:d4e5f6...", "kind": "Heuristic", "body": {"text": "..."}}
        ],
        "since_ms": 1712345678000
    },
    "id": 2
}
```

---

## Relationship to WASM Deployment

Edge deployment and WASM deployment share the same core: the cognitive kernel compiled without
I/O dependencies. The difference is the compilation target:

| Aspect | Edge (native) | WASM |
|---|---|---|
| Target triple | `aarch64-unknown-linux-musl` | `wasm32-wasi` |
| Binary format | ELF static binary | `.wasm` module |
| Runtime | Linux kernel | WASM runtime (wasmtime, browser) |
| Performance | Full native speed, SIMD | ~2-5× slower, limited SIMD |
| Binary size | ~500KB | ~500KB (gzipped) |
| Filesystem | Possible (musl libc) | Not available |
| Networking | Possible (TCP/UDP) | Via host imports only |

For ARM edge devices running Linux, native edge deployment is preferred (better performance,
full system access). For browser-based or sandboxed environments, WASM deployment is necessary.
The shared feature flag system (`--no-default-features --features "serde,hdc,decay"`) ensures
both targets compile the same cognitive kernel.

---

## Current Status

Edge deployment is at **Tier 3H** priority (P3 — future). The core cognitive primitives already
compile with `--no-default-features` (this is tested in CI), and the `MemorySubstrate` is used
extensively in unit tests. The ~500KB binary size target has not been validated with a dedicated
edge build profile.

What needs to be done for edge readiness:

1. Create a `[profile.edge]` in workspace `Cargo.toml` with size-optimized settings
2. Validate that `roko-core` compiles standalone without pulling in Tokio or other heavy deps
3. Measure actual binary size with the edge profile
4. Create an example edge agent (`examples/edge-agent/`) demonstrating the scoring + forwarding
   pattern
5. Define the edge-core sync protocol and implement it in a lightweight transport crate
