# Current Status and Port Allocation

> This document tracks the implementation status of all deployment features described in the
> 19-deployment section, the port allocation table for all Roko services, and the Tier 3H
> roadmap for deployment-related work items.


> **Implementation**: Specified

---

## Implementation Status Overview

| Feature | Status | Priority | Blocking Dependencies |
|---|---|---|---|
| Native build (x86_64, aarch64) | **Working** | Tier 0 | None — the workspace builds and tests |
| Docker images | **Not built** | Tier 1 | Need static Dockerfiles in `docker/` |
| cargo install | **Not published** | Tier 1 | Need publish metadata in Cargo.toml |
| Homebrew tap | **Not created** | Tier 2 | Needs cargo-dist + release pipeline |
| Release pipeline (release-plz + cargo-dist) | **Not configured** | Tier 2 | Need release-plz.toml, cliff.toml, dist-workspace.toml |
| Daemon mode (launchd) | **Scaffolded** | Tier 3H | IPC protocol, subscription system |
| Daemon mode (systemd) | **Designed** | Tier 3H | Same as launchd |
| Fly.io deployment | **Not configured** | Tier 2 | Need fly.toml per service, deploy scripts |
| WASM target | **Feature flags exist** | Tier 3H | Need end-to-end validation |
| Edge target | **Designed** | P3 | Need edge build profile, validation |
| Subscription configuration | **Designed** | Tier 3H | Daemon mode |
| Multi-repo coordination | **Designed** | Tier 3H | Daemon mode, subscription system |
| Secret management (env vars) | **Working** | Tier 0 | None — roko-agent reads env vars |
| Secret management (dotenvy) | **Not wired** | Tier 2 | Need dotenvy dependency |
| Secret management (keychain) | **Not wired** | Tier 3H | Need keyring dependency |
| Secret management (${VAR} interpolation) | **Not implemented** | Tier 2 | Config parser update |
| Remote orchestrator (roko-serve) | **Scaffold** | Tier 3H | Need HTTP API wiring |
| Production hardening: adaptive timeouts | **Implemented** | Tier 2G | In roko-agent |
| Production hardening: backoff + retry | **Implemented** | Tier 2G | In roko-agent |
| Production hardening: RetryAction enum | **Implemented** | Tier 2G.17 | In roko-agent |
| Production hardening: per-provider semaphores | **Scaffolded** | Tier 2G | Designed, not wired |
| Production hardening: graceful shutdown | **Partial** | Tier 2 | ProcessSupervisor handles agents |
| Production hardening: dedup cache | **Designed** | Tier 3H | Need implementation |
| Production hardening: hedged requests | **Designed** | P3 | Need implementation |
| Console (web terminal) | **Not built** | Tier 2 | Need Caddyfile, xterm.js frontend |
| Self-update (axoupdater) | **Not wired** | Tier 2 | Needs cargo-dist release pipeline |
| Shell completions | **Not wired** | Tier 2 | Need clap_complete integration |
| Doctor subcommand | **Scaffold** | Tier 2 | Need full health checks |

---

## Port Allocation Table

All Roko services and their assigned ports:

| Port | Service | Protocol | Binding | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3000 | Roko Console (web terminal) | HTTPS | Public | Caddy proxy → ttyd |
| 7681 | ttyd (per-service, internal) | WSS | Internal | WebSocket-to-PTY bridge |
| 8080 | roko-serve HTTP API | HTTPS | Public | REST + SSE |
| 8080 | roko-cli server mode | HTTPS | Public | Same API as roko-serve |
| 8443 | roko-serve WebSocket | WSS | Public | Bidirectional event stream |
| 8545 | mirage-rs JSON-RPC | HTTPS | Public | Anvil-compatible EVM RPC |
| 9090 | Webhook ingress | HTTPS | Public | GitHub/GitLab webhooks |
| 9100 | Roko TUI headless RPC | JSON-RPC | Local | For programmatic TUI control |

### Fly.io Internal Ports

On Fly.io, services communicate over the private 6PN network using `.internal` DNS:

| Internal Address | Port | Service |
|---|---|---|
| `roko-cli.internal` | 8080 | Roko CLI orchestrator |
| `roko-serve.internal` | 8080 | Roko HTTP API |
| `roko-mirage.internal` | 8545 | mirage-rs EVM fork |
| `roko-console.internal` | 3000 | Web terminal console |

### Port Conflicts

When running multiple services locally (via docker-compose or direct), map to non-conflicting
host ports:

```yaml
# docker-compose port mapping
services:
  roko-cli:
    ports: ["8080:8080"]     # CLI on 8080
  roko-serve:
    ports: ["8081:8080"]     # Serve on 8081 (remapped)
  mirage:
    ports: ["8545:8545"]     # mirage-rs on 8545
  console:
    ports: ["8443:3000"]     # Console on 8443
```

---

## Tier 3H Deployment Roadmap

The deployment-related work items in the implementation plan, organized by priority:

### Tier 1 — Foundation (Can Do Now)

These items have no dependencies beyond the existing codebase:

| Item | Description | Estimated Effort |
|---|---|---|
| Static Dockerfiles | Create `docker/*.Dockerfile` (slim + full) | 1 day |
| Deploy scripts | Create `deploy/scripts/fly-*.sh` | Half day |
| fly.toml configs | Create `deploy/fly/*/fly.toml` per service | Half day |
| docker-compose.yml | Create `docker/docker-compose.yml` | 2 hours |
| Publish metadata | Add `publish = true`, description, keywords to Cargo.toml | 2 hours |
| .dockerignore | Create `docker/.dockerignore` | 15 minutes |

### Tier 2 — Distribution Pipeline

Depends on Tier 1 (having something to deploy):

| Item | Description | Estimated Effort |
|---|---|---|
| release-plz setup | Create `release-plz.toml`, `cliff.toml` | Half day |
| cargo-dist setup | Run `cargo dist init`, configure targets | Half day |
| CI workflows | Create release-plz.yml, release.yml, docker.yml | 1 day |
| Homebrew tap | Create `nunchi/homebrew-roko` repo | 2 hours |
| axoupdater | Wire self-update notifications + `roko update` | Half day |
| clap_complete | Wire shell completions for all binaries | 2 hours |
| dotenvy integration | Add .env file loading to CLI main() | 1 hour |
| ${VAR} interpolation | Implement in config parser | 2 hours |
| Console service | Create Caddyfile + xterm.js frontend + Dockerfile | 1 day |

### Tier 3H — Daemon and Remote (P2)

Depends on the core framework being stable:

| Item | Description | Estimated Effort |
|---|---|---|
| Daemon infrastructure | IPC socket, event loop, signal handling | 2-3 days |
| launchd integration | Plist generation, lifecycle commands | 1 day |
| systemd integration | Unit file generation, journald, watchdog | 1 day |
| Subscription system | Cron, file watch, webhook triggers | 2-3 days |
| Multi-repo scheduler | Priority queue, per-repo isolation, agent limits | 1-2 days |
| roko-serve HTTP API | Wire Axum endpoints to orchestration engine | 2-3 days |
| Authentication | API key generation, scopes, rate limiting | 1-2 days |
| Webhook ingress | GitHub/GitLab webhook handling | 1 day |
| Keychain integration | Add keyring crate, wire into secret resolution | Half day |

### P3 — Future

These are designed but not in the near-term roadmap:

| Item | Description |
|---|---|
| WASM validation | End-to-end WASM build, wasm-bindgen wrapper crate |
| Edge build profile | Create `[profile.edge]`, measure binary size, example agent |
| Hedged requests | Implement dual-provider hedging with cost limits |
| Playground infrastructure | Per-user Fly Machines, warm pool, landing page |
| Content-addressed cache | LRU dedup cache for LLM responses |

---

## Configuration File Locations

Summary of all configuration file paths for deployment:

| File | Location | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `roko.toml` | Project root | Per-project configuration |
| `.roko/config.toml` | Project `.roko/` dir | Per-project overrides (alternative location) |
| `~/.config/roko/config.toml` | XDG config | Global configuration |
| `~/.config/roko/daemon.env` | XDG config | Daemon environment variables (Linux systemd) |
| `~/.local/state/roko/` | XDG state | Daemon logs, subscription state, cost history |
| `~/.local/share/roko/` | XDG data | Learned patterns, playbooks (portable across projects) |
| `.env` | Project root | Per-project secrets (gitignored) |
| `release-plz.toml` | Workspace root | Release pipeline configuration |
| `cliff.toml` | Workspace root | Changelog generation configuration |
| `dist-workspace.toml` | Workspace root | cargo-dist binary distribution configuration |
| `deploy/fly/*/fly.toml` | Deploy directory | Per-service Fly.io configuration |
| `docker/*.Dockerfile` | Docker directory | Per-service Docker build definitions |
| `docker/docker-compose.yml` | Docker directory | Local development stack |

---

## Deployment Targets Summary

| Target | Binary | Features | Binary Size | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Native x86_64 macOS | roko-cli | All | ~30MB | **Working** |
| Native aarch64 macOS | roko-cli | All | ~30MB | **Working** |
| Native x86_64 Linux (glibc) | roko-cli | All | ~30MB | **Working** |
| Native x86_64 Linux (musl) | roko-cli | All | ~35MB | **Working** (CI cross-compile) |
| Native aarch64 Linux (musl) | roko-cli | All | ~35MB | **Working** (CI cross-compile) |
| Docker slim | roko-cli | All | ~20MB image | **Designed** |
| Docker full (tmux+ttyd) | roko-cli | All + web terminal | ~80MB image | **Designed** |
| WASM (wasm32-wasi) | roko-core | Core traits, HDC, serde | ~500KB | **Feature flags exist** |
| Edge (aarch64 musl, size-opt) | roko-core | Core traits, HDC | ~500KB | **Designed** |
| Daemon (macOS launchd) | roko-cli | All + IPC | ~30MB | **Scaffolded** |
| Daemon (Linux systemd) | roko-cli | All + IPC | ~30MB | **Designed** |
| Cloud (Fly.io) | roko-serve | All | Docker image | **Designed** |

---

## Test Matrix

Deployment features should be validated against this test matrix:

| Test | What It Validates | Automation |
|---|---|---|
| `cargo build --workspace --release` | Native build succeeds | CI (existing) |
| `cargo test --workspace` | All 1,000+ tests pass | CI (existing) |
| `cargo build -p roko-core --no-default-features` | Minimal build works | CI (add) |
| `docker build -f docker/roko-cli.Dockerfile .` | Docker slim image builds | CI (add) |
| `docker compose up --wait` | Local stack starts healthy | CI (add) |
| `fly deploy --config deploy/fly/roko-serve/fly.toml` | Fly.io deployment works | Manual initially |
| `roko doctor` | Installation health check | Manual |
| `roko daemon install && roko daemon status` | Daemon installs and runs | Manual |
| `curl https://roko-serve.fly.dev/health` | Remote health check | Uptime monitor |

input.name: research-cargo-dist-sbom
input.prompt:
Research the following topics thoroughly via web search. For each topic, I need: current state of the art (2025-2026), key features, Rust integration patterns, configuration examples, and references to papers or docs.

1. **cargo-dist v0.31+**: Latest features including singular announcements for monorepos, CycloneDX SBOM generation, axoupdater self-update, npm wrapper packages, install receipts. How does it integrate with release-plz? What are the latest configuration options in dist-workspace.toml?

2. **Supply chain security for Rust binaries**: Sigstore/cosign binary signing, SLSA provenance attestations, cargo-vet for dependency auditing, cargo-deny for license compliance. How do these integrate into CI pipelines?

3. **cargo-binstall**: Latest protocol for pre-built binary distribution. How does it discover binaries? What metadata goes in Cargo.toml?

Return detailed findings with specific version numbers, configuration snippets, and links to official documentation.

input.prompt:
Research the following topics thoroughly via web search. For each topic, I need: current state (2025-2026), Rust integration, configuration, performance characteristics, and references.

1. **WASI Preview 2 (P2) / Component Model**: What is the current state of WASI P2? How does the component model work? What interfaces are available (wasi:http, wasi:io, wasi:clocks)? How do Rust crates compile to WASI P2 components? What tooling exists (cargo-component, wasm-tools)?

2. **Fermyon Spin**: How does Spin work for serverless WASM? What is the Spin SDK for Rust? How are components deployed? What is the spin.toml configuration format? What triggers are available (HTTP, Redis, timer)?

3. **wasmCloud**: How does wasmCloud work? What are capability providers? How does the lattice networking work? What is the wasmCloud deployment model?

4. **Cloudflare Workers with Rust/WASM**: How do Workers compile Rust to WASM? What is the worker-rs crate? What are the resource limits (CPU time, memory)?

5. **WASM SIMD**: Current browser support for WASM SIMD128. Performance characteristics for bitwise operations (relevant to HDC vectors).

Return detailed findings with configuration snippets, version numbers, and performance data.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about cargo-dist v0.31 and related features:

## CycloneDX SBOM Support

cargo-dist has added support for generating CycloneDX Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) files for Rust projects using the cargo-cyclonedx tool. This data is stored as a standalone bom.xml file which is distributed with your software.

## Axoupdater Changes

Previously, dist always shipped the latest version of the standalone updater provided via axoupdater, but beginning with this release, dist will always package the same version of axoupdater when building and shipping your app. If you prefer the old behavior and would like to receive whatever's the latest, you can set always-use-latest-updater = true in your configuration.

## NPM Wrapper Improvements

In previous versions, enabling the npm installer would require your package to be distributed using .tar.gz archives on all platforms, including Windows. This release removes that restriction by rewriting a section of the npm installer code.

## Monorepo Support

The search results indicate that cargo-dist supports monorepo configurations, though more specific details about v0.31's announcements regarding singular versus monorepo handling would require additional documentation searches.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about cargo-dist, release-plz integration, and dist-workspace.toml configuration:

## Key Findings:

### dist-workspace.toml Configuration

The new dist-workspace.toml file format uses separate configuration files that were originally created for non-Rust projects. This allows you to configure cargo-dist without adding content to your root Cargo.toml, and newly cargo-initted Rust projects will use this new format.

### cargo-dist and release-plz Integration

When integrating with cargo-dist, you should set git_release_enable to false in release-plz configuration since cargo-dist will handle creating GitHub releases instead.

In release-plz.toml configuration, you can set options like changelog_config, changelog_update, dependencies_update, git_tag_enable, pr_labels, allow_dirty, publish_allow_dirty, and semver_check.

### Tag Format Handling

dist interprets a git tag of "v1.0.0" as "Announce/Release the whole workspace" (Unified Announcement) and "my-app-v1.0.0" or "my-app/v1.0.0" as "Announce/Release that one package" (Singular Announcement).

### Configuration in Cargo.toml

The default configuration includes settings like cargo-dist-version, ci backends to support, installers to generate, and target platforms to build apps for, all stored under [workspace.metadata.dist].

The search results contain references to the official cargo-dist documentation which likely has more detailed configuration options for dist-workspace.toml available in their workspace docs.

Based on the search results, here's information about cargo-binstall and its approach to pre-built binary distribution using Cargo.toml metadata:

## Overview

Cargo binstall provides a low-complexity mechanism for installing rust binaries as an alternative to building from source (via cargo install) or manually downloading packages, and is intended to work with existing CI artifacts and infrastructure with minimal overhead for package maintainers.

## Metadata Discovery in Cargo.toml

Binstall works with existing CI-built binary outputs, with configuration via [package.metadata.binstall] keys in the relevant crate manifest. To get started, you can add a [package.metadata.binstall] section to your Cargo.toml, with template variables like pkg-url, bin-dir, and pkg-fmt that are templated to support different names for different versions and architectures.

## Default Auto-Detection

Binstall is generally smart enough to auto-detect artifacts in most situations. The default value for pkg-url will depend on the repository of the package and is set up to work with GitHub releases, GitLab releases, bitbucket downloads, source forge downloads and Codeberg releases. If your package already uses any of these URLs, you shouldn't need to set anything.

## Manual Configuration

If a package fails to install, you can manually specify the pkg-url, bin-dir, and pkg-fmt as needed at the command line, with values as documented in SUPPORT.md.

I've performed the web search on Rust binary supply chain security with the tools and standards you specified. Here are the key findings from recent 2025 sources:

## Rust Supply Chain Security Practices

For Rust, cargo audit, cargo deny, and cargo vet are core tools; SBOMs are generated via cargo-cyclonedx; containers are signed with cosign; and builds should include SLSA provenance at your organization's accepted level.

## Integration of Key Tools

Artifacts are signed with Sigstore cosign and developers should strive for SLSA-aligned provenance from their build systems. These practices are fast-becoming table stakes in Europe and the U.S. for demonstrating a trustworthy supply chain.

## How Sigstore & Cosign Work

Sigstore's approach uses short-lived certificates issued by the Fulcio certificate authority, tied to OIDC tokens, and every signing event is recorded in an append-only transparency log called Rekor. Cosign is the Sigstore tool for signing and verifying container images, storing signatures in the same OCI registry as the image itself.

## 2025 Supply Chain Context

The GhostAction attack of early 2025 was a watershed moment for GitHub Actions supply chain security, with attackers compromising a widely-used third-party action by modifying workflow code to exfiltrate secrets.

## Implementation Status

Tooling for implementing SLSA Level 2 is mature: cosign, Syft, Kyverno, GUAC, and GitHub OIDC make it achievable in weeks. Open standards have stabilized—SLSA 1.0, SPDX 3, Sigstore—enabling vendor-neutral pipelines.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about cargo-component, wasm-tools, WASI P2, and Rust compilation:

## cargo-component and WASI P2 Compilation

cargo-component is a Cargo subcommand for creating WebAssembly components based on the component model proposal. 

### Current Compilation Approach

cargo-component currently uses the wasm32-wasip1 target to produce core Wasm modules, and then adapts them into WASIp2 aka "preview2" components. The adaptation is automatically performed when wasm32-wasip1 is targeted using a built-in WASI adapter snapshotted out of the Wasmtime repository.

### Native Upstream Support

The Rust compiler now has an upstream Rust wasm32-wasip2 target that produces components. As of Rust 1.82, there is now an upstream Rust wasm32-wasip2 target, which produces components using plain cargo, and doesn't use cargo-component.

### Use Cases

If you only need WASI interfaces, then the wasm32-wasip2 target and the wasi crate should work. If you have non-WASI WIT interfaces, whether third-party WIT interfaces or your own custom WIT interfaces, then use cargo component.

The search results show that as of 2025, there have been significant developments in native Rust support for WASI P2 compilation, though cargo-component remains useful for components requiring non-WASI WIT interfaces.

Links: [{"title":"WASI Preview 2 vs WASIX (2026): The WebAssembly System Interface Battle | wasmRuntime.com","url":"https://wasmruntime.com/en/blog/wasi-preview2-vs-wasix-2026"},{"title":"WASI and the WebAssembly Component Model: Current Status - eunomia","url":"https://eunomia.dev/blog/2025/02/16/wasi-and-the-webassembly-component-model-current-status/"},{"title":"GitHub - WebAssembly/component-model: Repository for design and specification of the Component Model · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/WebAssembly/component-model"},{"title":"Introduction · WASI.dev","url":"https://wasi.dev/"},{"title":"Bytecode Alliance — WebAssembly: An Updated Roadmap for Developers","url":"https://bytecodealliance.org/articles/webassembly-the-updated-roadmap-for-developers"},{"title":"WebAssembly in 2026: Three Years of \"Almost Ready\" - Java Code Geeks","url":"https://www.javacodegeeks.com/2026/04/webassembly-in-2026-three-years-of-almost-ready.html"},{"title":"The State of WebAssembly – 2024 and 2025","url":"https://platform.uno/blog/state-of-webassembly-2024-2025/"},{"title":"WASI 1.0: You Won't Know When WebAssembly Is Everywhere in 2026 - The New Stack","url":"https://thenewstack.io/wasi-1-0-you-wont-know-when-webassembly-is-everywhere-in-2026/"},{"title":"Wasm 3.0: No Component Model and No 'Docker Moment' - The New Stack","url":"https://thenewstack.io/wasm-3-0-no-component-model-and-no-docker-moment/"},{"title":"WASI 2.0 Components: Portable, Fast Plugins | by Nikulsinh Rajput | Medium","url":"https://medium.com/@hadiyolworld007/wasi-2-0-components-portable-fast-plugins-58c24d891584"}]

Based on the search results, here's the current state of WASI Preview 2 and the Component Model in 2025-2026:

## WASI Preview 2 Status

WASM Component Model, WASI Preview 2, and Wasmtime 30+ shipped in 2025. WASI Preview 2 was stabilized in late 2024 and widely adopted through 2025, adding networking, sockets, HTTP client/server support, and the Component Model foundation. Wasmtime, Spin, and wasmCloud all fully implement it.

The Bytecode Alliance changed to dot releases instead of referring to releases as previews. "Preview 2" is now referred to as WASI 0.2. The next release will be WASI 0.3, formerly 'Preview 3' and then the WASI 1.0 release will be when WASI is standardized.

## WASI Interfaces (wasi:http and wasi:io)

The wasi-http world is a set of interfaces that allows your module to send and receive HTTP requests. wasi-io contains interfaces for working with different kinds of host streams. wasi-sockets brings the ability to work with TCP/UDP sockets.

## Component Model

The Component Model is currently being incrementally developed and stabilized as part of WASI Preview 2. The WebAssembly Component Model standardizes how larger applications can be built from smaller WebAssembly components. It builds on Interface Types (now embodied as WIT, the WebAssembly Interface Definition Language) to allow high-level data (strings, structured records, etc.) to be passed between modules. The Component Model lets developers treat WebAssembly modules like "LEGO bricks" that plug together securely and interoperably.

## WASI 0.3 (Preview 3) Timeline

The subsequent WASI Preview 3 milestone will be primarily concerned with the addition of async and thread support. The planned release of WASI 0.3.0 is set to come in 2026, likely in February. Asynchronous I/O in WASI 0.3 should land in 2025, covering not only network sockets but also files and other operations, so that languages like Go or Node that rely on async patterns can integrate cleanly.

# Cargo-Binstall: Binary Discovery and Metadata

## How Binstall Discovers Binaries

Cargo-binstall employs a multi-strategy approach to locate pre-compiled binaries:

1. **Repository Search**: It first fetches crate metadata from crates.io and searches the linked repository for matching releases and artifacts matching the target platform
2. **Quickinstall Fallback**: If repository artifacts aren't found, it falls back to the quickinstall third-party artifact host
3. **Alternate Targets**: It attempts alternate target variations as supported
4. **Source Compilation**: Finally, it defaults to `cargo install` as a last resort

The tool automatically detects most artifacts intelligently, though maintainers can provide explicit metadata to improve discovery.

## Cargo.toml Metadata Fields

Maintainers can add binstall-specific metadata under `[package.metadata.binstall]` in their Cargo.toml. According to the documentation, this section allows specifying:

- Package URL templates
- Binary directory locations  
- Package formats
- Signing information
- Strategy preferences (enabled/disabled)

These fields help binstall locate the appropriate binary package for specific versions and target platforms.

## Template Variables

The documentation references template variables like `{ repo }`, `{ version }`, `{ name }`, `{ target }`, and `{ archive-format }` for constructing dynamic URLs to binary packages.

## Supported Platforms and Formats

Binstall provides pre-built binaries for:
- **Linux**: x86_64, armv7, arm64
- **macOS**: Intel, Apple Silicon, Universal
- **Windows**: x86_64, ARM64

Common archive formats include `.tgz` and `.zip`, with support for additional formats like `.txz` via manual configuration.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding cargo-dist's 2026 latest version and changelog information related to SBOM, install receipts, and other features:

## Latest Version

The latest version is 0.31.0, released on 2026-02-23.

## Key Features in Recent Releases

### SBOM Support
cargo-dist has added support for generating CyloneDX Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) files for Rust projects using the cargo-cyclonedx tool. This data is stored as a standalone bom.xml file which is distributed with your software.

### Installation Receipts
Recent releases switched the preferred installation location to GitHub, where if the user had both GitHub Releases and Axo Releases selected, generated installation scripts and receipts would prefer to fetch from GitHub.

### New Mirrors Feature
Version 0.31.0 includes several new features, including the major introduction of mirrors that installers can fallback to, and adds a new hosting method called "simple" which supports static file hosting.

### Other Notable Updates
Version 0.30.4 (released 2026-02-16) contains minor bugfixes and updates dependencies, including a rimraf upgrade that resolves a CVE.

You can find the complete changelog at the official cargo-dist GitHub repository for more detailed information about all changes and features.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Fermyon Spin serverless WASM with Rust SDK, focusing on spin.toml, triggers, HTTP, and Redis:

## Overview

Spin is an open source developer tool for building and running serverless functions and microservices with WebAssembly. Fermyon's Spin works with mainstream languages like JS/TS, Python, .NET, Rust, and Go.

## Key Features

Spin has a built-in HTTP web server and pub-sub Redis triggers, routing requests and messages to components. Spin has built-in support for connecting to Redis, MySQL, and PostgreSQL databases through the various language SDKs.

## spin.toml Configuration

The spin.toml file is the manifest file, which tells Spin what events should trigger what components. In this case the trigger is HTTP, for a Web application, and components are associated with routes.

An example structure shows:
The manifest includes trigger configuration (like `[[trigger.http]]` with a route) and component configuration with the Wasm module source.

## HTTP Triggers

The HTTP trigger type in Spin is a web server. When an application has HTTP triggers, Spin listens for incoming requests and, based on the application manifest, it routes them to a component, which provides an HTTP response.

## Redis Support and Triggers

Available templates include http-rust for HTTP request handlers and redis-rust for Redis message handlers in Rust.

## Rust SDK

Spin aims to have best-in-class support for building components in Rust. The Rust SDK provides libraries for building components with features including HTTP handling, Redis connectivity, and database access.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about wasmCloud capability providers, lattice networking, and the deployment model for 2025:

## Lattice Networking Model

The lattice is a self-forming, self-healing mesh network that provides a unified, flat topology across any number of environments, clouds, browsers, or even hardware. A lattice allows components, providers, and hosts to communicate with each other across any number of infrastructure barriers.

The wasmCloud lattice is built on NATS, an open source connective technology hosted by the Cloud Native Computing Foundation (CNCF). NATS enables secure application-layer networking across diverse environments including edge, different vendors' clouds, and on-premise datacenters.

## Capability Providers

Providers are swappable host plugins—executables (usually dedicated to longer-lived processes) that deliver common functionalities called capabilities. External providers run on the wasmCloud lattice like components—they are "external" in the sense that they are external to any given wasmCloud host. All third-party providers are external.

Since external providers are standalone entities on the lattice, they may be scaled and updated independently of any given wasmCloud host.

## 2025 Roadmap Changes

The wasmCloud Q3 2025 Roadmap sets out plans for an overhaul to capability providers in the next major release of wasmCloud. This overhaul transitions providers to a "wRPC server" model in which WIT interfaces are served via one of the transports available with wRPC (e.g., TCP, NATS, QUIC, or UDP), enabling capability implementations to be written in any language and to be deployed independently in containers.

Additionally, the wasmCloud Q3 2025 Roadmap sets out plans for an overhaul to the transport layer in the next major release of wasmCloud. In the new approach, wRPC serves as the transport layer, with NATS as the default underlying transport.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Mozilla SOPS and its encryption capabilities:

## Overview

SOPS is an editor of encrypted files that supports YAML, JSON, ENV, INI and BINARY formats and encrypts with AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault, HuaweiCloud KMS, age, and PGP.

## Key Encryption Providers

SOPS supports multiple key providers:

- **AWS KMS**: All a user of SOPS needs is valid AWS credentials and the necessary permissions on KMS keys. SOPS has the ability to use KMS in multiple AWS accounts by assuming roles in each account. Using roles, a single file can be encrypted with KMS keys in multiple accounts, thus increasing reliability and ease of use.

- **Age**: The default order is age,pgp. Offline methods are tried first and then the remaining ones.

- **GCP KMS and Azure Key Vault**: You can use SOPS CLI to encrypt Kubernetes secrets with OpenPGP, AWS KMS, GCP KMS and Azure Key Vault.

## File Format Support

SOPS encrypts individual values within YAML, JSON, ENV, and INI files, leaving the keys visible. SOPS encrypts only values in YAML/JSON, keeping keys readable for diffing.

## Recent Resources

A comprehensive 2026 guide covers practical usage with these providers, noting that SOPS supports age, GPG, and AWS KMS key providers with Git workflow integration.

Based on my web search results, here's the current state of OpenTelemetry Rust crates in 2025-2026:

## Current OpenTelemetry Rust State (2025-2026)

### Overall Status
As of 2026, the OpenTelemetry Rust implementation is actively maintained, with Beta support for traces and stable support for logs and metrics.

### Core Crate Versions

**opentelemetry-otlp**: The latest version is 0.31.1 (released 2026-03-19), with version 0.31.0 released 2025-09-25. This crate contains the OpenTelemetry Exporter implementation for OTLP.

**opentelemetry (API)**: All crates in this repo follows same version (0.30.0 for the latest release), with this release also upgrading Metrics-SDK to stable!

**tracing-opentelemetry**: This crate provides a subscriber that connects spans from multiple systems into a trace and emits them to OpenTelemetry-compatible distributed tracing systems for processing and visualization. The OpenTelemetry tracing specification is stable but the underlying opentelemetry crate is not so some breaking changes will still occur in this crate as well.

### Minimum Rust Version
OpenTelemetry is built against the latest stable release. The minimum supported version is 1.75.0.

### Key Architecture Notes
Actual implementation and the heavy lifting of telemetry data collection, processing, and exporting are delegated to the opentelemetry-sdk crate and various exporter crates such as opentelemetry-otlp. opentelemetry-otlp is an exporter to send telemetry (logs, metrics and traces) in the OTLP format to an endpoint accepting OTLP.

I've completed the web search for your query on canary deployment, progressive rollout patterns, Fly.io machines API, and blue-green deployment strategies. Here's a summary of the relevant findings:

## Key Deployment Strategies Overview

Blue/green deployment and canary release are two CI/CD deployment strategies designed to minimize downtime, reduce the blast radius of bad releases, and enable fast rollbacks. Blue/green swaps all traffic between two identical environments instantly, while canary incrementally routes a small percentage of users to the new version before a full rollout.

## Canary Deployment Patterns

In a canary deployment, the new version of the application is deployed to a small subset of the infrastructure or a specific group of users. This "canary" release is monitored closely to identify any issues or performance problems.

The fundamental principle behind canary deployments is progressive validation. You start with a small percentage, maybe 5-10% of traffic, and watch closely. If metrics remain stable for a defined period, perhaps 15-30 minutes, you increase to 25%.

## Blue-Green Deployment Benefits

One of the benefits of the blue-green deployment is that it is simple, fast, well-understood, and easy to implement. Rollback is also straightforward, because you can simply flip traffic back to the old environment in case of any issues.

## Hybrid Approaches

A common pattern is blue-green deployment with canary testing: maintain blue and green environments, but when switching, first route a small percentage of traffic to green while most stays on blue.

A Complete Guide to Secrets Management in 2025 - DEVOPS DONE RIGHT.","url":"https://opstree.com/blog/2025/08/05/what-is-hashicorp-vault-a-complete-guide-to-secrets-management-in-2025/"},{"title":"Hashicorp Vault: Secret Management Engines | by Sebastian | Medium","url":"https://admantium.medium.com/hashicorp-vault-secret-management-engines-1245a74eccd4"},{"title":"AWS secrets engine | Vault | HashiCorp Developer","url":"https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/secrets/aws"}]

Based on the search results, here's an overview of HashiCorp Vault's secret management concepts, including secrets engines, auth methods, and policies:

## What is HashiCorp Vault?

Vault is an identity-based secrets and encryption management system that centralizes secret management, rotates old credentials, generates credentials on demand, audits client interactions, and supports regulatory compliance. A secret is anything that you want to tightly control access to, such as tokens, API keys, passwords, encryption keys or certificates.

## Secrets Engines

Hashicorp Vault secrets engine are plugins that interact with the Vault storage backend and router component. They provide both CLI access, and REST API like endpoints for creating, reading, writing and deleting data. Secrets engines are at the heart of Hashicorp Vault. They enable the full lifecycle management of all finds of sensitive data, from credentials to encryption keys.

Secret engines vary greatly in what they offer but generically are used to manage static or ephemeral secrets. Examples include KV - Secure secrets at rest for static secret integrations; AWS - Generate ephemeral AWS STS access keys for IAM Roles; MySQL/RDS - Short lived database access via dynamically generated credentials.

## Authentication Methods

Before a human or machine can gain access, an administrator must configure Vault with an auth method. Authentication is the process by which human or machine-supplied information is verified against an internal or external system. Vault supports multiple auth methods, including Kubernetes, AWS IAM, LDAP, GitHub, Azure AD, and JWT/OIDC, enabling flexible identity-based access control.

## Policies

Everything in Vault is path-based, and policies are no exception. Policies provide a declarative way to grant or forbid access to certain paths and operations in Vault. Policies are deny by default, so an empty policy grants no permission in the system.

Using Vault's policy-based access control, you can ensure that apps, users, or systems only get access to the secrets they're authorized for—least privilege enforced.

# Cargo-Dist Version Summary (v0.25 - Latest)

## Version 0.31.0 (2026-02-23)

**Key Features:**
- **Simple hosting (mirrors):** New hosting method supporting static file hosts as fallback sources alongside GitHub
- **npm-shrinkwrap.json control:** Option to disable shrinkwrap generation for looser dependency specification
- **Generic project build directory:** Configurable `out-dir` setting for specifying artifact output locations
- **Installation safety:** "Reduce risk of an interrupted installation leading to a partial installation"

## Version 0.30.4 (2026-02-16)

Minor bugfixes and dependency updates addressing security vulnerabilities in rimraf/glob packages (not exploitable in dist's usage patterns).

## Version 0.30.3 (2025-12-14)

**Key Fixes:**
- File permissions correction when running shell installer under `sudo`
- macOS codesigning configuration now properly respected

## Version 0.30.2 (2025-10-31)

Added support for customizing GitHub attestations during the `announce` phase.

## Version 0.30.1 (2025-10-29)

- Updated default macOS runners to macos-15-intel
- GitHub attestations permissions moved to job level
- Host job skipped if plan fails

## Version 0.30.0 (2025-09-07)

**Major Features:**
- **ZIP compression:** Archives now compressed; Unix permissions preserved
- **Installer environment variables:** All installer configuration variables now have branded `{APP_NAME}_*` versions
- **Snap curl detection:** Shell installer rejects Snap-installed curl due to sandbox limitations
- **PowerShell proxy support:** Respects `HTTPS_PROXY` and `ANY_PROXY` environment variables
- **macOS code signing:** Supports `--options` flag via `CODESIGN_OPTIONS`
- **Linux arm64 native runners:** Defaults to native arm64 GitHub runners instead of cross-compilation
- **Customizable attestations:** Configure which artifacts receive attestations and build phase

## Version 0.29.0 (2025-07-31)

**Major Features:**
- **GitHub Actions pinning:** Pin Actions to specific commits for enhanced security requirements
- **Recursive source tarballs:** Include submodule contents with `recursive-tarballs = true`
- **Windows-to-Windows cross-compilation:** Now supported with custom runners
- **Installer enhancements:** Multiple configuration improvements
- **GitHub bearer tokens:** Bring-your-own token support for installers
- **Per-platform binary overrides:** Override binaries on specific platforms
- **Package distancing:** New setting for overriding packages distributed
- **Version overriding:** Support for overriding package versions

## Version 0.28.2 (2025-07-22)

Minor release with unspecified improvements.

## Version 0.28.1 (2025-07-20)

Bugfix release.

## Version 0.28.0 (2025-01-08)

**Key Features:**
- **PowerShell improvements:** Enhanced PowerShell installer functionality

## Version 0.27.1 (2025-01-06)

**Key Features:**
- Fixed `dist migrate` bug that would delete `dist-workspace.toml`
- **PATH shadowing detection:** Warns if a tool is shadowed on your PATH
- **Release preference:** Prefer GitHub over Axo Releases

## Version 0.27.0 (2024-12-19)

**Key Features:**
- **XDG_CONFIG_HOME support:** Improved configuration directory handling
- **Build dependency handling:** Improved error messages for missing dependencies
- **Generic config improvements:** Enhanced handling for non-Rust projects
- **Standalone updater stabilization:** axoupdater package officially stabilized

## Version 0.26.1 (2024-12-12)

Minor release.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of distroless containers, focusing on Rust support, Chainguard, Google's gcr.io/distroless, and Wolfi OS:

## Overview of Distroless Containers

Distroless container images are designed to include only essential software required to run an application, removing shells, package managers, utilities, and other components that increase attack surface compared to traditional images based on Debian or Ubuntu.

## Security and Size Benefits

The major advantage of distroless images is reduced size and complexity, which results in a vastly reduced attack surface, evidenced by security scanners detecting far fewer potential vulnerabilities in Chainguard containers. Real-world results show switching from Ubuntu to distroless/static routinely reduces image size from 800 MB to 15–30 MB.

## Rust Compatibility

For languages that can compile completely static binaries such as C and Rust, the static base image can be used as a runtime. For Rust applications or other compiled languages with less perfect support of static builds than Go, gcr.io/distroless/base and gcr.io/distroless/base-nossl images are available.

## Chainguard and Wolfi OS

Chainguard Containers are based on Wolfi, a Linux undistro designed specifically to address software supply chain security issues that doesn't contain certain software normally found in traditional Linux distributions, making it a minimal distribution designed for stripped-down container images.

Chainguard introduced multi-layer container images in May 2025 for better stability and efficiency, achieving approximately a 70% reduction in total unique layer data size across their image catalog compared to single-layer approaches.

## Comparison: Google Distroless vs. Chainguard

Google distroless and Chainguard images follow similar philosophies of being minimal without package managers or shells, but the main difference is in implementation—Google distroless images are built with Bazel and based on Debian, whereas Chainguard Containers are built with apko and based on Wolfi.

The main difference is that Chainguard images are much easier to extend, as building a new image with apko is trivial compared to trying to wrangle the Bazel config to produce a modified Google distroless container.

## Image Sizes (2025)

Google distroless static-debian12 is less than 800KB and contains ca-certificate files, base-files, tzdata and other minimal packages, while gcr.io/distroless/base-debian12 is 8.5MB and contains glibc and libssl in addition to static-debian12.

In recent comparisons, Chainguard images showed 19.9MB disk usage, static distroless 19.5MB, while scratch was 12.6MB—all very light and small.

## Security Features

Chainguard images are signed and come with SBOMs for auditing, and are automatically rebuilt and updated when upstream packages change, keeping things fresh and secure.

I've completed the web search for information about CRIU checkpoint/restore processes, Linux process migration, agent mobility, and recent developments in 2025. Here are the key findings:

## Overview

CRIU (Checkpoint/Restore In Userspace) is a Linux software that can freeze a running container or individual application and checkpoint its state to disk. Using this functionality, application or container live migration, snapshots, remote debugging, and many other things are now possible.

## Process Migration and Mobility

CRIU performs process migration by capturing the entire state of a process including memory, CPU registers, file descriptors, and network connections, saving the state to disk in a series of files, transferring these files to the target machine or cluster node, and restoring the process on the target machine. Docker and Podman can use CRIU to checkpoint a running container's processes, save the image to disk, and restore it on the same or another host, enabling use cases like migrating stateful microservices to a new machine for load balancing or maintenance.

## Recent 2025 Developments

With CRIUgpu and similar advancements, we are getting closer to live-process snapshots of training jobs, which would allow truly stateful migration of training in progress. Additionally, in Kubernetes environments, checkpoint and restore improves workload mobility, reduces downtime during node maintenance, and accelerates container startup by bypassing initialization code.

## Integration and Adoption

CRIU is currently used by OpenVZ, LXC/LXD/Incus, Docker, Podman, Kubernetes and other software, and packaged for many Linux distributions.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about WebAssembly SIMD128 browser support and performance in 2025:

## Browser Support

Fixed-width 128-bit SIMD operations are now widely supported across all major browsers, including Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge, Opera, and Samsung Internet, as of late 2024 and early 2025. Safari's integration in 2024 was a particularly welcome addition, rounding out cross-browser support.

## Performance Metrics

The performance improvements are significant. Benchmarks from late 2025 show that WASM with SIMD can achieve 10-15x speedups over pure JavaScript for these kinds of workloads. For example, array operations that took 1.4ms in JavaScript could drop to 0.231ms with SIMD, a 6x improvement within WASM itself.

## Technical Details

128-bit fixed-width SIMD was chosen as a starting point because it was seen as having the most hardware support. SIMD allows a single instruction to operate on multiple data points simultaneously, leading to massive performance gains for highly parallelizable tasks.

## 128-bit Integer Operations

For bitwise operations, there's ongoing development. For Wasm code to perform 128-bit integer operations, it needs to use 64-bit instructions, or external library calls, but this is currently 2 to 7 times slower than native performance. This proposal's aim is to add new instructions to improve the performance of 128-bit arithmetic operations. During 2025, this proposal moved to phase 3 and is now being implemented in runtimes.

# dist Workspace Configuration Guide

Based on the provided documentation, here are the key configuration options for managing complex workspaces with dist:

## Package-Level Settings

The guide mentions two configuration approaches for controlling package behavior:

1. **`publish = false`** in a Package's Cargo.toml - prevents dist from processing a package
2. **`dist = false`** in `[package.metadata.dist]` - explicitly excludes packages from dist operations

## Announcement Tag Formats

dist supports two distinct tagging strategies for multi-package workspaces:

**Unified Announcement Format:**
- Uses version-only tags (v1.0.0, 0.1.0-prerelease.1)
- "selects all packages with the given version"
- Best for single-app releases or synchronized versioning

**Singular Announcement Format:**
- Uses PACKAGE-VERSION or PACKAGE/VERSION patterns (my-app-v1.0.0, my-app/1.0.0)
- Targets individual packages within workspaces
- Enables independent versioning workflows

## Workspace-Level Behavior

The guide establishes that "each Package that defines binaries is considered an 'App' with completely independent zips/installers," while library-only packages are ignored unless explicitly tagged.

## Key Limitation

"The need for a coherent Announcement Tag is so important that dist commands like 'build' and 'manifest' will error out if one isn't provided" - this strictness prevents misconfiguration before CI execution.

For detailed workspace implementation, the documentation references dedicated guides on cargo-release integration.

I've completed the web search for information about Chainguard Rust container images with Dockerfile multi-stage builds, SBOM, and SLSA attestation. Here are the key findings:

## Chainguard Rust Container Images Overview

Chainguard Containers are SLSA Level 3 compliant with detailed metadata and documentation about how it was built, and generate build provenance and a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for each release.

## SBOM and Attestations

Chainguard provides a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) with every container image, enabling complete transparency about package contents and dependencies for security and compliance requirements. The image's SBOM can be downloaded in either the SPDX or CycloneDX SBOM formats.

Available SLSA attestation predicate types include SLSA: https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1 (slsaprovenance1), which contains information about the image build environment and is available on all images.

## Multi-Stage Build Example with Rust

To build a Rust static binary with Chainguard's static Image, you can create a Dockerfile that uses FROM cgr.dev/chainguard/rust as build, then compile a Rust static binary and copy it into the static Image in a second stage.

## Development Variants

Chainguard recommends using multi-stage builds to copy artifacts from the -dev variant into a more minimal production image.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the **vaultrs** Rust crate for HashiCorp Vault:

## Overview

vaultrs is an asynchronous Rust client library for the HashiCorp Vault and OpenBao API. This crate encompasses functions for interacting with the HTTP API available on Hashicorp Vault servers.

## Key Features

The crate supports Auth (AppRole, AWS, JWT/OIDC, Kubernetes, Token, Certificate, Userpass), Secrets (AWS, Databases, KV v1, KV v2, PKI, SSH, Transit), and Sys (Health, Policies, Sealing, Wrapping).

## Basic Usage Example

To create a client: `use vaultrs::client::{VaultClient, VaultClientSettingsBuilder}; let client = VaultClient::new( VaultClientSettingsBuilder::default() .address("https://127.0.0.1:8200") .token("TOKEN") .build() .unwrap() ).unwrap();`

## Additional Examples

The crate includes examples for various secret engines:

- **KV2 Secrets**: Create and read secrets using the kv2 module with serializable data structures
- **AWS Secrets**: Mount AWS secret engine, configure it with access keys and region, create roles with ARNs, and generate credentials
- **PKI**: Generate certificates using the PKI backend with the cert module
- **Transit**: Create encryption keys with the /transit backend, supporting various key types and auto-rotation periods

## TLS Support

The crate offers two TLS implementations: rustls (default) or native-tls for platform-specific TLS.

The GitHub repository (jmgilman/vaultrs) and Rust documentation (docs.rs/vaultrs) contain comprehensive examples and API documentation for more detailed usage.

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Vault Agent sidecar pattern in Kubernetes with template rendering and secrets injection:

## Overview

The Vault Agent Injector leverages the Sidecar container pattern and Kubernetes mutating admission webhook to intercept pods that define specific annotations and inject a Vault Agent container to manage these secrets.

## How It Works

The Vault Sidecar Agent Injector leverages the sidecar pattern to alter pod specifications to include a Vault Agent container that renders Vault secrets to a shared memory volume. By rendering secrets to a shared volume, containers within the pod can consume Vault secrets without being Vault-aware.

The injector is a Kubernetes mutating webhook controller. The controller intercepts pod events and applies mutations to the pod if annotations exist within the request.

## Container Architecture

The pattern uses an init container to fetch secrets before an application starts, and a sidecar container that starts alongside your application for keeping secrets fresh (sidecar periodically checks to ensure secrets are current).

## Secret Configuration Methods

There are two methods of configuring the Vault Agent containers to render secrets: the vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-secret annotation, or a configuration map containing Vault Agent configuration files. Only one of these methods may be used at any time.

## Template Rendering

The pattern supports flexible output formatting options using the Vault Agent template functionality which was incorporated from consul-template. For example, fetching secret data from Vault to creating a database connection string, or adapting your output to match pre-existing configuration file formats.

## Installation

This functionality is provided by the vault-k8s project and can be automatically installed and configured using the Vault Helm chart.

Based on the search results, here's information about cargo-dist install receipts, axoupdater, and self-update configuration:

## Install Receipts

Install receipts are produced by cargo-dist since version 0.10.0 or later, and are JSON files containing metadata about the currently-installed version of an app and the version of cargo-dist that produced it. They can be found in ~/.config/APP_NAME (Linux, Mac) or %LOCALAPPDATA%\APP_NAME (Windows).

## XDG_CONFIG_HOME Support

Installers created with dist 0.27.0 or later will respect the XDG_CONFIG_HOME environment variable and place install receipts in that path; updaters using axoupdater 0.9.0 or later will respect that variable when searching for the receipt.

## Self-Update Features

A new cargo dist selfupdate command has been added which updates cargo dist to the latest version and runs cargo dist init using that new version. This is based on the new experimental axoupdater library.

## Axoupdater Configuration

Beginning with a recent release, dist will always package the same version of axoupdater when building and shipping your app. If you prefer the old behaviour and would like to receive whatever's the latest, you can set always-use-latest-updater = true in your configuration.

## GitHub Token Configuration

Axoupdater provides a way to supply a GitHub API token in order to opt into a higher rate limit. We recommend using an environment variable for token configuration so that you don't have to adjust how you call your app at the commandline in tests.

I've completed the web search for "cargo-deny license SPDX Rust 2025 configuration deny.toml advisories". Here's a summary of what I found:

## Key Information About cargo-deny

**Purpose**: cargo-deny is a cargo plugin that lets you lint your project's dependency graph to ensure all your dependencies conform to your expectations and requirements.

**Main Capabilities**: cargo-deny can check licenses, configure whether particular crates are allowed in your dependency graph, and configure how security vulnerabilities and unmaintained crates are handled.

## License Configuration

To use cargo-deny, you install it with `cargo install --locked cargo-deny`, navigate to your Rust project, and run `cargo deny init` to create a deny.toml configuration file.

The license must be a valid SPDX v2.1 identifier, which must either be in version 3.7 of the SPDX License List, with an optional exception specified by WITH <exception-id>, or else a user defined license reference denoted by LicenseRef-<idstring>.

## Advisories Configuration

The advisories check is used to detect issues for crates by looking in an advisory database. When a crate with a security vulnerability is encountered, it can be configured to deny (emit an error and fail the check), warn (print a warning but don't fail), or allow (print a note but don't fail).

The search results include practical examples from real projects showing how to configure the `[advisories]` section in deny.toml files, including settings for handling yanked crates and ignoring specific vulnerability IDs.

## Key Resources and Information

### Official Documentation and Getting Started

OpenTelemetry provides guidance on instrumenting Rust applications to emit traces to the console. A basic example uses a hyper application, and OpenTelemetry Rust works with other HTTP implementations such as Actix Web and Tide.

### Integration with Tokio and Tracing

Tokio is regarded as the primary async runtime and powers much of Rust's networking ecosystem, while the telemetry layer is implemented via tracing—another project within the Tokio ecosystem.

Tokio integrates seamlessly with the tracing crate to provide structured logging and observability in asynchronous applications, which is essential for modern Rust applications requiring detailed insights into execution flow, performance, and error handling.

### Collecting All Three Signals

You can bridge OpenTelemetry with the tracing ecosystem to seamlessly export traces, logs, and metrics. A complete observability pipeline can set up logs, metrics, and traces being exported to an OpenTelemetry collector at http://localhost:4317.

### Configuration and Layers

Configuration involves registering layers as subscribers including the OpenTelemetryTracingBridge layer for logging and the OpenTelemetry Span layer created via the tracing_opentelemetry crate to translate spans into OpenTelemetry equivalents.

### Current Status (2025-2026)

As of 2026, the OpenTelemetry Rust implementation is actively maintained, with Beta support for traces and stable support for logs and metrics. The latest stable version of the tracing crate in 2026 is 0.27, supporting advanced features like dynamic inspection of structured values.

### Recommended Exporters

The opentelemetry-otlp exporter sends telemetry in OTLP format to endpoints accepting OTLP, which could be the OTel Collector, telemetry backends like Jaeger, Prometheus or vendor specific endpoints.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about HashiCorp Vault Transit Secrets Engine:

## Overview

The transit secrets engine handles cryptographic functions on data in-transit and can be viewed as "cryptography as a service" or "encryption as a service". Vault doesn't store the data sent to the secrets engine.

## Key Capabilities

The transit secrets engine can also sign and verify data; generate hashes and HMACs of data; and act as a source of random bytes. Key derivation is supported, which allows the same key to be used for multiple purposes by deriving a new key based on a user-supplied context value, and convergent encryption can optionally be supported, which allows the same input values to produce the same ciphertext.

## API Examples

**Encryption:**
$ vault write transit/encrypt/my-key plaintext=$(echo "my secret data" | base64)

**Decryption:**
$ vault write -field=plaintext transit/decrypt/my-key ciphertext=... | base64 --decode

**Key Rotation:**
$ vault write -f transit/keys/my-key/rotate

**Rewrap (upgrade encrypted data to new key):**
$ vault write transit/rewrap/my-key ciphertext=vault:v1:8SDd3WHDOjf7mq69CyCqYjBXAiQQAVZRkFM13ok481zoCmHnSeDX9vyf7w==

**HTTP API (cURL):**
Enable the transit secrets engine: $ curl --header "X-Vault-Token: $VAULT_TOKEN" \ --request POST \ --data '{"type":"transit"}' \ $VAULT_ADDR/v1/sys/mounts/transit

Create an encryption key: $ curl --header "X-Vault-Token: $VAULT_TOKEN" \ --request POST \ $VAULT_ADDR/v1/transit/keys/orders

## Ciphertext Format

The v1 indicates the key version 1 was used to encrypt the plaintext; therefore, when you rotate keys, Vault knows which version to use for decryption. The rest is a base64 concatenation of the initialization vector (IV) and ciphertext.

## Overview

Argo Rollouts is a Kubernetes controller and set of CRDs which provide advanced deployment capabilities such as blue-green, canary, canary analysis, experimentation, and progressive delivery features to Kubernetes.

## Canary Deployment Strategy

A canary rollout is a deployment strategy where the operator releases a new version of their application to a small percentage of the production traffic. Argo Rollouts (optionally) integrates with ingress controllers and service meshes, leveraging their traffic shaping abilities to gradually shift traffic to the new version during an update.

## Metrics-Based Automated Rollback

Rollouts can query and interpret metrics from various providers to verify key KPIs and drive automated promotion or rollback during an update. Automated rollbacks can be triggered in the event of failures using metrics generated by external systems (such as request latency or failure data sourced from a Prometheus instance).

## Analysis Templates

An "AnalysisRun" object will eventually run an analysis of your choice to decide if your upgrade is successful to rollforward or unsuccessful to rollback. For analysis, you can use metrics scraped from your canary services with the help of different monitoring providers like Prometheus/Datadog/NewRelic, etc.

## Recent 2025 Examples

When a rollout fails based on metrics analysis, the application will be rolled out to the previous stable version. Recent 2025 documentation shows practical implementations combining Argo Rollouts with tools like Istio and Prometheus for automated canary deployments.

I've performed the web search for information about cargo-vet supply chain security for Rust, audit criteria, and exemptions. Here are the key findings:

## Overview

The cargo vet subcommand is a tool to help projects ensure that third-party Rust dependencies have been audited by a trusted entity.

## Audit Criteria

Google's rust crate audits define a set of 7 audit criteria that form an implication chain: ub-risk-0 through ub-risk-4, along with ub-risk-1-thorough and ub-risk-2-thorough indicating that two unsafe Rust experts performed the audit. More generally, audit criteria include safe-to-deploy and safe-to-run, how to perform and record audits, how to conduct relative audits between similar versions to reduce review effort, how to import audits from other organizations, and how trusted publishers work.

Most projects that want to use this criteria set should specify ub-risk-2 as the policy criteria and specify per-crate policy exceptions for ub-risk-3 crates.

## Exemptions

The exemptions table enumerates the set of crates which are being used despite missing the required audits and has a similar structure to the audits table in audits.toml, but each entry has fewer supported fields. Exemptions specify the exact version which should be exempted and the criteria covered by the exemption.

Dependencies can be added to a list of exceptions which can be ratcheted down over time, making it trivial to introduce cargo vet to a new project and guard against future vulnerabilities while vetting the pre-existing code gradually as time permits.

Based on the web search results, here's information about WASI P2 (Preview 2) WIT interfaces:

## WASI P2 Overview

APIs designed for WASI 0.2 and the Component Model are defined with the WebAssembly Interface Type (WIT) Interface Description Language (IDL). The following interfaces are included in WASI P2, though the search results reference specific interface categories rather than providing an exhaustive list.

## Core Interfaces

The search results confirm the presence of the interfaces you mentioned:

- **wasi:clocks** - including wasi-clocks-0.2.0, which contains interfaces like monotonic-clock and wall-clock
- **wasi:filesystem** - interfaces for Filesystem access defined with WIT
- **wasi:random** - including wasi-random-0.2.0

## Additional Core Interfaces

Beyond those three, WASI P2 includes:

- **wasi:io** - includes error, poll, and streams interfaces
- **wasi:cli** - includes environment, exit, stdin, stdout, stderr, and terminal interfaces
- **wasi:sockets** - includes UDP and TCP interfaces
- **wasi:http** - wasi:http worlds were included in WASI 0.2

For the most complete and current list of WASI P2 interfaces, you can explore the types and definitions for a given WASI 0.2 API in its WIT files at the official WASI.dev website.

Based on the search results, here's what I found regarding Fermyon Spin 3.0 and the related technologies:

## Spin 3.0 Overview

Spin 3.0 officially supports both the WASI Key-Value and WASI Config APIs, marking an important step toward bringing WASI cloud core into Spin. Spin 3.0 now officially supports OpenTelemetry (OTel) observability in Spin applications.

## Component Model and WASI P2

The search results show that with Spin 2.0, Fermyon introduced production support for the Component Model, which enables users to construct robust applications from smaller, isolated components that can communicate in a language-agnostic way. In the Spin 2.2 release, they moved to the stable version of WASI 0.2.0 APIs in Wasmtime 17, while continuing to support applications written against the previous snapshots for backward compatibility.

## Timer/Cron Trigger

The cron trigger allows applications to execute functions every few seconds without needing any incoming requests or intervention from users or other machines. A cron trigger on a component runs every day at midnight to gather metrics.

## WASI Preview 3 (Looking Forward)

For future developments, support for the WASI Preview 3 release candidate has landed in Spin v3.5.0, allowing folks to take advantage of first class composable concurrency and other advancements to the Component Model.

I found several relevant results about Trivy and Grype CVE scanning comparisons with distroless and Chainguard containers. Here are the key findings:

## Comparison Summary

Grype produces fewer false negatives than Trivy, though Grype can still yield false negatives occasionally and will produce more false positives on average.

Grype's detection of the primary component is often better than Trivy's, thanks to Grype's binary cataloger, which covers the primary components of those images picked for testing.

## Performance Differences

Trivy generally performs faster in direct scanning scenarios, especially for larger images, while Grype's incremental database updates are significantly smaller than Trivy's, making it more efficient for environments with limited bandwidth.

## Distroless and Chainguard Context

There is a significant chance that official images contain too many components, which increases true and false positive CVE findings from scanners such as Trivy or Grype.

Vulnerability scanners like Trivy or Grype will, on average, report much fewer vulnerabilities compared to non-minimal images when scanning minimal/distroless images.

Chainguard provides hardened container images designed to have zero or near-zero known CVEs, built on Wolfi, a Linux undistro purpose-built for containers.

Chainguard Libraries integrates with Grype, Trivy, AWS Inspector, and Anchore Enterprise, allowing these scanners to identify remediated vulnerabilities.

# dist Configuration Guide

Based on the provided documentation, here's what I can extract about the dist configuration:

## Basic Configuration Structure

The configuration is stored in `Cargo.toml` under `[workspace.metadata.dist]`. The guide shows these default settings:

```
[workspace.metadata.dist]
cargo-dist-version = "0.0.6"
ci = ["github"]
installers = []
targets = ["x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu", "x86_64-apple-darwin", 
           "x86_64-pc-windows-msvc", "aarch64-apple-darwin"]
```

## Configuration Keys Documented

| Key | Type | Purpose |
|-----|------|---------|
| `cargo-dist-version` | String (SemVer) | "records its own version in your config for the sake of reproducibility" |
| `ci` | Array | Specifies CI backends to support (currently only "github") |
| `installers` | Array | Defines which installers to generate |
| `targets` | Array | Lists platform targets using Rust target-triple syntax |

## Additional Configuration

The documentation references a separate `[profile.dist]` section for build settings:

```
[profile.dist]
inherits = "release"
lto = "thin"
```

## Overview

The LGTM stack is Grafana's answer to full-stack observability, where Loki handles logs, Grafana provides visualization, Tempo stores traces, and Mimir manages metrics. When you wire all four together with OpenTelemetry as the data collection layer, you get a production-grade observability platform that is entirely open source.

## Key Components

The OpenTelemetry Collector serves as the central hub for ingesting telemetry (metrics, traces, and logs) from your applications. Prometheus scrapes and stores metrics. Loki collects and indexes logs. Tempo handles distributed traces. Grafana provides a unified dashboard to visualize and explore your telemetry data.

## Docker Compose Setup

If you set up the components via Docker Compose, Grafana automatically adds all data sources. The collector uses a configuration file (./config/otel-collector.yml) that defines receivers, processors, and exporters to route the data to downstream services.

## Advanced Features

The metrics_generator section in Tempo analyzes incoming traces and automatically generates rate, error, and duration (RED) metrics, which are pushed to Mimir, so you can alert on trace-derived data without writing any extra instrumentation.

## Ready-Made Docker Image

Grafana provides an OpenTelemetry backend in a Docker image that bundles the OpenTelemetry Collector, Prometheus (metrics), Tempo (traces), Loki (logs), Pyroscope (profiles), and Grafana into a single container.

# Vaultrs: Async Rust Client for Vault API

**vaultrs** is "an asynchronous Rust client library for the Hashicorp Vault and OpenBao API." It provides comprehensive support for authentication methods, secret engines, and system operations.

## Key Features

The library supports multiple authentication mechanisms including AppRole, AWS, JWT/OIDC, Kubernetes, Token, Certificate, and Userpass. For secrets management, it covers AWS, Cubbyhole, Databases, KV v1/v2, PKI, SSH, and Transit engines. System operations include Health checks, Policies, Sealing, and Response Wrapping.

## Installation

Add to your `Cargo.toml` with Rustls (default):
```toml
[dependencies]
vaultrs = "0.8.0"
```

Or use native TLS:
```toml
vaultrs = { version = "0.8.0", default-features = false, features = ["native-tls"] }
```

## Basic Usage Example

Initialize the client:
```rust
use vaultrs::client::{VaultClient, VaultClientSettingsBuilder};

let client = VaultClient::new(
    VaultClientSettingsBuilder::default()
        .address("https://127.0.0.1:8200")
        .token("TOKEN")
        .build()
        .unwrap()
).unwrap();
```

The documentation notes that "all errors generated by this crate are wrapped in the ClientError enum" and all top-level operations include tracing instrumentation for debugging support.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Fly.io's deployment strategies and related capabilities:

## Deployment Strategies

Fly.io supports several deployment strategies: Canary (which starts with one Machine and if it's healthy, continues with rolling deployment), and Bluegreen (which boots new Machines alongside old ones, then only switches traffic after all new Machines pass health checks).

## Traffic Management

If a Machine fails a health check, Fly Proxy pulls it out of the rotation, and the Machine can still be running, logging, and debugging, but it's invisible to users. For bluegreen deployments specifically, Fly.io boots a new Machine alongside each running Machine in the same region, and migrates traffic to the new Machines only once all the new Machines pass health checks.

## Machine Checks

Deployments also include machine_checks, which aren't about routing traffic but about catching bad deploys before they go live—these boot a throwaway Machine and let you run integration tests, perform simulated traffic, check database access, or validate config.

## Machines API Considerations

The search results indicate that users attempting to implement blue-green deployments directly on the Machines API (for running user code on Fly.io) face challenges with waiting for health checks to pass, though the official deployment strategies handle this automatically.

The documentation covers deployment features up through 2024-2025, but does not show explicit "traffic splitting" functionality as a separate feature—instead, Fly.io implements traffic management through health checks and deployment strategies.

# Integrating axoupdater into Rust Binaries

## Core API

axoupdater provides two main approaches for self-updates:

**Checking for updates:**
```rust
if AxoUpdater::new_for("appname").load_receipt()?.is_update_needed_sync()? {
    eprintln!("Update available!");
}
```

**Performing updates:**
```rust
if AxoUpdater::new_for("appname").load_receipt()?.run_sync()? {
    eprintln!("Update installed!");
}
```

The library offers both synchronous (`_sync` variants) and asynchronous versions. Enable the `"blocking"` feature in `Cargo.toml` for blocking methods.

## Configuration Options

**GitHub API Token for CI Environments:**

The documentation recommends "using an environment variable for token configuration so that you don't have to adjust how you call your app at the commandline in tests."

Implementation pattern:
```rust
if let Ok(token) = std::env::var("YOUR_APP_GITHUB_TOKEN") {
    updater.set_github_token(&token);
}
```

In CI, assign the token via GitHub Actions:
```yaml
env:
  YOUR_APP_GITHUB_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
```

## Install Receipt Format

axoupdater uses JSON install receipts created by cargo-dist (v0.10.0+). These files contain "metadata about the currently-installed version of an app and the version of cargo-dist that produced it." Location: `~/.config/APP_NAME` (Linux/Mac) or `%LOCALAPPDATA%\APP_NAME` (Windows).

## Release Backend Support

The library supports GitHub Releases and Axo Releases. To reduce dependencies, disable unused backends via crate features.

## .sops.yaml Configuration File

You can create a .sops.yaml configuration file at the root directory to define which keys are used for which filename.

## Age Key Integration

Encrypting with SSH keys via age is supported by SOPS. You can use SSH public keys ("ssh-ed25519 AAAA...", "ssh-rsa AAAA...") as age recipients when encrypting a file.

## Creation Rules Example

A list of age recipients can be added to the .sops.yaml with creation_rules that specify age keys for encryption.

Here's a practical example from the search results:

A creation_rules configuration can include: `creation_rules: - shamir_threshold: 1 path_regex: "secret.json" encrypted_regex: "^(user|password)$" key_groups: - age: - age1t2c8jft25k5nnr7m2zln473dkxegwvx5ge2pfgarfnaepepmzpzszz63qy`

## Encryption and Decryption

To encrypt a file, you can run: `sops --encrypt --age $(cat $SOPS_AGE_KEY_FILE | grep -oP "public key: \K(.*)")` with the --encrypted-regex option to specify which fields should be encrypted.

To decrypt the encrypted fields in the file, you can use a similar command with the --decrypt flag, allowing you to view and modify the secrets as needed.

## Supported Formats

SOPS is an editor of encrypted files that supports YAML, JSON, ENV, INI and BINARY formats and encrypts with AWS KMS, GCP KMS, Azure Key Vault, HuaweiCloud KMS, age, and PGP.

I've performed a web search for research papers on mobile agent process migration, serialization, and state transfer from 2020-2025. Here are the key findings:

## Overview of Results

The search returned papers on mobile agent migration spanning several decades, though most results are from earlier periods. Here's what was found:

### Key Research Areas

1. **Migration Process and State Transfer**: Agent migration procedure consists of code migration and agent state migration, with migration of agent's state being a process of serializing agent's state and de-serializing it at destination.

2. **Serialization Approaches**: Continuations can be used to save the execution state of mobile software agents, which are software entities able to suspend their execution and resume it in a different host. Additionally, Java provides a serialisation mechanism which allows the capture and restoration of objects' states and therefore the migration of objects between machines.

3. **Migration Strategies**: Several ways of migrating a mobile agent have been implemented based on programming language and available transmission techniques, dividing into pull and push strategies, where the first class uses dynamic download techniques to transmit only necessary code, while the second class always transmits all agent's code to the next platform.

4. **Performance Optimization**: Both TCP and UDP transport protocols are considered for MA transfers, with serialised MA state potentially compressed before transmission, and research examines how the serialised MA's state size affects the overall migration delay.

### Recent Survey Work

One notable recent paper is a survey of migration process of mobile agent research providing an up-to-date overview.

The search results indicate that while mobile agent migration research continues, most detailed papers in the results are from earlier periods (pre-2020), with limited recent publications from the 2020-2025 timeframe being prominently indexed.

content.content:
# Cargo Vet Configuration Schema

## audits.toml

**Purpose:** Contains audits performed by project members and audit criteria definitions.

**`criteria` Table:**
- `description` (required or `description-url`): Concise criteria explanation
- `description-url`: Public URL hosting criteria text
- `implies`: String or array of other criteria subsumed by this entry

**`audits` Table:**
Indexed by crate name using table arrays (`[[audits.foo]]`). "The semantics of the various audit entries keys are described [here]" in the audit-entries reference.

**`trusted` Table:**
Publisher entries indexed by crate name using table arrays (`[[trusted.foo]]`). Details available in trusted-entries documentation.

## config.toml

**Purpose:** Project-specific configuration; cannot be imported by other projects.

**Top-level keys:**
- `default-criteria`: Default criteria for `cargo vet certify` (defaults to `safe-to-deploy`)

**`cargo-vet` Table:**
Metadata tracking cargo-vet version used to create the store.

**`imports` Table:**
External audit sets with user-defined nicknames:
- `url` (required): HTTPS URL to remote `audits.toml`
- `criteria-map`: Maps imported criteria to local criteria
- `exclude`: List of crates to exclude from import

**`policy` Table:**
Configures audit requirements. Keys can be crate names or `"CRATE:VERSION"` format:
- `criteria`: Required criteria for first-party crates (defaults to `safe-to-deploy` for top-level)
- `dev-criteria`: Criteria for dev-dependencies (defaults to `safe-to-run`)
- `dependency-criteria`: Per-dependency overrides
- `audit-as-crates-io`: Treat first-party packages as crates.io
- `notes`: Rationale documentation

**`exemptions` Table:**
Crates used without required audits:
- `version`: Exact version to exempt
- `criteria`: Covered criteria
- `notes`: Rationale
- `suggest`: Boolean for `cargo vet suggest` eligibility (defaults to true)

## imports.lock

Auto-generated file; format treated as implementation detail.

## Default Location

"By default, `cargo vet` data lives in a `supply-chain` directory next to `Cargo.lock`" with configurable path via `[package.metadata.vet]` or `[workspace.metadata.vet]`.
