## Research Report: WASM Ecosystem (2025-2026)

---

### 1. WASI Preview 2 (P2) / Component Model

#### Current State

WASI Preview 2 was stabilized as **WASI 0.2** in late 2024 and is now the active production standard as of 2025-2026. The Bytecode Alliance moved away from the "Preview" naming — "Preview 2" is now called WASI 0.2, and the next release is WASI 0.3 (formerly "Preview 3"). WASI 1.0 will be the formal ISO/ECMA standardization milestone.

**WASI 0.3** (targeted for early 2026) adds first-class async and thread support. WASM components targeting WASI 0.2 and 0.3 use the same binary representation; 0.3 is additive.

The Component Model standardizes how WebAssembly modules compose into larger applications using WIT (WebAssembly Interface Types) as the IDL. Components are language-agnostic, type-safe, and share-nothing — they communicate only through explicitly declared interfaces.

#### WASI 0.2 Interface Catalog

All six interface groups shipped in WASI 0.2:

| Interface | Package | What it provides |
|---|---|---|
| `wasi:io` | `wasi:io/poll`, `wasi:io/streams`, `wasi:io/error` | Pollable handles, byte streams, error resources |
| `wasi:clocks` | `wasi:clocks/monotonic-clock`, `wasi:clocks/wall-clock` | Timers and wall time |
| `wasi:filesystem` | `wasi:filesystem/types`, `wasi:filesystem/preopens` | File/directory access |
| `wasi:sockets` | `wasi:sockets/tcp`, `wasi:sockets/udp`, `wasi:sockets/ip-name-lookup` | Network I/O |
| `wasi:cli` | `wasi:cli/environment`, `wasi:cli/stdin`, `wasi:cli/stdout`, `wasi:cli/stderr`, `wasi:cli/exit` | Command-line environments |
| `wasi:http` | `wasi:http/types`, `wasi:http/incoming-handler`, `wasi:http/outgoing-handler` | HTTP request/response |
| `wasi:random` | `wasi:random/random`, `wasi:random/insecure`, `wasi:random/insecure-seed` | Randomness |

Reference: [WASI.dev interfaces](https://wasi.dev/interfaces)

#### WIT Example (component world)

```wit
package my:service;

world handler {
    import wasi:http/outgoing-handler@0.2.0;
    import wasi:clocks/monotonic-clock@0.2.0;
    export wasi:http/incoming-handler@0.2.0;
}
```

#### Rust Compilation to WASI P2 Components

**Two paths exist:**

**Path A — Native target (Rust 1.82+, simple WASI-only components):**
```bash
rustup target add wasm32-wasip2
cargo build --target wasm32-wasip2 --release
```
The `wasm32-wasip2` target reached Tier 2 in Rust 1.82 (October 2024). The compiler directly emits a component binary. Limitation: stdlib WASI 0.2 API wrappers (`std::net`, `std::fs` over WASI sockets/filesystem) are still being wired — use the `wasi` crate for direct bindings.

**Path B — cargo-component (custom WIT interfaces, any complexity):**
```bash
cargo install --locked cargo-component   # current: v0.21.1, April 2025
cargo component new --lib my-component
cargo component build --release
```
Internally uses `wasm32-wasip1` + Wasmtime WASI adapter to produce a P2 component. Required when you have non-WASI WIT interfaces (third-party or custom).

**Cargo.toml for cargo-component:**
```toml
[package]
name = "my-component"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
wit-bindgen = "0.36"

[package.metadata.component]
package = "my:component"
```

**wit/ directory structure:**
```
wit/
  world.wit      # declares world, imports, exports
```

**Generated binding usage:**
```rust
// src/lib.rs
mod bindings;
use bindings::exports::my::component::MyInterface;

struct Component;
impl MyInterface for Component {
    fn run() -> String {
        "hello from component".into()
    }
}
bindings::export!(Component with_types_in bindings);
```

#### wasm-tools

`wasm-tools` is the low-level toolkit for inspecting and transforming WebAssembly:
```bash
cargo install --locked wasm-tools@1.235.0
wasm-tools component new core.wasm -o component.wasm  # wrap core wasm
wasm-tools component wit component.wasm               # inspect WIT
wasm-tools validate component.wasm                    # validate
```

#### Compatibility Matrix

The Bytecode Alliance maintains a [tooling compatibility matrix](https://bytecodealliance.org/articles/component-model-tooling-compatibility) for aligning `cargo-component`, `wit-bindgen`, `wasm-tools`, and `wasmtime` versions.

Sources:
- [WASI P2 vs WASIX 2026](https://wasmruntime.com/en/blog/wasi-preview2-vs-wasix-2026)
- [WASI and Component Model: Current Status (eunomia)](https://eunomia.dev/blog/2025/02/16/wasi-and-the-webassembly-component-model-current-status/)
- [Rust Blog: wasm32-wasip2 Tier 2](https://blog.rust-lang.org/2024/11/26/wasip2-tier-2/)
- [cargo-component GitHub](https://github.com/bytecodealliance/cargo-component)

---

### 2. Fermyon Spin

#### Current State

Spin 3.0 shipped in late 2024, and Spin 3.5 shipped in 2025. Spin is now under the `spinframework` GitHub org (moved from Fermyon). The runtime is built on Wasmtime and fully targets WASI 0.2. Spin 3.5 has experimental WASIp3 support via feature flag.

#### Spin SDK for Rust

**Current version:** `spin-sdk 5.x` (5.0.0 on crates.io, 5.1.1 for WASIp3 unstable)

**Cargo.toml:**
```toml
[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
spin-sdk = "5"
anyhow = "1"

[build-dependencies]
# none needed for simple components
```

For WASIp3 experimental:
```toml
[dependencies]
spin-sdk = { version = "5.1.1", features = ["wasip3-unstable"] }
```

#### HTTP Trigger Component

```rust
use spin_sdk::http::{IntoResponse, Request, Response};
use spin_sdk::http_component;

#[http_component]
async fn handle_request(req: Request) -> anyhow::Result<impl IntoResponse> {
    println!("Handling request to {:?}", req.header("spin-full-url"));
    Ok(Response::builder()
        .status(200)
        .header("content-type", "text/plain")
        .body("Hello from Spin/Rust")
        .build())
}
```

#### spin.toml Configuration

```toml
spin_manifest_version = 2

[application]
name = "my-spin-app"
version = "1.0.0"
authors = ["Will <will@example.com>"]
description = "Multi-trigger Spin app"

# HTTP trigger
[[trigger.http]]
route = "/api/..."
component = "api-handler"

[component.api-handler]
source = "target/wasm32-wasi/release/api_handler.wasm"
allowed_outbound_hosts = ["https://api.example.com"]

[component.api-handler.build]
command = "cargo build --target wasm32-wasi --release"
workdir = "."
watch = ["src/**/*.rs", "Cargo.toml"]

# Redis trigger (application-level address)
[application.trigger.redis]
address = "redis://localhost:6379"

[[trigger.redis]]
channel = "events"
component = "event-processor"

[component.event-processor]
source = "target/wasm32-wasi/release/event_processor.wasm"

[component.event-processor.build]
command = "cargo build --target wasm32-wasi --release"
```

#### Available Triggers

| Trigger | Status | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP | Stable | Primary trigger; async supported in 3.x |
| Redis | Stable | Pub/sub channel subscription |
| Cron | Available | `[[trigger.cron]]` with cron expression |
| MQTT | Plugin | Via community trigger plugins |

**Cron example:**
```toml
[[trigger.cron]]
cron_expression = "0 0 * * *"   # daily at midnight
component = "daily-job"
```

#### Spin 3.0 / 3.5 New Features

- **3.0**: WASI Key-Value API, WASI Config API, OpenTelemetry (OTel) observability, polyglot component composition (Rust lib used by JS component)
- **3.5**: WASIp3 RC support (`wasi@0.3.0-rc-2025-09-16`) — composable concurrency, concurrent outbound HTTP from within a handler
- **wasi-grpc**: introduced alongside Spin 3.x for gRPC over WASI streams

#### Build and Deploy

```bash
spin build                          # builds all components
spin up                             # local dev server
spin deploy --from .                # deploy to Fermyon Cloud (Wasm Functions)
```

Sources:
- [Spin v3.5 Announcement](https://spinframework.dev/blog/announcing-spin-3-5)
- [Building Spin components in Rust](https://spinframework.dev/v2/rust-components)
- [Spin Redis trigger](https://developer.fermyon.com/spin/v2/redis-trigger)
- [Spin cron trigger](https://www.fermyon.com/blog/spin-cron-trigger)

---

### 3. wasmCloud

#### Architecture Overview

wasmCloud is a CNCF project (Incubating) that runs WebAssembly components across a distributed mesh of hosts. The core abstraction is:

- **Component**: A WASI P2 WebAssembly component containing business logic
- **Provider**: A capability plugin (external process or built-in) that handles I/O, storage, networking
- **Host**: A wasmCloud runtime process; multiple hosts form a lattice
- **Lattice**: The self-forming distributed mesh over NATS

#### Capability Providers

Built-in providers (in-process, no NATS hop):
- `wasi:logging` — structured logging
- `wasi:random` — secure randomness
- `wasi:clocks` — wall clock and monotonic

External/first-party providers (separate processes on the lattice):
| Provider | OCI image | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP Server | `ghcr.io/wasmcloud/http-server:0.26.0` | `wasi:http/incoming-handler` |
| KV Redis | `ghcr.io/wasmcloud/keyvalue-redis:0.28.2` | `wasi:keyvalue/atomics,store` |
| Blobstore S3 | `ghcr.io/wasmcloud/blobstore-s3` | `wasi:blobstore` |
| Messaging Kafka | `ghcr.io/wasmcloud/messaging-kafka` | `wasmcloud:messaging` |

As of Q3 2025 roadmap, providers are transitioning to a **wRPC server** model where WIT interfaces are served over TCP/NATS/QUIC/UDP, allowing providers to be written in any language and deployed in containers independently.

#### Lattice Networking

The lattice is built on **NATS** (Cloud Native Computing Foundation). Components and providers communicate via NATS subjects. The lattice is:
- **Self-forming**: hosts auto-discover each other via NATS
- **Self-healing**: link failures cause re-routing
- **Flat topology**: no hierarchy — any component can reach any provider across cloud boundaries, edge, K8s

#### Rust Component Development

```bash
cargo install wash-cli              # wasmCloud shell
wash new component http-test --template-name hello-world-rust
cd http-test
wash build                          # produces build/http_hello_world_s.wasm
wash dev                            # local dev with hot reload
```

**Cargo.toml for a wasmCloud component:**
```toml
[package]
name = "http-hello-world"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
wit-bindgen = "0.36"
wasi = "0.2"

# Target: wasm32-wasip2
```

**rust-toolchain.toml:**
```toml
[toolchain]
channel = "stable"
targets = ["wasm32-wasip2"]
```

#### WADM Deployment (OAM format)

```yaml
apiVersion: core.oam.dev/v1beta1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: hello-world
  annotations:
    description: 'HTTP hello world'
spec:
  components:
    - name: http-component
      type: component
      properties:
        image: file://./build/http_hello_world_s.wasm
      traits:
        - type: spreadscaler
          properties:
            instances: 1
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: httpserver
            namespace: wasi
            package: http
            interfaces: [incoming-handler]

    - name: httpserver
      type: capability
      properties:
        image: ghcr.io/wasmcloud/http-server:0.26.0
      traits:
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: http-component
            namespace: wasi
            package: http
            interfaces: [incoming-handler]
            source_config:
              - name: default-http
                properties:
                  address: 0.0.0.0:8000
```

```bash
wash app deploy wadm.yaml
wash app status hello-world
```

Sources:
- [wasmCloud Providers](https://wasmcloud.com/docs/concepts/providers/)
- [Lattice concept](https://wasmcloud.com/docs/v1/concepts/lattice/)
- [wasmCloud Rust components](https://wasmcloud.com/docs/developer/languages/rust/components/)
- [WADM OAM model](https://wasmcloud.com/docs/ecosystem/wadm/model/)

---

### 4. Cloudflare Workers with Rust/WASM

#### How It Works

Cloudflare Workers runs Rust via WebAssembly using the **workers-rs** (`worker` crate). The glue layer is **wasm-bindgen** — it generates JS bindings that expose the Workers runtime APIs (fetch, KV, R2, D1, Queues, Durable Objects) to the WASM module. A build tool called `worker-build` wraps this into a Workers-deployable bundle.

Note: This is **not** WASI P2. Workers uses its own ABI via wasm-bindgen + JS interop, not the Component Model. Workers announced Component Model support via `workers-wasm-builder` but it is still evolving.

#### worker-rs Setup

**Current version:** `worker 0.7.4` (crates.io, 2025)

**Cargo.toml:**
```toml
[package]
name = "my-worker"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
worker = "0.7"

[profile.release]
opt-level = "s"    # size optimization for WASM
lto = true
```

**wrangler.toml:**
```toml
name = "my-rust-worker"
main = "build/worker/shim.mjs"
compatibility_date = "2025-04-01"

[build]
command = "worker-build --release"
```

**HTTP handler example:**
```rust
use worker::*;

#[event(fetch)]
async fn main(req: Request, env: Env, _ctx: Context) -> Result<Response> {
    Router::new()
        .get_async("/api/data", |_req, ctx| async move {
            let kv = ctx.kv("MY_KV")?;
            let val = kv.get("key").text().await?;
            Response::ok(val.unwrap_or_default())
        })
        .run(req, env)
        .await
}
```

**KV, R2, D1 bindings in wrangler.toml:**
```toml
[[kv_namespaces]]
binding = "MY_KV"
id = "abc123"

[[r2_buckets]]
binding = "MY_BUCKET"
bucket_name = "my-bucket"

[[d1_databases]]
binding = "MY_DB"
database_name = "my-database"
database_id = "def456"
```

#### Resource Limits

| Resource | Free Plan | Paid Plan |
|---|---|---|
| CPU time per request | 10 ms | 30 s default; up to **5 minutes** (configurable) |
| Memory per isolate | 128 MB | 128 MB |
| WASM module size | No explicit limit | ~10 MB practical |
| Cron trigger CPU | 10 ms | 30 s (< 1 hr interval); 15 min (>= 1 hr) |
| Subrequests per request | 50 | 1000 |

CPU time measures actual CPU execution, **not** wall time — I/O await does not count. Average Worker uses ~2.2 ms per request.

To configure max CPU time on paid plan:
```toml
# wrangler.toml
[limits]
cpu_ms = 30000    # 30 seconds; max 300000 (5 minutes)
```

#### Supported Crates

Cloudflare maintains a [supported crates list](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/languages/rust/crates/). Key constraint: crates that use system calls not available in WASM (threads, raw sockets, mmap) require `no_std` alternatives or will fail to compile. Recommended: `reqwest` via `fetch` API, `serde`/`serde_json`, `regex`, `uuid`.

Sources:
- [Cloudflare Workers Rust support](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/languages/rust/)
- [workers-rs GitHub](https://github.com/cloudflare/workers-rs)
- [Workers platform limits](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/platform/limits/)
- [Higher CPU limits changelog (March 2025)](https://developers.cloudflare.com/changelog/post/2025-03-25-higher-cpu-limits/)

---

### 5. WASM SIMD128

#### Browser Support (as of 2025)

WASM SIMD128 (fixed-width 128-bit SIMD, spec proposal finalized 2021) has **universal browser support** as of 2025:

| Browser | SIMD128 | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Chrome/V8 | Yes (since v91) | Full 236+ instruction set |
| Firefox | Yes (since v89) | Full support |
| Safari/WebKit | Yes (since 2024) | Completed cross-browser coverage |
| Edge | Yes | Follows V8 |
| Samsung Internet | Yes | |

**Relaxed SIMD** (allows non-deterministic lane ordering for extra perf) was standardized in 2023 and is now broadly supported, enabling backends to use AVX2/NEON-specific reorderings.

#### SIMD128 Instruction Set Relevant to HDC

The 128-bit `v128` type maps to:
- `v128.and`, `v128.or`, `v128.xor`, `v128.not` — bitwise boolean ops (directly useful for HDC bundling/binding)
- `i8x16.popcnt` — **popcount per lane** (added in SIMD proposal; critical for Hamming distance)
- `v128.bitselect` — conditional select
- `i32x4`, `i64x2`, `f32x4`, `f64x2` — typed lane operations

For HDC binary hypervectors, the critical path is:
```
hamming_distance(a, b) = popcount(xor(a, b))
```
This maps directly to `v128.xor` + `i8x16.popcnt` + horizontal sum. 16 bytes processed per instruction.

#### Performance Characteristics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| SIMD vs scalar JS | 6-15x speedup (benchmark dependent) |
| SIMD vs scalar WASM | 4-8x speedup for vectorizable workloads |
| WASM SIMD vs native AVX2 | ~4x slower (128-bit vs 256-bit registers) |
| WASM SIMD vs native NEON (ARM) | Closer; ~1.2-2x overhead |

**Key limitation:** WASM SIMD is limited to 128-bit registers. Native AVX2 uses 256-bit (AVX-512 uses 512-bit). This means WASM SIMD throughput is capped at ~50% of AVX2 native throughput for pure SIMD workloads.

**For HDC specifically (binary XOR + popcount):**
- 128-bit `v128.xor` + `i8x16.popcnt` = 16 bytes of binary hypervector per instruction
- A 10,000-bit hypervector requires ~625 SIMD instructions per distance computation
- Achievable throughput in WASM: millions of distance computations per second in browser
- Native AVX-512 has dedicated `vpopcntq` (512-bit popcount) — ~4x advantage over WASM

**128-bit integer arithmetic (WASM proposal, Phase 3 as of 2025):** There is a WASM proposal to add native 128-bit arithmetic instructions. Currently 128-bit integer ops require 64-bit pairs, which is 2-7x slower than native. This proposal is Phase 3 (implementations underway in Wasmtime/V8 in 2025).

#### Rust WASM SIMD Example

```rust
// Enable SIMD target features
// In Cargo.toml or RUSTFLAGS:
// RUSTFLAGS="-C target-feature=+simd128"

use std::arch::wasm32::*;

pub fn hamming_distance_simd(a: &[u8; 16], b: &[u8; 16]) -> u32 {
    unsafe {
        let va = v128_load(a.as_ptr() as *const v128);
        let vb = v128_load(b.as_ptr() as *const v128);
        let xored = v128_xor(va, vb);
        // i8x16_popcnt available in SIMD proposal extension
        // Sum lanes for total Hamming distance
        let counts = i8x16_popcnt(xored);
        // Horizontal add all 16 bytes
        let sum = i32x4_dot_i8x16_add_sat_u(counts, i8x16_splat(1), i32x4_splat(0));
        // Extract final sum...
        i32x4_extract_lane::<0>(sum) as u32
    }
}
```

**Build configuration for Rust WASM SIMD:**
```toml
# .cargo/config.toml
[target.wasm32-unknown-unknown]
rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+simd128"]

[target.wasm32-wasip2]
rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+simd128"]
```

Sources:
- [caniuse WASM SIMD](https://caniuse.com/wasm-simd)
- [Rust + WebAssembly 2025: WasmGC and SIMD](https://dev.to/dataformathub/rust-webassembly-2025-why-wasmgc-and-simd-change-everything-3ldh)
- [8 WASM SIMD patterns for data crunching](https://medium.com/@Nexumo_/8-webassembly-simd-patterns-for-data-crunching-8333c6c088b4)
- [Emscripten SIMD docs](https://emscripten.org/docs/porting/simd.html)

---

| Topic | Key Version | Rust Target | Key Tooling | Maturity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| WASI P2 / Component Model | 0.2.0 stable, 0.3 RC | `wasm32-wasip2` (Rust 1.82+) | `cargo-component 0.21.1`, `wasm-tools 1.235.0`, `wit-bindgen 0.36` | Production |

Here is a comprehensive research report on all five topics.

---

## Research Report: WASM Ecosystem (2025-2026)

---

### 1. WASI Preview 2 (P2) / Component Model

#### Current State

WASI Preview 2 was stabilized as **WASI 0.2** in late 2024 and is now the active production standard as of 2025-2026. The Bytecode Alliance moved away from the "Preview" naming — "Preview 2" is now called WASI 0.2, and the next release is WASI 0.3 (formerly "Preview 3"). WASI 1.0 will be the formal ISO/ECMA standardization milestone.

**WASI 0.3** (targeted for early 2026) adds first-class async and thread support. WASM components targeting WASI 0.2 and 0.3 use the same binary representation; 0.3 is additive.

The Component Model standardizes how WebAssembly modules compose into larger applications using WIT (WebAssembly Interface Types) as the IDL. Components are language-agnostic, type-safe, and share-nothing — they communicate only through explicitly declared interfaces.

#### WASI 0.2 Interface Catalog

All six interface groups shipped in WASI 0.2:

| Interface | Package | What it provides |
|---|---|---|
| `wasi:io` | `wasi:io/poll`, `wasi:io/streams`, `wasi:io/error` | Pollable handles, byte streams, error resources |
| `wasi:clocks` | `wasi:clocks/monotonic-clock`, `wasi:clocks/wall-clock` | Timers and wall time |
| `wasi:filesystem` | `wasi:filesystem/types`, `wasi:filesystem/preopens` | File/directory access |
| `wasi:sockets` | `wasi:sockets/tcp`, `wasi:sockets/udp`, `wasi:sockets/ip-name-lookup` | Network I/O |
| `wasi:cli` | `wasi:cli/environment`, `wasi:cli/stdin`, `wasi:cli/stdout`, `wasi:cli/stderr`, `wasi:cli/exit` | Command-line environments |
| `wasi:http` | `wasi:http/types`, `wasi:http/incoming-handler`, `wasi:http/outgoing-handler` | HTTP request/response |
| `wasi:random` | `wasi:random/random`, `wasi:random/insecure`, `wasi:random/insecure-seed` | Randomness |

Reference: [WASI.dev interfaces](https://wasi.dev/interfaces)

#### WIT Example (component world)

```wit
package my:service;

world handler {
    import wasi:http/outgoing-handler@0.2.0;
    import wasi:clocks/monotonic-clock@0.2.0;
    export wasi:http/incoming-handler@0.2.0;
}
```

#### Rust Compilation to WASI P2 Components

**Two paths exist:**

**Path A — Native target (Rust 1.82+, simple WASI-only components):**
```bash
rustup target add wasm32-wasip2
cargo build --target wasm32-wasip2 --release
```
The `wasm32-wasip2` target reached Tier 2 in Rust 1.82 (October 2024). The compiler directly emits a component binary. Limitation: stdlib WASI 0.2 API wrappers (`std::net`, `std::fs` over WASI sockets/filesystem) are still being wired — use the `wasi` crate for direct bindings.

**Path B — cargo-component (custom WIT interfaces, any complexity):**
```bash
cargo install --locked cargo-component   # current: v0.21.1, April 2025
cargo component new --lib my-component
cargo component build --release
```
Internally uses `wasm32-wasip1` + Wasmtime WASI adapter to produce a P2 component. Required when you have non-WASI WIT interfaces (third-party or custom).

**Cargo.toml for cargo-component:**
```toml
[package]
name = "my-component"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
wit-bindgen = "0.36"

[package.metadata.component]
package = "my:component"
```

**wit/ directory structure:**
```
wit/
  world.wit      # declares world, imports, exports
```

**Generated binding usage:**
```rust
// src/lib.rs
mod bindings;
use bindings::exports::my::component::MyInterface;

struct Component;
impl MyInterface for Component {
    fn run() -> String {
        "hello from component".into()
    }
}
bindings::export!(Component with_types_in bindings);
```

#### wasm-tools

`wasm-tools` is the low-level toolkit for inspecting and transforming WebAssembly:
```bash
cargo install --locked wasm-tools@1.235.0
wasm-tools component new core.wasm -o component.wasm  # wrap core wasm
wasm-tools component wit component.wasm               # inspect WIT
wasm-tools validate component.wasm                    # validate
```

### 3. wasmCloud

#### Architecture Overview

wasmCloud is a CNCF project (Incubating) that runs WebAssembly components across a distributed mesh of hosts. The core abstraction is:

- **Component**: A WASI P2 WebAssembly component containing business logic
- **Provider**: A capability plugin (external process or built-in) that handles I/O, storage, networking
- **Host**: A wasmCloud runtime process; multiple hosts form a lattice
- **Lattice**: The self-forming distributed mesh over NATS

#### Capability Providers

Built-in providers (in-process, no NATS hop):
- `wasi:logging` — structured logging
- `wasi:random` — secure randomness
- `wasi:clocks` — wall clock and monotonic

External/first-party providers (separate processes on the lattice):
| Provider | OCI image | Interface |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP Server | `ghcr.io/wasmcloud/http-server:0.26.0` | `wasi:http/incoming-handler` |
| KV Redis | `ghcr.io/wasmcloud/keyvalue-redis:0.28.2` | `wasi:keyvalue/atomics,store` |
| Blobstore S3 | `ghcr.io/wasmcloud/blobstore-s3` | `wasi:blobstore` |
| Messaging Kafka | `ghcr.io/wasmcloud/messaging-kafka` | `wasmcloud:messaging` |

As of Q3 2025 roadmap, providers are transitioning to a **wRPC server** model where WIT interfaces are served over TCP/NATS/QUIC/UDP, allowing providers to be written in any language and deployed in containers independently.

#### Lattice Networking

The lattice is built on **NATS** (Cloud Native Computing Foundation). Components and providers communicate via NATS subjects. The lattice is:
- **Self-forming**: hosts auto-discover each other via NATS
- **Self-healing**: link failures cause re-routing
- **Flat topology**: no hierarchy — any component can reach any provider across cloud boundaries, edge, K8s

#### Rust Component Development

```bash
cargo install wash-cli              # wasmCloud shell
wash new component http-test --template-name hello-world-rust
cd http-test
wash build                          # produces build/http_hello_world_s.wasm
wash dev                            # local dev with hot reload
```

**Cargo.toml for a wasmCloud component:**
```toml
[package]
name = "http-hello-world"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
wit-bindgen = "0.36"
wasi = "0.2"

# Target: wasm32-wasip2
```

**rust-toolchain.toml:**
```toml
[toolchain]
channel = "stable"
targets = ["wasm32-wasip2"]
```

#### WADM Deployment (OAM format)

```yaml
apiVersion: core.oam.dev/v1beta1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: hello-world
  annotations:
    description: 'HTTP hello world'
spec:
  components:
    - name: http-component
      type: component
      properties:
        image: file://./build/http_hello_world_s.wasm
      traits:
        - type: spreadscaler
          properties:
            instances: 1
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: httpserver
            namespace: wasi
            package: http
            interfaces: [incoming-handler]

    - name: httpserver
      type: capability
      properties:
        image: ghcr.io/wasmcloud/http-server:0.26.0
      traits:
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: http-component
            namespace: wasi
            package: http
            interfaces: [incoming-handler]
            source_config:
              - name: default-http
                properties:
                  address: 0.0.0.0:8000
```

```bash
wash app deploy wadm.yaml
wash app status hello-world
```

### 4. Cloudflare Workers with Rust/WASM

#### How It Works

Cloudflare Workers runs Rust via WebAssembly using the **workers-rs** (`worker` crate). The glue layer is **wasm-bindgen** — it generates JS bindings that expose the Workers runtime APIs (fetch, KV, R2, D1, Queues, Durable Objects) to the WASM module. A build tool called `worker-build` wraps this into a Workers-deployable bundle.

Note: This is **not** WASI P2. Workers uses its own ABI via wasm-bindgen + JS interop, not the Component Model. Workers announced Component Model support via `workers-wasm-builder` but it is still evolving.

#### worker-rs Setup

**Current version:** `worker 0.7.4` (crates.io, 2025)

**Cargo.toml:**
```toml
[package]
name = "my-worker"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
worker = "0.7"

[profile.release]
opt-level = "s"    # size optimization for WASM
lto = true
```

**wrangler.toml:**
```toml
name = "my-rust-worker"
main = "build/worker/shim.mjs"
compatibility_date = "2025-04-01"

[build]
command = "worker-build --release"
```

**HTTP handler example:**
```rust
use worker::*;

#[event(fetch)]
async fn main(req: Request, env: Env, _ctx: Context) -> Result<Response> {
    Router::new()
        .get_async("/api/data", |_req, ctx| async move {
            let kv = ctx.kv("MY_KV")?;
            let val = kv.get("key").text().await?;
            Response::ok(val.unwrap_or_default())
        })
        .run(req, env)
        .await
}
```

**KV, R2, D1 bindings in wrangler.toml:**
```toml
[[kv_namespaces]]
binding = "MY_KV"
id = "abc123"

[[r2_buckets]]
binding = "MY_BUCKET"
bucket_name = "my-bucket"

[[d1_databases]]
binding = "MY_DB"
database_name = "my-database"
database_id = "def456"
```

#### Resource Limits

| Resource | Free Plan | Paid Plan |
|---|---|---|
| CPU time per request | 10 ms | 30 s default; up to **5 minutes** (configurable) |
| Memory per isolate | 128 MB | 128 MB |
| WASM module size | No explicit limit | ~10 MB practical |
| Cron trigger CPU | 10 ms | 30 s (< 1 hr interval); 15 min (>= 1 hr) |
| Subrequests per request | 50 | 1000 |

CPU time measures actual CPU execution, **not** wall time — I/O await does not count. Average Worker uses ~2.2 ms per request.

To configure max CPU time on paid plan:
```toml
# wrangler.toml
[limits]
cpu_ms = 30000    # 30 seconds; max 300000 (5 minutes)
```

#### Supported Crates

Cloudflare maintains a [supported crates list](https://developers.cloudflare.com/workers/languages/rust/crates/). Key constraint: crates that use system calls not available in WASM (threads, raw sockets, mmap) require `no_std` alternatives or will fail to compile. Recommended: `reqwest` via `fetch` API, `serde`/`serde_json`, `regex`, `uuid`.

### 5. WASM SIMD128

#### Browser Support (as of 2025)

WASM SIMD128 (fixed-width 128-bit SIMD, spec proposal finalized 2021) has **universal browser support** as of 2025:

| Browser | SIMD128 | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Chrome/V8 | Yes (since v91) | Full 236+ instruction set |
| Firefox | Yes (since v89) | Full support |
| Safari/WebKit | Yes (since 2024) | Completed cross-browser coverage |
| Edge | Yes | Follows V8 |
| Samsung Internet | Yes | |

**Relaxed SIMD** (allows non-deterministic lane ordering for extra perf) was standardized in 2023 and is now broadly supported, enabling backends to use AVX2/NEON-specific reorderings.

#### SIMD128 Instruction Set Relevant to HDC

The 128-bit `v128` type maps to:
- `v128.and`, `v128.or`, `v128.xor`, `v128.not` — bitwise boolean ops (directly useful for HDC bundling/binding)
- `i8x16.popcnt` — **popcount per lane** (added in SIMD proposal; critical for Hamming distance)
- `v128.bitselect` — conditional select
- `i32x4`, `i64x2`, `f32x4`, `f64x2` — typed lane operations

For HDC binary hypervectors, the critical path is:
```
hamming_distance(a, b) = popcount(xor(a, b))
```
This maps directly to `v128.xor` + `i8x16.popcnt` + horizontal sum. 16 bytes processed per instruction.

#### Performance Characteristics

| Metric | Value |
|---|---|
| SIMD vs scalar JS | 6-15x speedup (benchmark dependent) |
| SIMD vs scalar WASM | 4-8x speedup for vectorizable workloads |
| WASM SIMD vs native AVX2 | ~4x slower (128-bit vs 256-bit registers) |
| WASM SIMD vs native NEON (ARM) | Closer; ~1.2-2x overhead |

**Key limitation:** WASM SIMD is limited to 128-bit registers. Native AVX2 uses 256-bit (AVX-512 uses 512-bit). This means WASM SIMD throughput is capped at ~50% of AVX2 native throughput for pure SIMD workloads.

**For HDC specifically (binary XOR + popcount):**
- 128-bit `v128.xor` + `i8x16.popcnt` = 16 bytes of binary hypervector per instruction
- A 10,000-bit hypervector requires ~625 SIMD instructions per distance computation
- Achievable throughput in WASM: millions of distance computations per second in browser
- Native AVX-512 has dedicated `vpopcntq` (512-bit popcount) — ~4x advantage over WASM

**128-bit integer arithmetic (WASM proposal, Phase 3 as of 2025):** There is a WASM proposal to add native 128-bit arithmetic instructions. Currently 128-bit integer ops require 64-bit pairs, which is 2-7x slower than native. This proposal is Phase 3 (implementations underway in Wasmtime/V8 in 2025).

#### Rust WASM SIMD Example

```rust
// Enable SIMD target features
// In Cargo.toml or RUSTFLAGS:
// RUSTFLAGS="-C target-feature=+simd128"

use std::arch::wasm32::*;

pub fn hamming_distance_simd(a: &[u8; 16], b: &[u8; 16]) -> u32 {
    unsafe {
        let va = v128_load(a.as_ptr() as *const v128);
        let vb = v128_load(b.as_ptr() as *const v128);
        let xored = v128_xor(va, vb);
        // i8x16_popcnt available in SIMD proposal extension
        // Sum lanes for total Hamming distance
        let counts = i8x16_popcnt(xored);
        // Horizontal add all 16 bytes
        let sum = i32x4_dot_i8x16_add_sat_u(counts, i8x16_splat(1), i32x4_splat(0));
        // Extract final sum...
        i32x4_extract_lane::<0>(sum) as u32
    }
}
```

**Build configuration for Rust WASM SIMD:**
```toml
# .cargo/config.toml
[target.wasm32-unknown-unknown]
rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+simd128"]

[target.wasm32-wasip2]
rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+simd128"]
```

Sources:
- [caniuse WASM SIMD](https://caniuse.com/wasm-simd)
- [Rust + WebAssembly 2025: WasmGC and SIMD](https://dev.to/dataformathub/rust-webassembly-2025-why-wasmgc-and-simd-change-everything-3ldh)
- [8 WASM SIMD patterns for data crunching](https://medium.com/@Nexumo_/8-webassembly-simd-patterns-for-data-crunching-8333c6c088b4)
- [Emscripten SIMD docs](https://emscripten.org/docs/porting/simd.html)

# Research Findings: Secret Management, SOPS, Progressive Rollout, and Agent Mobility

---

## 1. HashiCorp Vault Secret Management

### How Vault Works

Vault is an identity-based secrets and encryption management system. Every request flows through four stages:

1. **Authenticate** - A client presents credentials to a configured auth method
2. **Validate** - Vault checks those credentials against a trusted third-party (GitHub, LDAP, Kubernetes API, etc.)
3. **Authorize** - The client is matched to security policies (HCL/JSON path-based rules)
4. **Access** - A short-lived token is issued, tied to lease expiry

Vault operates behind an **encryption barrier**. On startup it is "sealed" — no operations are possible until unseal keys are provided. By default, Vault uses Shamir's Secret Sharing to split the unseal key across N shards, requiring a threshold to reconstruct. All data is encrypted before reaching the storage backend (Consul, etcd, S3, filesystem, etc.).

Reference: [How Vault Works](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/about-vault/how-vault-works)

---

### Secrets Engines

Secrets engines are plugins mounted at paths (e.g., `secret/`, `aws/`, `transit/`). They handle the full lifecycle of secrets.

| Engine | Description |
|--------|-------------|
| **KV v2** | Static key/value store with versioning |
| **AWS** | Generates ephemeral STS credentials for IAM roles |
| **Database** | Short-lived DB credentials via dynamic generation |
| **PKI** | X.509 certificate authority, issues short-lived certs |
| **Transit** | Encryption as a service (never stores plaintext) |
| **SSH** | Signed SSH certificates or one-time passwords |
| **Cubbyhole** | Token-scoped, private, ephemeral storage |

Reference: [Secrets Management Tutorials](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/secrets-management)

---

### Auth Methods

Auth methods verify identity before granting access:

- **AppRole** - Machine-to-machine: `role_id` (like username) + `secret_id` (like a one-time password). Pull mode is preferred: the client retrieves a randomly generated `secret_id` from Vault rather than having it pushed.
- **Kubernetes** - Pod presents its service account JWT; Vault calls the Kubernetes TokenReview API to validate. Returns a Vault token tied to policies. Requires a `token_reviewer_jwt` bound to the `system:auth-delegator` ClusterRole.
- **AWS IAM** - Vault calls `sts:GetCallerIdentity` to verify AWS identity
- **JWT/OIDC** - For human SSO flows
- **TLS Certificate** - Client cert verification

**AppRole workflow:**
```
# Enable
vault auth enable approle

# Create role with policy and TTL
vault write auth/approle/role/my-app \
    token_policies="my-app-policy" \
    token_ttl=1h \
    token_max_ttl=4h \
    secret_id_ttl=60m

# Fetch role_id (distribute to app)
vault read auth/approle/role/my-app/role-id

# Fetch secret_id (distribute separately, via CI/CD)
vault write -f auth/approle/role/my-app/secret-id

# Login (performed by the app at runtime)
vault write auth/approle/login role_id=$ROLE_ID secret_id=$SECRET_ID
# → Returns: token, lease_duration, policies
```

Reference: [AppRole Auth Method](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/auth/approle)

---

### Policies (HCL Syntax)

Policies are deny-by-default, path-based HCL documents. The most-specific matching path wins.

```hcl
# Allow app to read its own KV secrets
path "secret/data/my-app/*" {
  capabilities = ["read", "list"]
}

# Allow transit encryption/decryption for a specific key
path "transit/encrypt/my-app-key" {
  capabilities = ["update"]
}
path "transit/decrypt/my-app-key" {
  capabilities = ["update"]
}

# Allow renewal of own token
path "auth/token/renew-self" {
  capabilities = ["update"]
}
```

Capabilities: `create`, `read`, `update`, `delete`, `list`, `patch`, `sudo`, `deny`. `deny` takes precedence over all other capabilities.

Fine-grained control includes `allowed_parameters` (whitelist specific request fields), `denied_parameters`, `required_parameters`, and wrapping TTL enforcement.

Reference: [Policies](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/concepts/policies)

---

### Transit Secrets Engine (Encryption as a Service)

The transit engine is Vault's "cryptography as a service" offering. It **does not store data** — it only provides cryptographic operations. Named keys are created per application; applications encrypt/decrypt via the API without ever touching key material.

**Supported key types:**
- Symmetric: `aes256-gcm96` (default), `aes128-gcm96`, `chacha20-poly1305`
- Asymmetric signing: `ed25519`, `ecdsa-p256`, `ecdsa-p384`, `rsa-2048`, `rsa-4096`
- HMAC and CMAC

**Operations:**
```bash
# Enable transit
vault secrets enable transit

# Create a named key (one per application/domain)
vault write -f transit/keys/my-app-key

# Encrypt (plaintext must be base64)
vault write transit/encrypt/my-app-key \
    plaintext=$(echo "secret payload" | base64)
# → ciphertext: vault:v1:abc123...

# Decrypt
vault write transit/decrypt/my-app-key \
    ciphertext="vault:v1:abc123..."
# → plaintext (base64-encoded) → decode to get original

# Rotate key (new encryptions use v2, v1 still decrypts old data)
vault write -f transit/keys/my-app-key/rotate

# Rewrap old ciphertext to new key version
vault write transit/rewrap/my-app-key \
    ciphertext="vault:v1:abc123..."
# → vault:v2:xyz789...

# Configure minimum decryption version (archive old keys)
vault write transit/keys/my-app-key/config \
    min_decryption_version=2

# Generate data key (for local bulk encryption)
vault write transit/datakey/plaintext/my-app-key
# → plaintext DEK (use locally) + ciphertext DEK (store with data)
```

The ciphertext format `vault:v1:...` embeds the key version — Vault automatically selects the right version for decryption.

NIST recommends rotating AES-GCM keys before ~2³² (4.3 billion) encryptions per key version.

Reference: [Transit Secrets Engine](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/docs/secrets/transit) | [EaaS Tutorial](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/encryption-as-a-service/eaas-transit)

---

### Vault Agent Sidecar Pattern (Kubernetes)

The Vault Agent Injector uses a Kubernetes mutating admission webhook to intercept pod creation events and inject two containers:

1. **Init container** (`vault-agent-init`) - Fetches secrets before the main container starts
2. **Sidecar container** (`vault-agent`) - Runs alongside the app, keeps secrets refreshed, handles token renewal

Secrets are rendered to a shared `tmpfs` volume at `/vault/secrets/`. The app container reads files from this volume — it never talks to Vault directly.

**Pod annotation configuration:**
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject: "true"
        vault.hashicorp.com/role: "my-app-role"
        # Inject secret at /vault/secrets/config.env
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-secret-config.env: "secret/data/my-app/config"
        # Template the output format
        vault.hashicorp.com/agent-inject-template-config.env: |
          {{- with secret "secret/data/my-app/config" -}}
          DATABASE_URL={{ .Data.data.db_url }}
          API_KEY={{ .Data.data.api_key }}
          {{- end }}
```

Three integration approaches in Kubernetes (from HashiCorp's comparison):
1. **Sidecar Agent Injector** (described above) - No app code changes, dynamic refresh, but adds container overhead
2. **Vault Secrets Operator** - Kubernetes-native CRDs sync secrets to `kubernetes.io/Secret` objects; GitOps friendly
3. **CSI Secrets Store Provider** - Mounts secrets as volumes via the Secrets Store CSI driver

Reference: [Kubernetes Sidecar Tutorial](https://developer.hashicorp.com/vault/tutorials/kubernetes/kubernetes-sidecar) | [Kubernetes vs Sidecar vs CSI](https://www.hashicorp.com/en/blog/kubernetes-vault-integration-via-sidecar-agent-injector-vs-csi-provider)

---

### Rust Integration: `vaultrs` Crate

**Cargo.toml:**
```toml
[dependencies]
vaultrs = "0.8.0"           # default: rustls
# vaultrs = { version = "0.8.0", default-features = false, features = ["native-tls"] }
tokio = { version = "1", features = ["full"] }
serde = { version = "1", features = ["derive"] }
```

**Client initialization:**
```rust
use vaultrs::client::{VaultClient, VaultClientSettingsBuilder};

let client = VaultClient::new(
    VaultClientSettingsBuilder::default()
        .address("https://vault.example.com:8200")
        .token("s.TOKEN")  // or acquire via auth method
        .build()
        .unwrap()
).unwrap();
```

**AppRole login (machine auth):**
```rust
use vaultrs::auth::approle;

let token = approle::login(&client, "approle", &role_id, &secret_id).await?;
// Returns a LoginResponse with client_token, lease_duration, policies
```

**KV v2 read/write:**
```rust
use vaultrs::kv2;
use serde::{Deserialize, Serialize};

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize, Serialize)]
struct MySecret {
    api_key: String,
    db_password: String,
}

// Write
kv2::set(&client, "secret", "my-app/config", &MySecret {
    api_key: "abc123".to_string(),
    db_password: "hunter2".to_string(),
}).await?;

// Read
let secret: MySecret = kv2::read(&client, "secret", "my-app/config").await?;
```

**Transit encryption/decryption:**
```rust
use vaultrs::transit;

// Create a key
transit::key::create(&client, "transit", "my-app-key", None).await?;

// Encrypt (plaintext is base64-encoded automatically)
let plaintext = base64::encode("secret payload");
let encrypt_resp = transit::data::encrypt(
    &client, "transit", "my-app-key",
    &plaintext, None
).await?;
// encrypt_resp.ciphertext = "vault:v1:abc123..."

// Decrypt
let decrypt_resp = transit::data::decrypt(
    &client, "transit", "my-app-key",
    &encrypt_resp.ciphertext, None
).await?;
let original = base64::decode(&decrypt_resp.plaintext)?;
```

**Kubernetes auth login:**
```rust
use vaultrs::auth::kubernetes;
use std::fs;

let jwt = fs::read_to_string("/var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token")?;
let response = kubernetes::login(&client, "kubernetes", "my-app-role", &jwt).await?;
```

All errors are wrapped in `ClientError`. All top-level operations include `tracing` instrumentation.

References: [vaultrs on docs.rs](https://docs.rs/vaultrs) | [vaultrs GitHub](https://github.com/jmgilman/vaultrs) | [crates.io](https://crates.io/crates/vaultrs/0.1.0)

## 2. Mozilla SOPS

### How SOPS Works

SOPS (Secrets OPerationS, now a CNCF sandbox project maintained at getsops/sops) encrypts **values** within structured files while leaving **keys** in cleartext. This enables meaningful `git diff` output and conflict resolution while keeping secrets protected.

For a YAML file:
```yaml
# Before encryption
database:
  host: db.example.com
  password: hunter2
```
After `sops encrypt`:
```yaml
# After encryption (keys visible, values encrypted)
database:
    host: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:abc...,type:str]
    password: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:xyz...,type:str]
sops:
    kms: []
    age:
    -   recipient: age1s3cq...
        enc: |
            -----BEGIN AGE ENCRYPTED FILE-----
            ...
    lastmodified: '2025-04-13T00:00:00Z'
    mac: ENC[AES256_GCM,data:...,type:str]
    version: 3.9.0
```

SOPS uses the configured KMS/age keys to wrap a per-file data encryption key (DEK). The DEK encrypts each value using AES-256-GCM. The MAC covers all values to detect tampering.

Formats supported: YAML, JSON, ENV, INI, BINARY (arbitrary).

Reference: [SOPS Official Docs](https://getsops.io/docs/) | [GitHub](https://github.com/getsops/sops)

---

### Key Providers

| Provider | Mechanism | Best For |
|----------|-----------|---------|
| **age** | Curve25519 asymmetric, modern, no config | Local dev, CI/CD, small teams |
| **AWS KMS** | Calls KMS API with IAM credentials | AWS workloads |
| **GCP KMS** | Application Default Credentials | GCP workloads |
| **Azure Key Vault** | Azure default credential chain | Azure workloads |
| **PGP/GPG** | OpenPGP key rings | Legacy, offline environments |
| **HashiCorp Vault** | Transit engine | Vault-native workflows |

SOPS tries offline providers first (age, PGP) before cloud KMS, reducing latency for local development.

---

### `.sops.yaml` Configuration

The `.sops.yaml` file at the repository root defines creation rules — which keys to use for which files, matched by regex on path:

```yaml
# .sops.yaml
creation_rules:
  # Development secrets use local age key only
  - path_regex: \.dev\.(yaml|toml|json)$
    age: >-
      age1s3cqcks5genc6ru8chl0hkkd04zmxvczsvdxq99ekffe4gmvjpzsedk23c

  # Production secrets require both AWS KMS and age (Shamir 2-of-2)
  - path_regex: \.prod\.(yaml|toml|json)$
    shamir_threshold: 2
    key_groups:
      - kms:
          - arn: arn:aws:kms:us-east-1:123456789:key/mrk-abc123
            role: arn:aws:iam::123456789:role/sops-role
        age:
          - age1s3cqcks5genc6ru8chl0hkkd04zmxvczsvdxq99ekffe4gmvjpzsedk23c

  # Match-all fallback
  - age: >-
      age1t2c8jft25k5nnr7m2zln473dkxegwvx5ge2pfgarfnaepepmzpzszz63qy
```

Rules are evaluated first-match-wins. Shamir's Secret Sharing requires `shamir_threshold` of the `key_groups` to decrypt — enabling multi-party authorization.

---

### Age Provider Setup

```bash
# Install age and sops
brew install age sops   # macOS
apt install age sops    # Debian/Ubuntu

# Generate age key pair
age-keygen -o ~/.config/sops/age/keys.txt
# Output: Public key: age1s3cqcks5...

# Set environment variable (alternative to default path)
export SOPS_AGE_KEY_FILE=~/.config/sops/age/keys.txt
# or directly:
export SOPS_AGE_KEY="AGE-SECRET-KEY-1..."
# or via command:
export SOPS_AGE_KEY_CMD="pass sops/age-key"
```

Platform key file locations:
- Linux: `$XDG_CONFIG_HOME/sops/age/keys.txt` or `~/.config/sops/age/keys.txt`
- macOS: `~/Library/Application Support/sops/age/keys.txt`
- Windows: `%AppData%\sops\age\keys.txt`

---

### Encrypting/Decrypting TOML Files

```bash
# Encrypt in-place (uses .sops.yaml creation rules)
sops encrypt --in-place secrets.toml

# Encrypt to stdout
sops encrypt secrets.toml > secrets.enc.toml

# Decrypt to stdout (for CI/CD)
sops decrypt secrets.enc.toml

# Decrypt in-place (only in-memory, re-encrypts on save)
sops edit secrets.enc.toml

# Encrypt specific keys only (leave others plaintext)
sops encrypt --encrypted-regex '^(password|api_key|token)$' config.toml

# Encrypt with explicit age recipient
sops encrypt \
  --age age1s3cqcks5genc6ru8chl0hkkd04zmxvczsvdxq99ekffe4gmvjpzsedk23c \
  secrets.toml
```

For TOML secrets.toml:
```toml
# Plaintext
[database]
password = "hunter2"
host = "db.example.com"  # not secret, exclude

[api]
key = "sk-abc123"
```

With `--encrypted-regex '^(password|key)$'`, only `password` and `key` are encrypted; `host` remains plaintext.

---

### CI/CD Integration

```yaml
# GitHub Actions example
- name: Decrypt secrets
  env:
    SOPS_AGE_KEY: ${{ secrets.SOPS_AGE_KEY }}
  run: |
    sops decrypt config/production.yaml > /tmp/decrypted.yaml
    # Use decrypted config...

# Or use sops directly for inline values
- run: |
    DB_PASSWORD=$(sops decrypt --extract '["database"]["password"]' secrets.yaml)
```

For GitOps with FluxCD, SOPS decryption is built-in:
```yaml
# flux-system/kustomization.yaml
apiVersion: kustomize.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v1
kind: Kustomization
spec:
  decryption:
    provider: sops
    secretRef:
      name: sops-age  # Kubernetes secret containing age private key
```

Reference: [Flux SOPS Guide](https://fluxcd.io/flux/guides/mozilla-sops/)

---

### Rust Integration: `rops` Crate

The `rops` crate is a pure-Rust SOPS implementation (MPL-2.0), available as both CLI and library:

```toml
[dependencies]
rops = "0.1"   # ~9,400 monthly downloads, ~5,000 LOC
```

It supports YAML, JSON, TOML formats and age + AWS KMS providers. For encrypting programmatically, the `age` crate can also be used directly for lower-level control:

```rust
use age::{x25519, Encryptor, Decryptor};
use std::io::{Read, Write};

// Encrypt
let recipient: x25519::Recipient = public_key.parse()?;
let encryptor = Encryptor::with_recipients(vec![Box::new(recipient)])?;
let mut output = vec![];
let mut writer = encryptor.wrap_output(&mut output)?;
writer.write_all(plaintext)?;
writer.finish()?;

// Decrypt
let identity: x25519::Identity = private_key.parse()?;
let decryptor = Decryptor::new(&output[..])?;
let mut decrypted = vec![];
let mut reader = decryptor.decrypt(std::iter::once(&identity as _))?;
reader.read_to_end(&mut decrypted)?;
```

References: [rops on lib.rs](https://lib.rs/crates/rops) | [age on docs.rs](https://docs.rs/age/latest/age/)

## 3. Progressive Rollout / Canary Deployments

### Core Patterns

**Rolling update**: Replace instances one at a time; no extra capacity needed but mixed versions serve traffic during transition.

**Blue-green**: Maintain two identical environments. Flip all traffic at once. Fast rollback (flip back), but requires 2x capacity during transition.

**Canary**: Route a small percentage of traffic (e.g., 5%) to the new version; monitor metrics; progressively increase weight if healthy; roll back if metrics degrade. Balances risk vs. resource efficiency.

**Canary + blue-green hybrid**: Boot green alongside blue; route canary percentage; promote on health; full blue-green swap at completion.

---

### Fly.io Deployment Strategies

Fly.io supports four strategies configured in `fly.toml` or via `fly deploy --strategy`:

```toml
# fly.toml
[deploy]
  strategy = "bluegreen"  # rolling | immediate | canary | bluegreen
```

**Canary**: Boots a single new Machine, runs health checks. If healthy, continues with a rolling restart of the remaining Machines. If the canary fails, the deploy halts — no users are affected.

**Blue-green**: For each running Machine, a new Machine is booted alongside it in the same region. Traffic migrates to new Machines only after **all** new Machines pass health checks. Old Machines are then destroyed. Requires at least one configured health check. Cannot be used with attached volumes.

**Health check configuration:**
```toml
[[services.http_checks]]
  interval = "10s"
  timeout = "2s"
  grace_period = "5s"
  method = "GET"
  path = "/healthz"
  protocol = "http"
  tls_skip_verify = false

[[services]]
  [[services.http_checks]]
    interval = 10000    # ms
    timeout = 2000
    grace_period = 5000
    method = "get"
    path = "/healthz"
    restart_limit = 0
```

**Machine checks** (pre-deploy validation, not traffic-related):
```toml
[[machine_checks]]
  command = ["./scripts/smoke-test.sh"]
  image = "myapp:latest"
  guest = { cpu_kind = "shared", cpus = 1, memory_mb = 256 }
```

Machine checks boot a throwaway Machine and run integration tests, DB access checks, or config validation. A failing machine check halts the deploy before any traffic shifts.

**Machines API for custom blue-green:**
For programmatic control via the Machines API, the pattern is:
1. Create new Machines with the new image, with metadata tagging them as "green"
2. Poll health endpoints until all green Machines are healthy
3. Update load balancer routing (or update the app's internal routing via metadata)
4. Destroy old "blue" Machines

Reference: [Seamless Deployments](https://fly.io/docs/blueprints/seamless-deployments/) | [Deploy Configuration](https://fly.io/docs/launch/deploy/)

---

### Argo Rollouts (Kubernetes)

Argo Rollouts is a Kubernetes controller providing progressive delivery via a `Rollout` CRD that replaces `Deployment`. It integrates with ingress controllers (Nginx, Traefik) and service meshes (Istio, Linkerd) for precise traffic splitting.

**Full canary Rollout spec with analysis:**
```yaml
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Rollout
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  replicas: 10
  strategy:
    canary:
      # Integrate with Istio for weight-based traffic splitting
      trafficRouting:
        istio:
          virtualService:
            name: my-app-vsvc
          destinationRule:
            name: my-app-destrule
            canarySubsetName: canary
            stableSubsetName: stable
      # Background analysis starts at step 2
      analysis:
        templates:
        - templateName: success-rate
        - templateName: error-rate
        startingStep: 2
        args:
        - name: service-name
          value: my-app-canary.default.svc.cluster.local
      steps:
      - setWeight: 5      # 5% to canary
      - pause: {duration: 10m}
      - setWeight: 20
      - pause: {duration: 10m}
      - setWeight: 50
      - pause: {duration: 10m}
      - setWeight: 80
      - pause: {duration: 10m}
      # Final step promotes automatically if analysis passes
```

**AnalysisTemplate for success rate (Prometheus):**
```yaml
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AnalysisTemplate
metadata:
  name: success-rate
spec:
  args:
  - name: service-name
  metrics:
  - name: success-rate
    interval: 5m
    # Fail if success rate drops below 95% for 3 consecutive measurements
    successCondition: result[0] >= 0.95
    failureLimit: 3
    provider:
      prometheus:
        address: http://prometheus.example.com:9090
        query: |
          sum(irate(
            istio_requests_total{
              reporter="source",
              destination_service=~"{{args.service-name}}",
              response_code!~"5.*"
            }[5m]
          )) /
          sum(irate(
            istio_requests_total{
              reporter="source",
              destination_service=~"{{args.service-name}}"
            }[5m]
          ))
---
apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: AnalysisTemplate
metadata:
  name: error-rate
spec:
  args:
  - name: service-name
  metrics:
  - name: error-rate
    interval: 5m
    # Fail if error rate exceeds 5%
    successCondition: result[0] <= 0.05
    failureLimit: 3
    provider:
      prometheus:
        address: http://prometheus.example.com:9090
        query: |
          sum(irate(
            istio_requests_total{
              reporter="source",
              destination_service=~"{{args.service-name}}",
              response_code=~"5.*"
            }[5m]
          )) /
          sum(irate(
            istio_requests_total{
              reporter="source",
              destination_service=~"{{args.service-name}}"
            }[5m]
          ))
```

**Rollback behavior**: When `failureLimit` is exceeded, Argo Rollouts automatically aborts: sets canary weight back to 0%, scales down the canary ReplicaSet, marks the Rollout as `Degraded`. The stable version continues serving 100% of traffic.

**Other metric providers supported**: Datadog, New Relic, CloudWatch, Graphite, InfluxDB, Apache SkyWalking, web (arbitrary HTTP endpoint), Kubernetes Jobs (for custom analysis scripts).

**Delayed analysis** (give the canary time to warm up before measuring):
```yaml
metrics:
- name: success-rate
  # Wait 5 minutes before starting measurements
  initialDelay: 5m
  interval: 5m
  successCondition: result[0] >= 0.95
```

**Key metrics to drive rollback decisions:**
- HTTP 5xx error rate (< 1-5% threshold)
- P99 latency (< 200ms or 1.5x baseline)
- Success rate (> 95%)
- Database connection pool exhaustion
- Memory growth rate (for leak detection)
- Custom business metrics (order completion rate, payment success rate)

References: [Argo Rollouts](https://argoproj.github.io/rollouts/) | [Analysis & Progressive Delivery](https://argo-rollouts.readthedocs.io/en/stable/features/analysis/) | [Canary with Analysis (CNCF 2025)](https://www.cncf.io/blog/2025/02/18/demo-an-automated-canary-deployment-on-kubernetes-with-argo-rollouts-istio-and-prometheus/)

## 4. Agent Mobility / Process Migration

### CRIU: Checkpoint/Restore In Userspace

CRIU is a Linux tool that captures the complete state of a running process (or container) to disk and restores it — on the same machine or a different one. It was initially merged into the Linux kernel in 2012 (version 3.4) and is now used by OpenVZ, LXC/LXD, Docker, Podman, Kubernetes, and many others.

**What CRIU captures:**
- Memory pages (all mappings, including shared)
- CPU registers and execution context
- Open file descriptors (files, pipes, sockets)
- TCP connections (via Linux's `TCP_REPAIR` socket option — requires kernel support and cooperative peer)
- Unix domain sockets
- Timers and signals
- Namespaces (PID, net, mount, etc.)
- Process group hierarchy

**Basic CRIU workflow:**
```bash
# Checkpoint a process (PID 1234)
criu dump -t 1234 --images-dir /checkpoint/dir --shell-job --leave-running

# Files produced: pstree.img, mm-1234.img, pagemap-1234.img, pages-1234.img,
# fdinfo-1234.img, files.img, core-1234.img, etc.

# Transfer checkpoint images to another host
rsync -av /checkpoint/dir/ remote-host:/checkpoint/dir/

# Restore on target host
criu restore --images-dir /checkpoint/dir --shell-job

# Container-level checkpoint (Docker / Podman)
docker checkpoint create my-container checkpoint-1
docker checkpoint ls my-container

# Kubernetes ForensicContainerCheckpointing (kubelet API, alpha since 1.25)
curl -sk -X POST \
  "https://localhost:10250/checkpoint/default/my-pod/my-container" \
  --cert /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-kubelet-client.crt \
  --key /etc/kubernetes/pki/apiserver-kubelet-client.key
```

Reference: [CRIU Main Page](https://criu.org/Main_Page) | [CRIU GitHub](https://github.com/checkpoint-restore/criu)

---

### Checkpoint/Restore Architecture Layers

From the eunomia.dev 2025 survey, C/R operates at five complementary levels:

| Layer | Tool | Granularity | Overhead |
|-------|------|-------------|---------|
| **OS-level** | CRIU, BLCR | Full process state, transparent | High (ptrace, kernel) |
| **Container-level** | Docker checkpoint, Podman, Kubernetes | Container process tree | Medium |
| **VM-level** | VMware vMotion, Xen, KVM | Full VM including kernel state | Low latency live migration |
| **Application-level** | ML framework checkpoints (PyTorch, TF), agent state serialization | Domain-specific, selective | Minimal, but manual |
| **Library-level** | DMTCP (plugin architecture) | User-space, no kernel changes | Moderate |

**Stateless vs. Stateful restoration** — the emerging consensus is a hybrid:
- **Frequent stateful checkpoints** (CRIU-level) for sub-second failover and true continuity
- **Periodic stateless snapshots** (application-level serialization) for cross-version migration and portability

Reference: [Checkpoint/Restore Systems: Evolution and AI Agent Applications](https://eunomia.dev/blog/2025/05/11/checkpointrestore-systems-evolution-techniques-and-applications-in-ai-agents/)

---

### GPU Checkpointing: CRIUgpu (2025)

CRIUgpu (arXiv 2502.16631, 2025) extends CRIU to GPU-accelerated workloads by integrating NVIDIA's `cuda-checkpoint` with CRIU, creating unified CPU-GPU snapshots without application modification. Demonstrated on GPT-2 and LLaMA 3.1 training workloads with minimal overhead.

Key to this: NVIDIA and AMD now expose driver APIs for capturing GPU context (registers, VRAM contents, streams), which CRIUgpu calls during the checkpoint phase while the CPU is frozen via CRIU's standard mechanisms.

Use cases directly relevant to AI agents:
- Preempt LLM inference processes mid-run for cluster scheduling
- Migrate LLM inference services between nodes with zero session loss
- Snapshot long-running agent "dreams" (multi-hour reasoning tasks) for cost management

Reference: [CRIUgpu arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2502.16631v1) | [DevZero GPU Checkpoint](https://www.devzero.io/blog/gpu-container-checkpoint-restore)

---

### Serialization Patterns for Agent State Transfer

For AI agents specifically, state transfer requires capturing several distinct layers:

**1. Conversation/context state** — The agent's working memory: conversation history, tool call results, intermediate reasoning. In roko's case: the signal DAG plus episode log.

```rust
// Roko already does this in .roko/state/executor.json and .roko/episodes.jsonl
// Pattern: serialize the PlanRunner + task states to JSON

#[derive(Serialize, Deserialize)]
struct AgentCheckpoint {
    conversation_history: Vec<Message>,
    current_task: TaskId,
    tool_results: HashMap<String, ToolResult>,
    signal_dag_state: DagState,
    timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
}
```

**2. Goal/plan state** — Which tasks are pending, in-progress, completed, with their dependencies. The DAG executor state.

**3. Knowledge base state** — Accumulated facts, research artifacts, learned patterns (the efficiency/experiment/gate-threshold stores).

**4. External resource state** — File handles, network connections, subprocess handles. These cannot be cleanly serialized; instead, reconnect on restore.

**Migration patterns:**
- **Pull**: Agent serializes self to a checkpoint store; target node fetches and restores (clean, cloud-native)
- **Push**: Source node sends checkpoint to target (faster for latency-sensitive migration)
- **Weak migration**: Transfer only code + current instruction pointer, not heap (lightweight but requires deterministic replay)
- **Strong migration**: Transfer full execution state including stack (requires CRIU or equivalent)

---

### ACRFence: Security in Agent Checkpoint-Restore (2025)

arXiv paper 2603.20625 (2025) identifies a critical security problem with naive checkpoint-restore of LLM agents: **semantic rollback attacks**.

**The problem**: C/R saves local process state but cannot undo actions already performed on external services. After restore, an LLM re-synthesizes subtly different requests (different idempotency keys, request IDs). A malicious service can exploit this to cause duplicate payments, double-sends, or authority resurrection.

Two attack classes:
- **Action Replay**: Trigger a crash after a successful operation; post-restore, the agent generates a different request ID, making the bank/service treat it as a new transaction.
- **Authority Resurrection**: An expired capability (API key, OAuth token) is rolled back to its valid state; the agent re-uses it, granting access that should have expired.

**ACRFence mitigation**: Log all irreversible tool effects before executing them. On restore, compare new tool calls against the log. Classify each post-restore call as:
1. Semantically equivalent → replay the recorded response (no re-execution)
2. Semantically different (different recipient/amount) → block, require explicit fork
3. Credential reuse → block

This is directly relevant to roko's plan execution loop, which checkpoints executor state and supports `--resume`. Any tool calls that modify external state (git commits, API calls, file writes) should be logged with their effects before the checkpoint, to enable safe replay semantics on resume.

Reference: [ACRFence arXiv](https://arxiv.org/html/2603.20625) | [Checkpoint/Restore Working Group (Kubernetes, 2026)](https://kubernetes.dev/blog/2026/01/21/introducing-checkpoint-restore-wg/)

---

### Distributed Consistency: Chandy-Lamport Snapshots

For multi-agent systems (where roko is heading with parallel plan execution), consistent global snapshots require the **Chandy-Lamport algorithm** (1985, still the reference):

1. A process records its own state
2. Sends a "marker" message on all output channels
3. On receiving a marker on a channel: if first marker, record own state + empty that channel's state; subsequent markers: record messages received since own-state-recording
4. Termination: all processes have recorded state, all channels have recorded state

This ensures a snapshot captures a consistent global state — no in-flight messages between agents are dropped or double-counted. Essential for roko's parallel DAG executor when checkpointing mid-execution.

Reference: [Process migration - ACM Computing Surveys](https://dl.acm.org/doi/10.1145/367701.367728) | [Mobile Agent migration survey](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/309779920_A_Survey_on_Migration_Process_of_Mobile_Agent)

---
