## Multi-Stage Build with cargo-chef and Chainguard

The optimal production Dockerfile combines cargo-chef for build caching with a Chainguard runtime image:

```dockerfile
# docker/roko-cli-production.Dockerfile
#
# Production-optimized build: cargo-chef for layer caching,
# Chainguard static base for zero-CVE runtime.

# Stage 1: Dependency planning
FROM lukemathwalker/cargo-chef:latest-rust-1 AS chef
WORKDIR /app

FROM chef AS planner
COPY . .
RUN cargo chef prepare --recipe-path recipe.json

# Stage 2: Build dependencies (cached layer) + source
FROM chef AS builder

# Install musl target for static linking
RUN rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-musl

COPY --from=planner /app/recipe.json recipe.json
RUN cargo chef cook --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl \
    --recipe-path recipe.json

COPY . .
RUN cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl -p roko-cli

# Stage 3: Chainguard static runtime (zero CVEs)
FROM cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest

COPY --from=builder /app/target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/roko-cli /roko

USER nonroot
WORKDIR /data
EXPOSE 8080

ENTRYPOINT ["/roko"]
CMD ["plan", "run", "--port", "8080", "--bind", "0.0.0.0"]
```

Build times with this approach:

| Scenario | Duration |
|---|---|
| First build (cold cache) | ~12-18 min |
| Source-only change (deps cached) | ~3-5 min |
| Dependency change (partial cache) | ~5-10 min |

### Image Size Comparison

| Image Variant | Compressed Size | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| `roko-cli:latest` (Chainguard static + musl) | ~12-18 MB | Binary only, zero CVEs |
| `roko-cli:latest-slim` (distroless/cc) | ~15-22 MB | Binary + glibc |
| `roko-cli:latest-full` (debian-slim + tmux + ttyd) | ~60-80 MB | Full interactive |

### Test Criteria

```
Docker deployment tests:
1. `docker build -f docker/roko-cli-chainguard.Dockerfile .` succeeds
2. `trivy image roko-cli:latest` reports zero high/critical CVEs
3. Container starts with `--read-only --cap-drop=ALL` without errors
4. Health check endpoint responds within 10s of container start
5. Persistent volume survives container restart
6. `cosign verify` succeeds on all published ghcr.io images
7. Image size < 20MB compressed for slim variant
8. cargo-chef build uses cached layers for source-only changes
```

## WASI Preview 2 and the Component Model

WASI 0.2 (formerly "Preview 2") was stabilized in late 2024 and is the active standard for server-side WASM. It introduces the Component Model — a standardized way for WASM modules to compose via typed interfaces defined in WIT (WebAssembly Interface Types).

### WASI 0.2 Interface Catalog

| Interface | Package | Roko Usage |
|---|---|---|
| `wasi:io` | Pollable handles, byte streams | Substrate I/O abstraction |
| `wasi:clocks` | Monotonic and wall clocks | Decay calculations, TTL |
| `wasi:http` | HTTP request/response | Edge-to-core forwarding |
| `wasi:random` | Secure randomness | HDC vector generation |
| `wasi:filesystem` | File/directory access | Optional: MemorySubstrate bypass |
| `wasi:sockets` | TCP/UDP networking | Direct LLM provider calls (future) |

### Compiling Roko to WASI P2 Components

Rust 1.82+ provides the `wasm32-wasip2` target at Tier 2:

```bash
# Add the WASI P2 target
rustup target add wasm32-wasip2

# Build roko-core as a WASI P2 component
cargo build --target wasm32-wasip2 -p roko-core \
    --no-default-features --features "serde,hdc,decay"
```

For custom WIT interfaces (exposing Roko's Synapse traits as component exports), use `cargo-component`:

```toml
# crates/roko-wasm/Cargo.toml
[package]
name = "roko-wasm"
version = "0.1.0"
edition = "2021"

[lib]
crate-type = ["cdylib"]

[dependencies]
roko-core = { path = "../roko-core", default-features = false, features = ["serde", "hdc", "decay"] }
roko-std = { path = "../roko-std", default-features = false, features = ["memory-substrate"] }
wit-bindgen = "0.36"

[package.metadata.component]
package = "roko:cognitive"
```

WIT interface definition for Roko's cognitive kernel:

```wit
// crates/roko-wasm/wit/world.wit
package roko:cognitive;

interface scorer {
    record engram {
        id: string,
        kind: string,
        body: string,
        tags: list<tuple<string, string>>,
    }

    record score {
        novelty: f64,
        utility: f64,
        confidence: f64,
        salience: f64,
        valence: f64,
        arousal: f64,
        coherence: f64,
    }

    score-engram: func(engram: engram) -> score;
}

interface substrate {
    use scorer.{engram, score};

    put: func(engram: engram) -> result<string, string>;
    query-by-kind: func(kind: string) -> result<list<engram>, string>;
    query-by-tag: func(key: string, value: string) -> result<list<engram>, string>;
}

interface hdc {
    record hdc-vector {
        bits: list<u64>,
        dimensions: u32,
    }

    random-vector: func(dimensions: u32) -> hdc-vector;
    hamming-similarity: func(a: hdc-vector, b: hdc-vector) -> f64;
    xor-bundle: func(vectors: list<hdc-vector>) -> hdc-vector;
    bind: func(a: hdc-vector, b: hdc-vector) -> hdc-vector;
}

world cognitive-kernel {
    export scorer;
    export substrate;
    export hdc;
    import wasi:clocks/monotonic-clock@0.2.0;
    import wasi:random/random@0.2.0;
}
```

### Component Composition

The Component Model enables composing Roko's cognitive kernel with platform-specific capability providers without recompilation:

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            Platform Runtime (Host)             │
│                                                │
│  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐  │
│  │ roko:cognitive   │  │ Platform Provider  │  │
│  │ (WASM component) │──│ (HTTP, KV, etc.)  │  │
│  │                  │  │                    │  │
│  │ • Scorer         │  │ • wasi:http       │  │
│  │ • Substrate      │  │ • wasi:keyvalue   │  │
│  │ • HDC vectors    │  │ • wasi:logging    │  │
│  └──────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Fermyon Spin Deployment

Spin provides a serverless WASM runtime ideal for deploying Roko's cognitive kernel as HTTP-triggered functions. Each request gets an isolated WASM instance with sub-millisecond cold start.

### Spin Application for Roko Edge Scoring

```toml
# spin.toml
spin_manifest_version = 2

[application]
name = "roko-edge"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Roko cognitive scoring at the edge"

[[trigger.http]]
route = "/score"
component = "scorer"

[[trigger.http]]
route = "/similarity"
component = "hdc-search"

[component.scorer]
source = "target/wasm32-wasi/release/roko_edge_scorer.wasm"
allowed_outbound_hosts = ["https://api.anthropic.com"]
[component.scorer.build]
command = "cargo build --target wasm32-wasi --release -p roko-edge-scorer"

[component.hdc-search]
source = "target/wasm32-wasi/release/roko_hdc_search.wasm"
[component.hdc-search.build]
command = "cargo build --target wasm32-wasi --release -p roko-hdc-search"
```

```rust
// crates/roko-edge-scorer/src/lib.rs
use spin_sdk::http::{IntoResponse, Request, Response};
use spin_sdk::http_component;
use roko_core::{Engram, Kind, Body};
use roko_std::default_scorer::DefaultScorer;

#[http_component]
async fn handle_score(req: Request) -> anyhow::Result<impl IntoResponse> {
    let body: serde_json::Value = serde_json::from_slice(req.body())?;

    let engram = Engram::builder()
        .kind(Kind::Observation)
        .body(Body::Text(body["text"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string()))
        .build();

    let scorer = DefaultScorer::default();
    let score = scorer.score(&engram);

    let response = serde_json::json!({
        "id": engram.id.to_string(),
        "score": {
            "novelty": score.novelty,
            "utility": score.utility,
            "confidence": score.confidence,
            "salience": score.salience,
        },
        "forward_to_core": score.novelty > 0.5 || score.utility > 0.7,
    });

    Ok(Response::builder()
        .status(200)
        .header("content-type", "application/json")
        .body(serde_json::to_vec(&response)?)
        .build())
}
```

### Spin Deployment Commands

```bash
# Local development with hot reload
spin build && spin up

# Deploy to Fermyon Cloud
spin deploy --from .

# Deploy as OCI artifact to any WASI P2 runtime
spin registry push ttl.sh/roko-edge:v0.1.0
```

## wasmCloud Distributed Deployment

wasmCloud (CNCF Incubating) runs WASM components across a distributed lattice of hosts connected via NATS. This enables deploying Roko's cognitive kernel across cloud, edge, and on-premises nodes with unified orchestration.

### wasmCloud Application Definition (WADM)

```yaml
# wadm.yaml
apiVersion: core.oam.dev/v1beta1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: roko-cognitive
  annotations:
    description: 'Roko cognitive kernel on wasmCloud lattice'
spec:
  components:
    - name: cognitive-kernel
      type: component
      properties:
        image: ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-wasm:0.1.0
      traits:
        - type: spreadscaler
          properties:
            instances: 3
            spread:
              - name: edge
                requirements:
                  zone: edge
                weight: 80
              - name: cloud
                requirements:
                  zone: cloud
                weight: 20
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: httpserver
            namespace: wasi
            package: http
            interfaces: [incoming-handler]

    - name: httpserver
      type: capability
      properties:
        image: ghcr.io/wasmcloud/http-server:0.26.0
      traits:
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: cognitive-kernel
            namespace: wasi
            package: http
            interfaces: [incoming-handler]
            source_config:
              - name: default-http
                properties:
                  address: 0.0.0.0:8080

    - name: kvstore
      type: capability
      properties:
        image: ghcr.io/wasmcloud/keyvalue-redis:0.28.2
      traits:
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: cognitive-kernel
            namespace: wasi
            package: keyvalue
            interfaces: [store, atomics]
```

The `spreadscaler` distributes instances across edge and cloud zones. The lattice handles routing — a request arriving at any node reaches the nearest cognitive kernel instance.

            // Load pre-computed fingerprints from KV

            Response::from_json(&serde_json::json!({"matches": []}))
        })
        .run(req, env)
        .await
}

## WASM SIMD128 for HDC Vectors

WASM SIMD128 has universal browser and runtime support (Chrome 91+, Firefox 89+, Safari 2024+). For Roko's HDC operations, SIMD provides ~4x speedup over scalar WASM.

### HDC Hamming Distance with SIMD128

The critical path `hamming_distance(a, b) = popcount(xor(a, b))` maps directly to SIMD128 instructions:

```rust
#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]
use std::arch::wasm32::*;

/// SIMD-accelerated Hamming distance for 128-bit chunks.
/// Processes 16 bytes per iteration using v128.xor + i8x16.popcnt.
#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]
pub fn hamming_distance_simd(a: &[u8], b: &[u8]) -> u32 {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut total: u32 = 0;

    // Process 16-byte chunks with SIMD
    let chunks = a.len() / 16;
    for i in 0..chunks {
        unsafe {
            let va = v128_load(a.as_ptr().add(i * 16) as *const v128);
            let vb = v128_load(b.as_ptr().add(i * 16) as *const v128);
            let xored = v128_xor(va, vb);
            let counts = i8x16_popcnt(xored);

            // Horizontal sum of 16 bytes
            // Widen to i16x8, then i32x4, then extract
            let widened = i16x8_extadd_pairwise_u8x16(counts);
            let widened32 = i32x4_extadd_pairwise_i16x8(widened);
            total += (i32x4_extract_lane::<0>(widened32)
                + i32x4_extract_lane::<1>(widened32)
                + i32x4_extract_lane::<2>(widened32)
                + i32x4_extract_lane::<3>(widened32)) as u32;
        }
    }

    // Handle remainder bytes with scalar
    let remainder_start = chunks * 16;
    for i in remainder_start..a.len() {
        total += (a[i] ^ b[i]).count_ones();
    }

    total
}
```

### Performance Comparison

| Environment | 10,000-bit vector comparison | Throughput |
|---|---|---|
| Native x86_64 (AVX2) | ~5 us | ~200K comparisons/sec |
| Native ARM64 (NEON) | ~8 us | ~125K comparisons/sec |
| WASM SIMD128 (browser) | ~15 us | ~67K comparisons/sec |
| WASM scalar (browser) | ~60 us | ~17K comparisons/sec |
| WASM SIMD128 (wasmtime) | ~12 us | ~83K comparisons/sec |

Build configuration for WASM SIMD:

```toml
# .cargo/config.toml
[target.wasm32-unknown-unknown]
rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+simd128"]

[target.wasm32-wasip2]
rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+simd128"]
```

### Test Criteria

```
WASM deployment tests:
1. `cargo build --target wasm32-wasip2 -p roko-core --no-default-features --features "serde,hdc,decay"` succeeds
2. BLAKE3 content hashes match between native and WASM targets (cross-environment identity)
3. HDC Hamming similarity produces identical results (within f64 precision) on native and WASM
4. MemorySubstrate put/query roundtrips correctly in WASM
5. WASM module size < 1MB gzipped for the cognitive kernel
6. SIMD128 build produces correct results (verify with wasmtime)
7. Spin application responds to HTTP score requests within 50ms
8. Cloudflare Worker stays under 10ms CPU time for single-engram scoring
```

WIT interface definition for Roko's cognitive kernel:

```wit
// crates/roko-wasm/wit/world.wit
package roko:cognitive;

interface scorer {
    record engram {
        id: string,
        kind: string,
        body: string,
        tags: list<tuple<string, string>>,
    }

    record score {
        novelty: f64,
        utility: f64,
        confidence: f64,
        salience: f64,
        valence: f64,
        arousal: f64,
        coherence: f64,
    }

    score-engram: func(engram: engram) -> score;
}

interface substrate {
    use scorer.{engram, score};

    put: func(engram: engram) -> result<string, string>;
    query-by-kind: func(kind: string) -> result<list<engram>, string>;
    query-by-tag: func(key: string, value: string) -> result<list<engram>, string>;
}

interface hdc {
    record hdc-vector {
        bits: list<u64>,
        dimensions: u32,
    }

    random-vector: func(dimensions: u32) -> hdc-vector;
    hamming-similarity: func(a: hdc-vector, b: hdc-vector) -> f64;
    xor-bundle: func(vectors: list<hdc-vector>) -> hdc-vector;
    bind: func(a: hdc-vector, b: hdc-vector) -> hdc-vector;
}

world cognitive-kernel {
    export scorer;
    export substrate;
    export hdc;
    import wasi:clocks/monotonic-clock@0.2.0;
    import wasi:random/random@0.2.0;
}
```

### Component Composition

The Component Model enables composing Roko's cognitive kernel with platform-specific capability providers without recompilation:

```
┌───────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│            Platform Runtime (Host)             │
│                                                │
│  ┌──────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐  │
│  │ roko:cognitive   │  │ Platform Provider  │  │
│  │ (WASM component) │──│ (HTTP, KV, etc.)  │  │
│  │                  │  │                    │  │
│  │ • Scorer         │  │ • wasi:http       │  │
│  │ • Substrate      │  │ • wasi:keyvalue   │  │
│  │ • HDC vectors    │  │ • wasi:logging    │  │
│  └──────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘  │
└───────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## Fermyon Spin Deployment

Spin provides a serverless WASM runtime ideal for deploying Roko's cognitive kernel as HTTP-triggered functions. Each request gets an isolated WASM instance with sub-millisecond cold start.

### Spin Application for Roko Edge Scoring

```toml
# spin.toml
spin_manifest_version = 2

[application]
name = "roko-edge"
version = "0.1.0"
description = "Roko cognitive scoring at the edge"

[[trigger.http]]
route = "/score"
component = "scorer"

[[trigger.http]]
route = "/similarity"
component = "hdc-search"

[component.scorer]
source = "target/wasm32-wasi/release/roko_edge_scorer.wasm"
allowed_outbound_hosts = ["https://api.anthropic.com"]
[component.scorer.build]
command = "cargo build --target wasm32-wasi --release -p roko-edge-scorer"

[component.hdc-search]
source = "target/wasm32-wasi/release/roko_hdc_search.wasm"
[component.hdc-search.build]
command = "cargo build --target wasm32-wasi --release -p roko-hdc-search"
```

```rust
// crates/roko-edge-scorer/src/lib.rs
use spin_sdk::http::{IntoResponse, Request, Response};
use spin_sdk::http_component;
use roko_core::{Engram, Kind, Body};
use roko_std::default_scorer::DefaultScorer;

#[http_component]
async fn handle_score(req: Request) -> anyhow::Result<impl IntoResponse> {
    let body: serde_json::Value = serde_json::from_slice(req.body())?;

    let engram = Engram::builder()
        .kind(Kind::Observation)
        .body(Body::Text(body["text"].as_str().unwrap_or("").to_string()))
        .build();

    let scorer = DefaultScorer::default();
    let score = scorer.score(&engram);

    let response = serde_json::json!({
        "id": engram.id.to_string(),
        "score": {
            "novelty": score.novelty,
            "utility": score.utility,
            "confidence": score.confidence,
            "salience": score.salience,
        },
        "forward_to_core": score.novelty > 0.5 || score.utility > 0.7,
    });

    Ok(Response::builder()
        .status(200)
        .header("content-type", "application/json")
        .body(serde_json::to_vec(&response)?)
        .build())
}
```

### Spin Deployment Commands

```bash
# Local development with hot reload
spin build && spin up

# Deploy to Fermyon Cloud
spin deploy --from .

# Deploy as OCI artifact to any WASI P2 runtime
spin registry push ttl.sh/roko-edge:v0.1.0
```

---

## wasmCloud Distributed Deployment

wasmCloud (CNCF Incubating) runs WASM components across a distributed lattice of hosts connected via NATS. This enables deploying Roko's cognitive kernel across cloud, edge, and on-premises nodes with unified orchestration.

### wasmCloud Application Definition (WADM)

```yaml
# wadm.yaml
apiVersion: core.oam.dev/v1beta1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: roko-cognitive
  annotations:
    description: 'Roko cognitive kernel on wasmCloud lattice'
spec:
  components:
    - name: cognitive-kernel
      type: component
      properties:
        image: ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-wasm:0.1.0
      traits:
        - type: spreadscaler
          properties:
            instances: 3
            spread:
              - name: edge
                requirements:
                  zone: edge
                weight: 80
              - name: cloud
                requirements:
                  zone: cloud
                weight: 20
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: httpserver
            namespace: wasi
            package: http
            interfaces: [incoming-handler]

    - name: httpserver
      type: capability
      properties:
        image: ghcr.io/wasmcloud/http-server:0.26.0
      traits:
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: cognitive-kernel
            namespace: wasi
            package: http
            interfaces: [incoming-handler]
            source_config:
              - name: default-http
                properties:
                  address: 0.0.0.0:8080

    - name: kvstore
      type: capability
      properties:
        image: ghcr.io/wasmcloud/keyvalue-redis:0.28.2
      traits:
        - type: link
          properties:
            target: cognitive-kernel
            namespace: wasi
            package: keyvalue
            interfaces: [store, atomics]
```

The `spreadscaler` distributes instances across edge and cloud zones. The lattice handles routing — a request arriving at any node reaches the nearest cognitive kernel instance.

---

## Cloudflare Workers Deployment

For Cloudflare Workers, Roko's cognitive primitives run as a WASM module within V8 isolates at 300+ edge locations worldwide. Sub-millisecond cold start, ~2ms average CPU time per request.

### Worker Configuration

```toml
# wrangler.toml
name = "roko-scorer"
main = "build/worker/shim.mjs"
compatibility_date = "2025-04-01"

[build]
command = "worker-build --release"

[[kv_namespaces]]
binding = "ENGRAM_CACHE"
id = "abc123"

[limits]
cpu_ms = 10000  # 10s for complex scoring
```

```rust
// src/lib.rs (Cloudflare Worker)
use worker::*;
use roko_core::{Engram, Kind, Body};
use roko_std::default_scorer::DefaultScorer;
use roko_primitives::HdcVector;

#[event(fetch)]
async fn main(req: Request, env: Env, _ctx: Context) -> Result<Response> {
    Router::new()
        .post_async("/score", |mut req, _ctx| async move {
            let body: serde_json::Value = req.json().await?;
            let text = body["text"].as_str().unwrap_or("");

            let engram = Engram::builder()
                .kind(Kind::Observation)
                .body(Body::Text(text.to_string()))
                .build();

            let scorer = DefaultScorer::default();
            let score = scorer.score(&engram);

            Response::from_json(&serde_json::json!({
                "score": score,
                "forward": score.novelty > 0.5
            }))
        })
        .post_async("/hdc/similarity", |mut req, _ctx| async move {
            let body: serde_json::Value = req.json().await?;
            // HDC similarity search at the edge
            // Uses WASM SIMD128 for ~4x speedup on bitwise operations
            let query_bits: Vec<u64> = serde_json::from_value(body["query"].clone())?;
            let query = HdcVector::from_raw(query_bits, 10_000);

            // Load pre-computed fingerprints from KV
            // ... similarity search logic ...

            Response::from_json(&serde_json::json!({"matches": []}))
        })
        .run(req, env)
        .await
}
```

### Cloudflare Workers Resource Limits

| Resource | Free | Paid |
|---|---|---|
| CPU time per request | 10 ms | 30s (configurable to 5 min) |
| Memory per isolate | 128 MB | 128 MB |
| Subrequests | 50 | 1,000 |
| KV reads | 100K/day | 10M/month |

---

## WASM SIMD128 for HDC Vectors

WASM SIMD128 has universal browser and runtime support (Chrome 91+, Firefox 89+, Safari 2024+). For Roko's HDC operations, SIMD provides ~4× speedup over scalar WASM.

### HDC Hamming Distance with SIMD128

The critical path `hamming_distance(a, b) = popcount(xor(a, b))` maps directly to SIMD128 instructions:

```rust
#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]
use std::arch::wasm32::*;

/// SIMD-accelerated Hamming distance for 128-bit chunks.
/// Processes 16 bytes per iteration using v128.xor + i8x16.popcnt.
#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]
pub fn hamming_distance_simd(a: &[u8], b: &[u8]) -> u32 {
    assert_eq!(a.len(), b.len());
    let mut total: u32 = 0;

    // Process 16-byte chunks with SIMD
    let chunks = a.len() / 16;
    for i in 0..chunks {
        unsafe {
            let va = v128_load(a.as_ptr().add(i * 16) as *const v128);
            let vb = v128_load(b.as_ptr().add(i * 16) as *const v128);
            let xored = v128_xor(va, vb);
            let counts = i8x16_popcnt(xored);

            // Horizontal sum of 16 bytes
            // Widen to i16x8, then i32x4, then extract
            let widened = i16x8_extadd_pairwise_u8x16(counts);
            let widened32 = i32x4_extadd_pairwise_i16x8(widened);
            total += (i32x4_extract_lane::<0>(widened32)
                + i32x4_extract_lane::<1>(widened32)
                + i32x4_extract_lane::<2>(widened32)
                + i32x4_extract_lane::<3>(widened32)) as u32;
        }
    }

    // Handle remainder bytes with scalar
    let remainder_start = chunks * 16;
    for i in remainder_start..a.len() {
        total += (a[i] ^ b[i]).count_ones();
    }

    total
}
```

### Performance Comparison

| Environment | 10,000-bit vector comparison | Throughput |
|---|---|---|
| Native x86_64 (AVX2) | ~5 μs | ~200K comparisons/sec |
| Native ARM64 (NEON) | ~8 μs | ~125K comparisons/sec |
| WASM SIMD128 (browser) | ~15 μs | ~67K comparisons/sec |
| WASM scalar (browser) | ~60 μs | ~17K comparisons/sec |
| WASM SIMD128 (wasmtime) | ~12 μs | ~83K comparisons/sec |

Build configuration for WASM SIMD:

```toml
# .cargo/config.toml
[target.wasm32-unknown-unknown]
rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+simd128"]

[target.wasm32-wasip2]
rustflags = ["-C", "target-feature=+simd128"]
```

### Test Criteria

```
WASM deployment tests:
1. `cargo build --target wasm32-wasip2 -p roko-core --no-default-features --features "serde,hdc,decay"` succeeds
2. BLAKE3 content hashes match between native and WASM targets (cross-environment identity)
3. HDC Hamming similarity produces identical results (within f64 precision) on native and WASM
4. MemorySubstrate put/query roundtrips correctly in WASM
5. WASM module size < 1MB gzipped for the cognitive kernel
6. SIMD128 build produces correct results (verify with wasmtime)
7. Spin application responds to HTTP score requests within 50ms
8. Cloudflare Worker stays under 10ms CPU time for single-engram scoring
```

content.content:
1→# Docker Deployment
     2→
     3→> Roko services are packaged as Docker images for server deployments, Fly.io, and local
     4→> development stacks. This document covers the Dockerfile architecture, the cargo-chef build
     5→> pattern, slim vs full image variants, the Docker registry strategy, the CI workflow for
     6→> automated image builds, and the docker-compose configuration for running the full stack
     7→> locally.
     8→
     9→
    10→> **Implementation**: Specified
    11→
    12→---
    13→
    14→## Image Registry and Naming
    15→
    16→All images are published to GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io). Naming convention:
    17→
    18→```
    19→ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-cli:latest              # Slim, latest
    20→ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-cli:0.3.0               # Slim, pinned version
    21→ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-cli:0.3.0-full          # Full (tmux + ttyd), pinned version
    22→ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-serve:latest
    23→ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-serve:0.3.0
    24→ghcr.io/nunchi/mirage-rs:latest
    25→ghcr.io/nunchi/mirage-rs:0.3.0
    26→```
    27→
    28→Each service produces two image variants:
    29→
    30→- **Slim** — Binary in a distroless or scratch image. For production deployments where
    31→  interactive access is unnecessary. ~5-20MB compressed depending on base image.
    32→- **Full** — Binary + tmux + ttyd (web terminal server). For deployments where operators need
    33→  to attach to the running process via a browser. Uses `debian:bookworm-slim` as the base
    34→  (~50MB compressed).
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→## Two Image Variants
    39→
    40→### Slim Images
    41→
    42→Slim images contain only the statically-linked binary and minimal system dependencies. They
    43→are the default for production deployments.
    44→
    45→**Base image hierarchy** (from practical to minimal):
    46→
    47→| Base | Size | Shell | Use case |
    48→|---|---|---|---|
    49→| `debian:bookworm-slim` | ~80MB | Yes | Full images (needs tmux, ttyd, shell) |
    50→| `gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12` | ~20MB | No | Slim images for dynamically-linked Rust |
    51→| `cgr.dev/chainguard/static` | ~2MB | No | Slim images for statically-linked (musl) |
    52→| `scratch` | 0 | No | Absolute minimum for musl binaries |
    53→
    54→For `roko-cli` and `roko-serve` slim images, use `distroless` (needs glibc for alloy/revm
    55→dependencies). For crates with no C FFI dependencies, use `scratch` with musl for the absolute
    56→minimum image size.
    57→
    58→All containers run as non-root (`USER 65532`) with `--read-only --cap-drop=ALL` for security.
    59→
    60→### Full Images (with Web Terminal)
    61→
    62→Full images add tmux (terminal multiplexer), ttyd (WebSocket-to-PTY bridge), and an entrypoint
    63→script that ties them together. The service runs inside a tmux session. ttyd serves that session
    64→over WebSocket on port 7681. A browser can connect to ttyd and see the TUI in real time.
    65→
    66→This is critical for `roko-cli` which has a ratatui-based TUI. In production, operators want to
    67→see the TUI from a browser without SSH access.
    68→
    69→---
    70→
    71→## Directory Structure
    72→
    73→All Docker-related files live in `docker/` at the repo root:
    74→
    75→```
    76→docker/
    77→  roko-cli.Dockerfile                # Slim: binary + ca-certificates + git
    78→  roko-cli-full.Dockerfile           # Slim + tmux + ttyd + entrypoint script
    79→  roko-serve.Dockerfile              # Slim: binary + ca-certificates
    80→  roko-serve-full.Dockerfile
    81→  mirage-rs.Dockerfile               # Slim: binary + ca-certificates
    82→  mirage-rs-full.Dockerfile
    83→  console.Dockerfile                 # Web terminal multiplexer (Caddy + xterm.js)
    84→  entrypoints/
    85→    roko-cli.sh                      # Starts roko-cli in tmux, starts ttyd alongside
    86→    roko-serve.sh
    87→    mirage-rs.sh
    88→  docker-compose.yml                 # Full local stack
    89→  docker-compose.dev.yml             # Dev overrides (mount source, rebuild on change)
    90→```
    91→
    92→---
    93→
    94→## Dockerfiles
    95→
    96→### Slim Dockerfile: roko-cli
    97→
    98→```dockerfile
    99→# docker/roko-cli.Dockerfile
   100→#
   101→# Slim roko-cli image. Just the binary. No terminal access.
   102→# Use roko-cli-full.Dockerfile if you want web terminal support.
   103→#
   104→# Build: docker build -f docker/roko-cli.Dockerfile -t roko-cli:latest .
   105→# Requires: target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/roko-cli (pre-compiled)
   106→
   107→FROM debian:trixie-slim
   108→
   109→RUN apt-get update \
   110→    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends ca-certificates git curl \
   111→    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
   112→
   113→# Create non-root user with home directory for state
   114→RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash -d /data roko \
   115→    && mkdir -p /data/.roko/state /data/.roko/prd \
   116→    && chown -R roko:roko /data
   117→
   118→COPY target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/roko-cli /usr/local/bin/roko
   119→
   120→USER roko
   121→WORKDIR /data
   122→
   123→EXPOSE 8080
   124→ENV RUST_LOG=info
   125→
   126→# Default: run roko in server mode.
   127→# Override CMD to run interactively or with different flags.
   128→ENTRYPOINT ["roko"]
   129→CMD ["plan", "run", "--port", "8080", "--bind", "0.0.0.0"]
   130→```
   131→
   132→### Slim Dockerfile: roko-serve
   133→
   134→```dockerfile
   135→# docker/roko-serve.Dockerfile
   136→#
   137→# Slim roko-serve image. Stateless HTTP API server.
   138→#
   139→# Build: docker build -f docker/roko-serve.Dockerfile -t roko-serve:latest .
   140→
   141→FROM debian:trixie-slim
   142→
   143→RUN apt-get update \
   144→    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends ca-certificates \
   145→    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
   146→
   147→RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash roko
   148→
   149→COPY target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/roko-serve /usr/local/bin/roko-serve
   150→
   151→USER roko
   152→EXPOSE 8080
   153→ENV RUST_LOG=info
   154→
   155→ENTRYPOINT ["roko-serve"]
   156→CMD ["--port", "8080", "--bind", "0.0.0.0"]
   157→```
   158→
   159→### Full Dockerfile: roko-cli (with Web Terminal)
   160→
   161→```dockerfile
   162→# docker/roko-cli-full.Dockerfile
   163→#
   164→# Roko CLI with web terminal access. Runs roko inside tmux,
   165→# serves the tmux session via ttyd on port 7681.
   166→#
   167→# Port 8080: roko server API
   168→# Port 7681: web terminal (ttyd WebSocket)
   169→
   170→FROM debian:trixie-slim
   171→
   172→# System dependencies
   173→RUN apt-get update \
   174→    && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends \
   175→       ca-certificates git curl tmux procps \
   176→    && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*
   177→
   178→# ttyd: WebSocket-to-PTY bridge. Single static binary, ~1.5MB.
   179→# Serves a terminal session over HTTP/WebSocket.
   180→# https://github.com/tsl0922/ttyd
   181→ARG TTYD_VERSION=1.7.7
   182→ARG TARGETARCH
   183→RUN case "${TARGETARCH}" in \
   184→      amd64) TTYD_ARCH="x86_64" ;; \
   185→      arm64) TTYD_ARCH="aarch64" ;; \
   186→      *) echo "unsupported arch: ${TARGETARCH}" && exit 1 ;; \
   187→    esac \
   188→    && curl -fsSL \
   189→       "https://github.com/tsl0922/ttyd/releases/download/${TTYD_VERSION}/ttyd.${TTYD_ARCH}" \
   190→       -o /usr/local/bin/ttyd \
   191→    && chmod +x /usr/local/bin/ttyd
   192→
   193→# Binary
   194→COPY target/release/roko-cli /usr/local/bin/roko
   195→
   196→# Entrypoint script: starts roko in tmux, starts ttyd alongside
   197→COPY docker/entrypoints/roko-cli.sh /usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh
   198→RUN chmod +x /usr/local/bin/entrypoint.sh
   199→
   200→# Data volume mount point
   201→RUN useradd -m -s /bin/bash -d /data roko \
   202→    && mkdir -p /data/.roko/state /data/.roko/prd \
   203→    && chown -R roko:roko /data
   204→
   205→USER roko
   206→WORKDIR /data
   207→
   208→EXPOSE 8080 7681
   209→ENV RUST_LOG=info
   210→
   211→CMD ["entrypoint.sh"]
   212→```
   213→
   214→---
   215→
   216→## Entrypoint Scripts
   217→
   218→These scripts start the service inside a tmux session and run ttyd alongside it. If the service
   219→crashes, the container exits so the orchestrator (Docker, Fly.io) can restart it.
   220→
   221→### roko-cli Entrypoint
   222→
   223→```bash
   224→#!/bin/bash
   225→# docker/entrypoints/roko-cli.sh
   226→#
   227→# Starts roko inside a tmux session with web terminal access via ttyd.
   228→#
   229→# Environment variables:
   230→#   ROKO_PORT       - roko server port (default: 8080)
   231→#   ROKO_DATA_DIR   - data directory (default: /data)
   232→#   TTYD_PORT       - ttyd WebSocket port (default: 7681)
   233→#   TTYD_AUTH       - ttyd basic auth, format "user:pass" (optional)
   234→#                     If not set, ttyd has no auth. Use network-level
   235→#                     isolation (Fly internal network, Docker network)
   236→#                     to restrict access.
   237→#   ROKO_EXTRA_ARGS - additional roko flags (optional)
   238→
   239→set -euo pipefail
   240→
   241→DATA_DIR="${ROKO_DATA_DIR:-/data}"
   242→ROKO_PORT="${ROKO_PORT:-8080}"
   243→TTYD_PORT="${TTYD_PORT:-7681}"
   244→
   245→# Ensure state directories exist on the volume
   246→mkdir -p "$DATA_DIR/.roko/state" "$DATA_DIR/.roko/prd"
   247→
   248→# ── Start roko inside tmux ───────────────────────────────────────────
   249→# -x 200 -y 50: initial terminal size (large enough for the TUI).
   250→# When a viewer attaches with a different size, tmux resizes to match.
   251→tmux new-session -d -s roko -x 200 -y 50
   252→
   253→# exec replaces the shell so roko is PID 1 inside the tmux pane.
   254→# tee captures output to a log file alongside the TUI rendering.
   255→tmux send-keys -t roko "exec roko plan run \
   256→  --port $ROKO_PORT \
   257→  --bind 0.0.0.0 \
   258→  ${ROKO_EXTRA_ARGS:-} \
   259→  2>&1 | tee $DATA_DIR/.roko/roko.log" Enter
   260→
   261→# ── Start ttyd ───────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   262→# ttyd serves the tmux session as a WebSocket on TTYD_PORT.
   263→# -W: enable WebSocket writing (allows keyboard input from browser)
   264→# -c: basic auth credentials (if TTYD_AUTH is set)
   265→TTYD_ARGS=(-p "$TTYD_PORT" -W)
   266→if [ -n "${TTYD_AUTH:-}" ]; then
   267→  TTYD_ARGS+=(-c "$TTYD_AUTH")
   268→fi
   269→
   270→ttyd "${TTYD_ARGS[@]}" tmux attach -t roko &
   271→TTYD_PID=$!
   272→
   273→# ── Health loop ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
   274→# If the tmux session dies (roko crashed or exited), kill ttyd and
   275→# exit the container. The orchestrator (Docker, Fly) restarts it.
   276→while tmux has-session -t roko 2>/dev/null; do
   277→  sleep 5
   278→done
   279→
   280→echo "roko tmux session exited, shutting down"
   281→kill $TTYD_PID 2>/dev/null || true
   282→exit 1
   283→```
   284→
   285→---
   286→
   287→## Docker Build Pattern: cargo-chef
   288→
   289→cargo-chef achieves ~5× speedup for Docker builds by caching dependency compilation in a
   290→separate layer from source code. The three-stage pattern:
   291→
   292→```dockerfile
   293→# Stage 1: Plan — extract dependency graph
   294→FROM lukemathwalker/cargo-chef:latest-rust-1 AS chef
   295→WORKDIR /app
   296→
   297→FROM chef AS planner
   298→COPY . .
   299→RUN cargo chef prepare --recipe-path recipe.json
   300→
   301→# Stage 2: Build — compile deps (cached layer), then source
   302→FROM chef AS builder
   303→COPY --from=planner /app/recipe.json recipe.json
   304→RUN cargo chef cook --release --recipe-path recipe.json  # Cached!
   305→COPY . .
   306→RUN cargo build --release --bin roko-cli
   307→
   308→# Stage 3: Runtime — minimal base image
   309→FROM gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12 AS runtime
   310→COPY --from=builder /app/target/release/roko-cli /usr/local/bin/
   311→ENTRYPOINT ["/usr/local/bin/roko"]
   312→```
   313→
   314→The key insight: stage 2's `cargo chef cook` compiles all dependencies without the source code.
   315→This layer is cached by Docker. When you change source code but not dependencies, the rebuild
   316→only recompiles your code — not all of Tokio, Axum, ratatui, alloy, etc.
   317→
   318→First build: ~10-15 minutes (full Rust compile). Subsequent builds with only source changes:
   319→~2-5 minutes (dependencies cached).
   320→
   321→### Build Strategy: Pre-Compiled Binaries
   322→
   323→The preferred build strategy for CI does not compile Rust inside Docker. Instead, CI
   324→cross-compiles the binary first (using the musl targets), then copies the static binary into
   325→the Docker image:
   326→
   327→```bash
   328→# CI step 1: Cross-compile
   329→cross build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl -p roko-cli
   330→cross build --release --target aarch64-unknown-linux-musl -p roko-cli
   331→
   332→# CI step 2: Build Docker image (just copies the binary, no Rust compile)
   333→docker build -f docker/roko-cli.Dockerfile -t roko-cli:latest .
   334→```
   335→
   336→This means Docker builds are fast (seconds, not minutes), require no Rust toolchain in the
   337→image, and support multi-arch via Docker buildx.
   338→
   339→The cargo-chef pattern is used for the remote builder path (Fly.io), where pre-compiled binaries
   340→are not available and the Docker build must compile from source on the remote builder.
   341→
   342→---
   343→
   344→## CI Workflow for Docker Builds
   345→
   346→`.github/workflows/docker.yml` — Triggers on tag push, builds multi-arch images, pushes to
   347→ghcr.io:
   348→
   349→```yaml
   350→name: Docker Images
   351→
   352→on:
   353→  push:
   354→    tags: ['*-v*']  # Triggers on: roko-cli-v0.3.0, mirage-rs-v0.5.0, etc.
   355→
   356→jobs:
   357→  docker:
   358→    strategy:
   359→      matrix:
   360→        include:
   361→          - service: roko-cli
   362→            binary: roko-cli
   363→            package: roko-cli
   364→          - service: roko-serve
   365→            binary: roko-serve
   366→            package: roko-serve
   367→          - service: mirage-rs
   368→            binary: mirage-rs
   369→            package: mirage-rs
   370→
   371→    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
   372→    permissions:
   373→      contents: read
   374→      packages: write
   375→
   376→    steps:
   377→      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
   378→
   379→      # Cross-compile the binary for both architectures
   380→      - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable
   381→        with:
   382→          targets: x86_64-unknown-linux-musl,aarch64-unknown-linux-musl
   383→      - name: Install cross-compilation tools
   384→        run: cargo install cross
   385→      - name: Build amd64
   386→        run: cross build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl -p ${{ matrix.package }}
   387→      - name: Build arm64
   388→        run: cross build --release --target aarch64-unknown-linux-musl -p ${{ matrix.package }}
   389→
   390→      # Build and push multi-arch Docker images
   391→      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3
   392→      - uses: docker/login-action@v3
   393→        with:
   394→          registry: ghcr.io
   395→          username: ${{ github.actor }}
   396→          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
   397→
   398→      # Slim image
   399→      - uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
   400→        with:
   401→          context: .
   402→          file: docker/${{ matrix.service }}.Dockerfile
   403→          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
   404→          push: true
   405→          tags: |
   406→            ghcr.io/nunchi/${{ matrix.service }}:${{ github.ref_name }}
   407→            ghcr.io/nunchi/${{ matrix.service }}:latest
   408→          build-args: |
   409→            BINARY=${{ matrix.binary }}
   410→
   411→      # Full image (with tmux + ttyd)
   412→      - uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
   413→        with:
   414→          context: .
   415→          file: docker/${{ matrix.service }}-full.Dockerfile
   416→          platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
   417→          push: true
   418→          tags: |
   419→            ghcr.io/nunchi/${{ matrix.service }}:${{ github.ref_name }}-full
   420→            ghcr.io/nunchi/${{ matrix.service }}:latest-full
   421→          build-args: |
   422→            BINARY=${{ matrix.binary }}
   423→```
   424→
   425→---
   426→
   427→## docker-compose.yml
   428→
   429→For running the full stack locally. Services discover each other by Docker Compose service
   430→names (e.g., roko-cli reaches the gateway proxy at `http://gateway:4000`).
   431→
   432→```yaml
   433→# docker/docker-compose.yml
   434→#
   435→# Full Roko stack for local development.
   436→#
   437→# Usage:
   438→#   cd docker
   439→#   cp ../.env .env              # Copy your API keys
   440→#   docker compose up            # Start core services
   441→#   docker compose --profile full up  # + console web terminal
   442→
   443→services:
   444→  # ── Roko CLI (orchestrator) ──────────────────────────────────────
   445→  roko:
   446→    image: ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-cli:latest
   447→    ports:
   448→      - "8080:8080"                  # Roko server API
   449→    volumes:
   450→      - roko-data:/data              # Persistent state (plans, state, indexes)
   451→    environment:
   452→      ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY:?Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in .env}
   453→      RUST_LOG: ${RUST_LOG:-info}
   454→    healthcheck:
   455→      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-sf", "http://localhost:8080/health"]
   456→      interval: 10s
   457→      timeout: 5s
   458→      retries: 3
   459→
   460→  # ── Roko Serve (HTTP API) ────────────────────────────────────────
   461→  roko-serve:
   462→    image: ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-serve:latest
   463→    ports:
   464→      - "8081:8080"                  # HTTP API
   465→    environment:
   466→      ANTHROPIC_API_KEY: ${ANTHROPIC_API_KEY:?Set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY in .env}
   467→      RUST_LOG: ${RUST_LOG:-info}
   468→    healthcheck:
   469→      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-sf", "http://localhost:8080/health"]
   470→      interval: 10s
   471→      timeout: 5s
   472→      retries: 3
   473→
   474→  # ── mirage-rs (EVM fork) ─────────────────────────────────────────
   475→  mirage:
   476→    image: ghcr.io/nunchi/mirage-rs:latest
   477→    ports:
   478→      - "8545:8545"                  # JSON-RPC (Anvil-compatible)
   479→    volumes:
   480→      - mirage-data:/data
   481→    environment:
   482→      MIRAGE_RPC_URL: ${MIRAGE_RPC_URL:-}
   483→    profiles:
   484→      - full
   485→
   486→  # ── Console (web terminal) ──────────────────────────────────────
   487→  console:
   488→    image: ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-console:latest
   489→    ports:
   490→      - "8443:3000"                  # Web UI
   491→    environment:
   492→      CONSOLE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${CONSOLE_AUTH_TOKEN:-}
   493→      SERVICES: "roko:roko:7681"
   494→    profiles:
   495→      - full
   496→
   497→volumes:
   498→  roko-data:
   499→  mirage-data:
   500→```
   501→
   502→### Usage
   503→
   504→```bash
   505→cd docker
   506→cp ../.env .env
   507→
   508→# Core stack (roko + serve)
   509→docker compose up
   510→
   511→# Full stack (everything including web terminal and mirage-rs)
   512→docker compose --profile full up
   513→
   514→# Just roko (for local development)
   515→docker compose up roko
   516→
   517→# Rebuild after code changes
   518→docker compose build roko
   519→docker compose up roko
   520→```
   521→
   522→---
   523→
   524→## .dockerignore
   525→
   526→Every Docker build context must include a `.dockerignore` to exclude build artifacts and
   527→large directories:
   528→
   529→```
   530→target/
   531→.git/
   532→.roko/
   533→*.log
   534→node_modules/
   535→docs/
   536→tmp/
   537→```
   538→
   539→Without this, the Docker context can include hundreds of MB of build artifacts, causing slow
   540→builds or outright failures due to context size limits.
   541→

Add these new sections:

---

## Distroless and Chainguard Images

For production hardened deployments, Roko uses minimal base images that eliminate the vast majority of CVE attack surface. Three options are supported, each with different trade-offs:

### Image Comparison

| Base Image | Size | Shell | CVEs (typical) | SBOM | SLSA | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `debian:trixie-slim` | ~80MB | Yes | 30+ | No | No | Full images (tmux, ttyd) |
| `gcr.io/distroless/cc-debian12` | ~8.5MB | No | 0-3 | Manual | No | Slim images (glibc, alloy deps) |
| `cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest` | ~2-3MB | No | 0 | Built-in | Level 3 | Minimal static binaries (musl) |
| `cgr.dev/chainguard/glibc-dynamic:latest` | ~5MB | No | 0 | Built-in | Level 3 | Minimal glibc binaries |
| `scratch` | 0 | No | 0 | No | No | Absolute minimum (musl required) |

### Chainguard Slim Dockerfile

Chainguard images ship with SLSA Level 3 provenance and signed SBOMs by default. Every image is continuously rebuilt when upstream packages receive security patches, maintaining a zero-known-CVE policy.

```dockerfile
# docker/roko-cli-chainguard.Dockerfile
#
# Zero-CVE slim image using Chainguard static base.
# Requires musl-linked static binary.

FROM cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest

COPY target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/roko-cli /roko

# Chainguard images run as nonroot (UID 65532) by default
USER nonroot
EXPOSE 8080

ENTRYPOINT ["/roko"]
CMD ["plan", "run", "--port", "8080", "--bind", "0.0.0.0"]
```

For services that need glibc (alloy/revm C FFI dependencies):

```dockerfile
# docker/roko-serve-chainguard.Dockerfile
#
# Zero-CVE slim image with glibc support.

FROM cgr.dev/chainguard/glibc-dynamic:latest

COPY target/release/roko-serve /roko-serve

USER nonroot
EXPOSE 8080

ENTRYPOINT ["/roko-serve"]
CMD ["--port", "8080", "--bind", "0.0.0.0"]
```

### Verifying Image Provenance

Chainguard images include cryptographically signed SBOM and SLSA attestations retrievable via cosign:

```bash
# Verify the image signature
cosign verify \
  --certificate-identity-regexp="https://github.com/chainguard-images" \
  --certificate-oidc-issuer="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" \
  cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest

# Retrieve the SBOM (SPDX format)
cosign download attestation \
  --platform=linux/amd64 \
  --predicate-type=https://spdx.dev/Document \
  cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest | jq -r .payload | base64 -d | jq .predicate

# Retrieve SLSA Level 3 provenance
cosign download attestation \
  --predicate-type=https://slsa.dev/provenance/v1 \
  cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest | jq -r .payload | base64 -d | jq .predicate
```

---

## Image Signing and Attestation

All Roko Docker images published to ghcr.io are signed using Sigstore/cosign keyless signing and include SLSA provenance attestations.

### Signing in CI

```yaml
# .github/workflows/docker.yml (additions)
- name: Install cosign
  uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@v3

- name: Sign Docker images
  env:
    DIGEST: ${{ steps.docker-push.outputs.digest }}
  run: |
    cosign sign --yes \
      ghcr.io/nunchi/${{ matrix.service }}@${DIGEST}

- name: Attest SBOM
  run: |
    cosign attest --yes \
      --predicate sbom.json \
      --type cyclonedx \
      ghcr.io/nunchi/${{ matrix.service }}@${DIGEST}
```

### Verification by Users

```bash
# Verify a pulled Roko image
cosign verify \
  --certificate-identity="https://github.com/nunchi/roko/.github/workflows/docker.yml@refs/tags/roko-cli-v0.3.0" \
  --certificate-oidc-issuer="https://token.actions.githubusercontent.com" \
  ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-cli:0.3.0

# Scan for vulnerabilities
trivy image ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-cli:0.3.0
grype ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-cli:0.3.0
```

---

## Container Security Hardening

### Runtime Security Configuration

```rust
/// Container security configuration for Roko deployments.
/// Applied via Docker CLI flags or Kubernetes security context.
pub struct ContainerSecurityConfig {
    /// Run as non-root user (UID 65532 = nonroot in distroless/Chainguard)
    pub run_as_user: u32,
    /// Read-only root filesystem
    pub read_only_rootfs: bool,
    /// Drop all Linux capabilities
    pub drop_capabilities: Vec<String>,
    /// Seccomp profile (default: RuntimeDefault)
    pub seccomp_profile: String,
    /// Writable volumes (for .roko/ state)
    pub writable_mounts: Vec<String>,
}

impl Default for ContainerSecurityConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            run_as_user: 65532,
            read_only_rootfs: true,
            drop_capabilities: vec!["ALL".to_string()],
            seccomp_profile: "RuntimeDefault".to_string(),
            writable_mounts: vec![
                "/data/.roko".to_string(),
                "/tmp".to_string(),
            ],
        }
    }
}
```

Docker run with hardened security:

```bash
docker run \
  --read-only \
  --cap-drop=ALL \
  --security-opt=no-new-privileges:true \
  --user=65532:65532 \
  --tmpfs /tmp:rw,noexec,nosuid \
  -v roko-data:/data/.roko \
  ghcr.io/nunchi/roko-cli:0.3.0
```

### Kubernetes Security Context

```yaml
# Kubernetes pod security context for Roko
securityContext:
  runAsNonRoot: true
  runAsUser: 65532
  runAsGroup: 65532
  fsGroup: 65532
  seccompProfile:
    type: RuntimeDefault

containers:
  - name: roko
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      capabilities:
        drop: ["ALL"]
    volumeMounts:
      - name: roko-state
        mountPath: /data/.roko
      - name: tmp
        mountPath: /tmp

volumes:
  - name: roko-state
    persistentVolumeClaim:
      claimName: roko-data
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir:
      medium: Memory
      sizeLimit: 100Mi
```

---

## Multi-Stage Build with cargo-chef and Chainguard

The optimal production Dockerfile combines cargo-chef for build caching with a Chainguard runtime image:

```dockerfile
# docker/roko-cli-production.Dockerfile
#
# Production-optimized build: cargo-chef for layer caching,
# Chainguard static base for zero-CVE runtime.

# Stage 1: Dependency planning
FROM lukemathwalker/cargo-chef:latest-rust-1 AS chef
WORKDIR /app

FROM chef AS planner
COPY . .
RUN cargo chef prepare --recipe-path recipe.json

# Stage 2: Build dependencies (cached layer) + source
FROM chef AS builder

# Install musl target for static linking
RUN rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-musl

COPY --from=planner /app/recipe.json recipe.json
RUN cargo chef cook --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl \
    --recipe-path recipe.json

COPY . .
RUN cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl -p roko-cli

# Stage 3: Chainguard static runtime (zero CVEs)
FROM cgr.dev/chainguard/static:latest

COPY --from=builder /app/target/x86_64-unknown-linux-musl/release/roko-cli /roko

USER nonroot
WORKDIR /data
EXPOSE 8080

ENTRYPOINT ["/roko"]
CMD ["plan", "run", "--port", "8080", "--bind", "0.0.0.0"]
```

Build times with this approach:

| Scenario | Duration |
|---|---|
| First build (cold cache) | ~12-18 min |
| Source-only change (deps cached) | ~3-5 min |
| Dependency change (partial cache) | ~5-10 min |

### Image Size Comparison

| Image Variant | Compressed Size | Contents |
|---|---|---|
| `roko-cli:latest` (Chainguard static + musl) | ~12-18 MB | Binary only, zero CVEs |
| `roko-cli:latest-slim` (distroless/cc) | ~15-22 MB | Binary + glibc |
| `roko-cli:latest-full` (debian-slim + tmux + ttyd) | ~60-80 MB | Full interactive |

### Test Criteria

```
Docker deployment tests:
1. `docker build -f docker/roko-cli-chainguard.Dockerfile .` succeeds
2. `trivy image roko-cli:latest` reports zero high/critical CVEs
3. Container starts with `--read-only --cap-drop=ALL` without errors
4. Health check endpoint responds within 10s of container start
5. Persistent volume survives container restart
6. `cosign verify` succeeds on all published ghcr.io images
7. Image size < 20MB compressed for slim variant
8. cargo-chef build uses cached layers for source-only changes
```
